chef_bio.philosophy: Ten seats so every guest receives the chef's full attention; seasonal fish selected daily; the red-vinegar shari is a signature across the Kiwami family and considered the foundation of the flavour profile.
chef_bio.specialty: Edomae nigiri using seasonal fish — particularly fatty nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch), kinmedai (alfonsino), and saba oshi-zushi — over red-vinegar shari

anecdotes.text: A reviewer who visited in July 2023 described it as 'the seated Kiwami — a must for sake lovers,' noting the counter setting transforms the standing-bar spirit of the original into an intimate, adult dining experience.

An August 2024 visitor highlighted the nodoguro as 'incredibly fatty — it just melts,' and praised the kinmedai equally, with the red-vinegar shari called out as the defining characteristic of the Kiwami style.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information on cross-contamination controls is published. Shared prep surfaces and utensils in a small sushi counter are a realistic concern for celiac guests.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Edomae nigiri is traditionally brushed or served with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat. No tamari or wheat-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned on any platform.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Seafood, fish, tamagoyaki, and eel are the entirety of the menu; no plant-based items or vegan accommodations exist.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy menu, gluten-free policy, or allergen labelling is documented on Tabelog, Gnavi, HotPepper, or the parent brand website. Guests with dietary restrictions should call ahead.

positives.text: The shari (sushi rice) is made with red vinegar and plain rice — both inherently gluten-free ingredients — so the rice base itself poses no gluten risk absent cross-contamination.

cultural_comfort.note: Intimate 10-seat counter with no English signage noted; the omakase-style format and no English menu make prior Japanese communication or a Japanese-speaking companion advisable.

places.name: カフェ ド SaRa (Cafe de SaRa)
places.gf_detail: Toast is the signature offering — wheat bread is central to the menu (black sesame toast, brown sugar toast, egg toast). No gluten-free menu, labeling, or awareness noted across multiple review sources. The kitchen is not celiac-safe. Customers with celiac disease or serious wheat allergy should not eat here.
places.vegan_detail: A traditional Nagoya kissaten focused on charcoal-roasted coffee and toast morning sets. The menu is built around wheat-based toast (black sesame, brown sugar, egg toast) and Western-style sweets such as chiffon cake, cheesecake, crème brûlée, and chocolate gateau. No vegan labeling or plant-based orientation found. Dairy and eggs appear throughout the menu. Dashi and other hidden animal products in sweets cannot be ruled out. Vegan diners should ask before ordering.
places.hours_raw: Mon & Thu 8:45–13:00 (may close at 12:00 when items sell out); Tue/Wed/Fri/Sat 8:45–16:00; Sun & holidays closed; irregular additional closures announced via Instagram
places.neighborhood: Nishi-ku, Nagono/Shikemichi historic merchant district
places.cuisine: Specialty coffee, morning service toast, Nag
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Cafe de SaRa occupies a renovated townhouse estimated at 120 years old or more in Shikemichi (四間道), Nagoya's best-preserved Edo-period merchant street in Nishi-ku. The building's exposed beams and alley setting earned recognition as part of the city's landscape heritage. The owne

chef_bio.background: Cafe de SaRa occupies a renovated townhouse estimated at 120 years old or more in Shikemichi (四間道), Nagoya's best-preserved Edo-period merchant street in Nishi-ku. The building's exposed beams and alley setting earned recognition as part of the city's landscape heritage. The owner runs the cafe single-handedly.
chef_bio.philosophy: Slow, attentive coffee service in a space that values quiet and unhurried time — the kissaten tradition at its most considered.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-roasted coffee brewed one cup at a time by paper drip, paired with morning toast sets — the half-and-half black sesame and brown sugar toast is the signature item. Coffee is served in rotating high-end dishware including Hermès and Herend cups. Iced coffee arrives with a companion vessel of coffee-ice cubes to prevent dilution.

anecdotes.text: The building has stood for over 120 years and the exposed timber interior earned a Nagoya city landscape commendation. The owner works alone, so patience is part of the experience.
Each coffee order on the regular menu comes with three types of complimentary mini-sweets — a hallmark of Nagoya's distinctive kissaten culture where morning and sweets are given, not charged extra.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Toast — wheat bread — is the core product of this cafe. Flour is present throughout the kitchen. No allergen controls or gluten-free procedures have been noted anywhere in available sources. This venue is not appropriate for anyone with celiac disease.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dairy and eggs appear throughout the menu (butter on toast, egg toast, cream-based sweets). No vegan accommodation has been documented.

positives.text: Entirely non-smoking interior.

cultural_comfort.note: A solo-operated Japanese kissaten with no English signage or menu noted; basic pointing or simple Japanese phrases will be needed, though the quiet, unhurried atmosphere is welcoming to visitors.

places.name: レイヤーズ 丸の内 本店 (LAYER'S Marunouchi Honten)
places.gf_detail: Standard wheat-flour burger buns are the core of every item on the menu and there is no gluten-free bun alternative documented anywhere. Fries and other sides are prepared in a shared kitchen environment; no dedicated fryer information is available. Soy sauce use in sauces cannot be ruled out. Not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: All burgers are built around 100% beef patties (Australian and Hida beef blend) with dairy cheese as standard; no plant-based patty or dairy-free option has been documented on any source reviewed. Buns are house-made and contain wheat and likely dairy/egg. Effectively no vegan path.
places.neighborhood: 丸の内 / 久屋大通駅から徒歩約5〜6分
places.notes: LAYER'S opened in Nagoya's Marunouchi district around 2006 and has grown into one of the city's most celebrated gourmet burger destinations, earning a place on Tabelog's Hamburger Top 100 list repeatedly, including for 2026. The founder trained at the respected Tokyo burger shop

chef_bio.background: The founder trained at the respected Tokyo burger shop BROTHERZ before establishing this location.
chef_bio.philosophy: Emphasis on materials and handcraft: every bun is made one by one in-house each day, and the beef blend is chosen for both flavour and quality. The kitchen has also produced a number of independent burger chefs who have gone on to open their own shops.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature LAYER'S Burger — a towering stack built on house-made, lightly sweetened buns crafted fresh daily, filled with a 100% beef patty blended from Australian and Hida wagyu, plus lettuce, onion, cheese, bacon, pineapple, and egg, finished with a choice of BBQ, teriyaki, red hot chili, or sweet chili sauce.

anecdotes.text: The owner trained at BROTHERZ, a respected Tokyo burger establishment, before bringing the concept to Nagoya — a pedigree that underpins the shop's reputation for technical craft.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free fryer or preparation area is documented. Wheat buns are central to every menu item and the kitchen is a standard burger operation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sauce ingredients (BBQ, teriyaki, chili variants) are not publicly disclosed; teriyaki sauce in Japanese kitchens typically contains wheat-based soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All patties are beef; cheese is standard on most burgers. No plant-based items exist to assess cross-contact.

cultural_comfort.note: Walk-in casual burger shop with no English menu; staff are accustomed to local clientele, but pointing at a menu or using a translation app will get you served without difficulty.

places.name: 美味しいごはん じらふ (Oishii Gohan Jirafu)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free awareness or labeling found anywhere on the official site, Tabelog, or review sources. Core menu items are high-gluten: kushiage uses four types of breadcrumbs (wheat-based), karaage is battered, and the house yakibuta sauce is a soy-based tare (standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat). No dedicated fryer information available; the same fryer almost certainly handles breadcrumbed kushiage. Celiac diners should not eat here without detailed staff consultation in Japanese — and even then, cross-contamination risk from frying is very high.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or labeling exists. Signature dishes center on Aichi Mikawa pork, local fried chicken, and kushiage (battered, deep-fried skewers). The special yakibuta sauce is soy-based and has been maintained since opening — likely contains wheat. Dashi type is unconfirmed but izakaya-style cooking typically uses katsuobushi-based dashi. Off-menu requests are accepted depending on available ingredients, so a diner willing to communicate needs in Japanese may find limited options, but nothing can be guaranteed without direct inquiry.
places.neighborhood: 丸の内 / 久屋大通
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner-chef has over 20 years of culinary experience and runs this intimate izakaya in Nagoya's Marunouchi business district, conveniently positioned between Hisaya-odori and Marunouchi subway stations.

chef_bio.background: The owner-chef has over 20 years of culinary experience and runs this intimate izakaya in Nagoya's Marunouchi business district, conveniently positioned between Hisaya-odori and Marunouchi subway stations.
chef_bio.philosophy: Handmade creative cuisine that pairs well with alcohol; the chef will prepare off-menu dishes when ingredients allow, making each visit feel personal.
chef_bio.specialty: 愛知みかわ豚の特製焼豚 — Aichi Mikawa pork that is steamed, pressure-cooked, then grilled with a house soy-tare sauce maintained continuously since the restaurant opened. Also known for local Oku-Mikawa chicken karaage and a rotating kushiage platter using seasonal ingredients.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's operating motto, noted on the official site, is that service continues 'as long as the chef's stamina permits' — a candid admission of the one-person-driven nature of the kitchen.
The special yakibuta tare (sauce) has been kept alive and continuously added to since the restaurant first opened, a technique reminiscent of long-running ramen broths.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kushiage (deep-fried skewers) is a core menu category using four types of breadcrumbs, almost certainly wheat-based. The fryer is very likely shared with other fried items including karaage. No dedicated fryer information found.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature yakibuta dish uses a soy-based tare that has been maintained since opening. Standard Japanese soy sauce (koikuchi shoyu) contains wheat. No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pork, chicken, and egg dishes dominate the menu. The frying oil is shared with animal-based kushiage. No vegan-safe cooking zones or oils confirmed.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner-chef accepts off-menu requests when ingredients are available, suggesting some flexibility, but no allergy protocol or dietary accommodation system is described on any public source. Communication in Japanese is essential.

positives.text: Owner-chef with 20+ years of experience runs a small, personal kitchen — direct communication about dietary needs is more feasible than at a chain restaurant.

cultural_comfort.note: A small neighborhood izakaya catering to Marunouchi office workers; menus and signage are in Japanese only, and while the owner is accommodating, communicating dietary restrictions will require Japanese or translation assistance.

places.name: いねのや (Inenoya)
places.gf_detail: Soba is listed alongside udon and ramen, so shared cooking water and wheat cross-contamination are near-certain. The restaurant's tsuyu is conventional and wheat-containing soy sauce is standard practice here. No allergen menu, no GF labeling, no staff allergy protocols are mentioned on any source. Not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options identified. The menu centers on soba and udon served with standard fish-based dashi (katsuobushi/niboshi), plus ramen and donburi. No plant-based broth alternatives are mentioned on any source.
places.neighborhood: 浄心 / 名西 (Nishi Ward)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Soba is listed alongside udon and ramen, so shared cooking water and wheat cross-contamination are near-certain. The restaurant's tsuyu is conventional and wheat-containing soy sauce is standard practice here. No allergen menu, no GF labeling, no staff allergy protocols are menti

chef_bio.specialty: Ebi-katsu don (shrimp-cutlet rice bowl) and chilled tanuki soba are the dishes most praised in visitor posts; the lunch sets are noted for generous portions at modest prices.

anecdotes.text: A visitor described a chilled tanuki soba set (¥1,200) featuring springy, chilled buckwheat noodles with crisp tenkasu, a rich tsuyu, and a side potato croquette — calling it reliable neighborhood value.
The weekday mini-curry-don lunch set (¥1,000) drew praise for its stable, well-seasoned Japanese-style curry broth.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Soba is cooked alongside udon and ramen in a small kitchen; shared boiling water and utensils make wheat cross-contamination very likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No tamari or GF soy sauce is mentioned; standard wheat-containing soy sauce is used in the tsuyu and dashi, as is universal practice at this type of conventional soba-ya.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is fish-based (katsuo/niboshi dashi standard for this style); no plant-based broth alternative is offered.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet, old-fashioned neighborhood soba shop with a local clientele and no English signage or menu; basic Japanese helpful for ordering.

places.name: 呑ひゃら (Donhyara)
places.gf_detail: Tempura is the core offering — batter is wheat flour. Standard Japanese soy sauce (containing wheat) is almost certainly used in sauces and marinades. A shared fryer handles shrimp, fish, and vegetables together, so cross-contamination is inherent to the operation. No gluten-free options, dedicated fryer, or allergen accommodation has been reported anywhere. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: This is a tempura-focused izakaya. Tempura batter is wheat-based and the frying oil is shared across shrimp, fish, and vegetable items. Miso soup accompanies most sets and likely contains katsuobushi (bonito) dashi. No vegan menu exists; vegetable-only ordering may be possible at dinner with advance notice but cannot be confirmed. Ask directly when reserving.
places.neighborhood: 西区 浅間町、浅間町駅徒歩2分
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-established neighborhood izakaya in Nagoya's Nishi Ward, a short walk from Senamamachi Station. The restaurant is run by an elderly couple and occupies a modest, traditional building with a distinctly Showa-era atmosphere.

chef_bio.background: A long-established neighborhood izakaya in Nagoya's Nishi Ward, a short walk from Senamamachi Station. The restaurant is run by an elderly couple and occupies a modest, traditional building with a distinctly Showa-era atmosphere.
chef_bio.philosophy: Simple, honest tempura crafted to order by a skilled master; lunch is limited to four items and runs only about 75 minutes, with dinner seats booked out roughly three months in advance.
chef_bio.specialty: Tempura rice bowls — especially the oversized kakiage-don (mixed vegetable fritter bowl) whose fritter famously extends well beyond the rim of the bowl — and the upper-grade ebi tendon with plump shrimp.

anecdotes.text: The kakiage fritter is described by multiple visitors as comically large — it domes high over the bowl — yet the oil feels light and the batter stays crispy throughout the meal.
Dinner reservations reportedly require a wait of around three months, a remarkable waitlist for a 16-seat neighborhood izakaya.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura is the restaurant's core offering; shrimp, fish, and vegetables are fried together in shared oil. Cross-contamination with shellfish and wheat is inherent and unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (which contains wheat) is the expected base for tempura dipping sauce (tentsuyu) and any seasoning used at this izakaya. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternatives have been mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Shrimp and fish are fried in the same oil as vegetable tempura; miso soup served with lunch sets likely uses katsuobushi (bonito) dashi. No vegan-separated preparation has been reported.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No information about allergy handling, allergen menus, or staff training for dietary restrictions was found across any source reviewed.

positives.text: Dinner is reservation-only and the small team takes each order seriously; advance reservation provides an opportunity to communicate dietary needs directly to the cook before arrival.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny Showa-era counter-and-table izakaya run by an elderly couple; no English menu or signage, weekday-only hours with dinner by reservation months in advance — comfortable for Japanese-speaking diners or confident regulars.

places.name: 桔梗 (Kikyo)
places.gf_detail: 北海道産蕎麦粉を毎朝手打ちしているが、そばつゆ（醤油）・天ぷら衣（小麦）・各種料理に小麦が使用される。純粋な蕎麦のみであれば蕎麦粉100%の可能性があるが、十割そばか否か、つゆ回避の可否、小麦粉使用調理器具との交差汚染リスクなど、セリアック対応の明示は一切なし。グルテン過敏の方は来店前に必ず確認を。
places.vegan_detail: 典型的な蕎麦居酒屋で、出汁はかつお・昆布ベースが標準的。天ぷら・煮物・酒肴など動物性食材を多用するメニュー構成。ヴィーガン対応の明示なし。北海道産蕎麦粉の手打ち蕎麦自体は植物性だが、つゆ（かえし＋鰹出汁）が動物性。季節野菜料理はあるが、隠れ動物性食材の回避は要問い合わせ。
places.neighborhood: 中区丸の内（丸の内駅 徒歩1分）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 名古屋・丸の内の地下鉄出口から徒歩1分、ビジネス街の一角に構える手打ち蕎麦と日本料理の店。本店はシーアイマンション丸の内1Fに70席を擁し、さらに本店近くに築160年の歴史的建造物を転用した完全予約制の別棟「はなれ」を持つ。

chef_bio.background: 名古屋・丸の内の地下鉄出口から徒歩1分、ビジネス街の一角に構える手打ち蕎麦と日本料理の店。 本店はシーアイマンション丸の内1Fに70席を擁し、さらに本店近くに築160年の歴史的建造物を転用した完全予約制の別棟「はなれ」を持つ。
chef_bio.philosophy: 毎日柳橋市場で仕入れる旬の鮮魚・野菜と手打ち蕎麦を軸に、日本酒・焼酎とともに楽しむ「蕎麦居酒屋」スタイルを追求。
chef_bio.specialty: 北海道産蕎麦粉を毎朝製粉・手打ちした蕎麦。 コシと香りが特徴で、薬味には信州産「親田辛味大根」を使用。

anecdotes.text: 別棟「はなれ」は築160年の情緒ある建物を活かした完全予約制の個室空間。ビジネス接待から少人数の会食まで対応し、都心の喧騒を忘れる静謐な雰囲気を提供する。

gf_cross_contamination.text: 天ぷらなど小麦粉を使用するメニューを提供しており、揚げ油・調理器具の共用による交差汚染リスクについての情報は一切公開されていない。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: そばつゆに使用される醤油は通常小麦を含む。グルテンフリー醤油（丸大豆醤油等）使用の記載なし。

vegan_cross_contact.text: かつおや昆布を使った出汁が料理全般に使われている蕎麦居酒屋。ヴィーガン向けの出汁分離・調理分離についての情報なし。

staff_allergy_handling.text: アレルギー・食事制限への対応方針について、公式サイト・各グルメサイトのいずれにも具体的な記載なし。来店前に直接電話での確認を推奨（052-201-1559、電話受付は15〜17時を避けること）。

positives.text: 毎朝手打ちの蕎麦は北海道産蕎麦粉100%使用の可能性があり、純粋な蕎麦のみ注文・つゆ別添えで対応できるかは要確認。

cultural_comfort.note: 週末は定休で平日ビジネス客が中心。英語メニュー・英語対応の記載なし。基本的に日本語でのコミュニケーションが必要。

places.name: メゼス サントリーニ (Mezes Santorini)
places.gf_detail: Greek cuisine avoids soy sauce and dashi, which removes two common Japanese gluten vectors. However, moussaka uses a wheat-flour béchamel, Greek pies (spanakopita-style) use phyllo, and no gluten-free menu or dedicated fryer is mentioned anywhere. The kitchen is small and likely shared. No source confirms GF awareness or accommodation. Diners with celiac should call in advance to ask which dishes are flour-free.
places.vegan_detail: Greek cuisine here centers on charcoal-grilled meats, lamb, seafood, and dairy-rich preparations like moussaka (béchamel) and yogurt sauces. Olive oil, fresh vegetables, and marinated seafood feature prominently, and some cold mezze plates may be naturally plant-based, but no menu is explicitly labeled vegan. Dairy (feta, yogurt, béchamel) appears throughout. Best to call ahead and ask.
places.neighborhood: 浅間町駅周辺（名古屋市西区城西）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 名古屋唯一のギリシャ料理店として2016年10月にオープン。ギリシャ人のオーナーシェフと日本人の奥様によって営まれる小さな一軒家レストラン。浅間町駅から徒歩数分の古い長屋建築を使い、ミドルイースタン音楽が流れる落ち着いた空間を作り出している。

roles: オーナーシェフ（ギリシャ人男性）
chef_bio.origin: 名古屋市西区城西、浅間町駅周辺
chef_bio.background: 名古屋唯一のギリシャ料理店として2016年10月にオープン。 ギリシャ人のオーナーシェフと日本人の奥様によって営まれる小さな一軒家レストラン。 浅間町駅から徒歩数分の古い長屋建築を使い、ミドルイースタン音楽が流れる落ち着いた空間を作り出している。
chef_bio.philosophy: 地中海食の原則に基づき、オリーブオイル・新鮮な野菜・魚介類・肉類をバランスよく使う「メゼス（小皿料理）」のスタイルでギリシャ本国の味を再現することを目指している。
chef_bio.specialty: 炭火焼ミックスグリル（仔羊・ソーセージなど6種）およびムサカ（ナス・ポテト・ミートソース・ベシャメルソースの重ね焼き）。 ギリシャ産エクストラバージンオリーブオイルとシーソルトを使用。

anecdotes.text: ギリシャ人オーナーはギリシャ本国の料理法をそのまま持ち込み、「日本にいながら本物のギリシャ料理を」というコンセプトで開業。アテネ出身のレビュアーが「本国と同じ味」と評した。
ムサカはキッシュのように切り分けて提供され、ヨーグルトソースとの組み合わせで「コクが増す」と評判。肉料理は炭火焼きにこだわる。

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information on kitchen cross-contamination practices was found in any source. The kitchen appears small (30-seat restaurant in a converted townhouse), raising the likelihood of shared surfaces.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Greek cuisine does not use soy sauce or dashi as base ingredients, which eliminates the most common Japanese gluten/hidden-fish vectors. However, wheat flour is used in béchamel (moussaka) and likely in Greek pies.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Charcoal grill is used for both meat and seafood; no separation from plant-based items confirmed. Dairy (feta, yogurt, béchamel) is central to the menu.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No source mentions any allergy-handling policy, allergen menu, or staff training for dietary restrictions. English communication is noted as possible with the Greek owner.

positives.text: Greek cuisine base avoids soy sauce, mirin, dashi, and other umami seasonings standard in Japanese kitchens, making it structurally safer from several common hidden-gluten and hidden-fish sources.
Olive-oil-based cooking and fresh vegetable/seafood focus means some dishes (marinated octopus, grilled fish, salads) may be naturally GF — but this must be verified per dish.

cultural_comfort.note: Staff includes both a Greek owner and a Japanese spouse; some English is available, but the menu and setting are primarily Japanese-language.

places.name: 越廼屋 (Koshinoya)
places.gf_detail: ソースカツ丼のカツは小麦パン粉を使用。越前おろしそばは純粋に蕎麦粉だけである可能性があるが（一部口コミに「十割」の言及あり）、出汁・醤油への小麦混入や厨房でのクロスコンタミネーションリスクについて公式情報が一切ない。グルテンフリー対応を明示しておらず、セリアックには不適。
places.vegan_detail: 越前おろしそばのトッピングにかつお節を使用。ソースカツ丼は豚カツ（ラード・豚肉）主体。出汁や醤油の詳細は不明だが動物性食材が全面的に使われており、ヴィーガン対応メニューは存在しない。
places.hours_raw: 月〜金 11:30〜14:00（LO 13:45）、土曜は不定休（Facebookで要確認）、日曜・祝日定休
places.neighborhood: 中区丸の内（丸の内駅 徒歩1〜4分）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 2015年4月13日開業。オーナーは福井県出身で、福井の郷土料理を名古屋のビジネス街に届けるべくこの店を開いた。地下鉄丸の内駅すぐそばのビル1階に構える小さなランチ専門店。

chef_bio.background: 2015年4月13日開業。 オーナーは福井県出身で、福井の郷土料理を名古屋のビジネス街に届けるべくこの店を開いた。 地下鉄丸の内駅すぐそばのビル1階に構える小さなランチ専門店。
chef_bio.philosophy: 地元福井の味を正直に再現する姿勢。 ランチのみの営業で、食材・仕込みの質を保つ。
chef_bio.specialty: 福井ソースカツ丼と越前おろしそば。 薄く大きなロース肉にきめ細かいパン粉をまとわせ、濃厚なウスターソースにくぐらせたカツを丼飯の上に載せた一品と、鰹節・青ねぎ・大根おろしをたっぷり乗せた清涼感のある越前おろしそばをセットで味わうのが定番。

anecdotes.text: 福井出身者が試食して「本物の味」と太鼓判を押したと地元ブロガーが報告している。

gf_cross_contamination.text: ソースカツ丼の揚げ物と蕎麦を同一厨房で調理しており、グルテン交差汚染のリスクが高い。専用フライヤーの有無は未確認。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: 出汁・つゆへの醤油使用が想定されるが、小麦含有の有無について公式情報なし。

vegan_cross_contact.text: かつお節を越前おろしそばのトッピングに使用しており、動物性食材を全メニューで使用。ヴィーガン対応不可。

cultural_comfort.note: 小さなランチ専門店でメニューは日本語のみ、英語対応の記録なし。

places.name: 天婦羅 稲葉 (Tenpura Inaba)
places.gf_detail: Standard tempura batter is wheat flour — this is not a GF-friendly format by default. Tentsuyu dipping broth contains wheat-based soy sauce. The restaurant requests allergy information be communicated at the time of reservation as same-day changes are difficult; whether wheat-free alternatives can be arranged is unknown and must be confirmed directly before booking.
places.vegan_detail: Tempura is traditionally fried in sesame/vegetable oil (potentially vegan-compatible frying medium), but tentsuyu dipping sauce is dashi-based (likely bonito/katsuobushi). No plant-based or vegan course is advertised. Allergy and dietary restrictions must be communicated at reservation time; viability for strict vegans is unconfirmed.
places.neighborhood: 西区樋の口町（浅間町駅徒歩約7分）
places.notes: Chef Masanobu Inaba was born in Tokyo in 1967 and entered professional Japanese cuisine at age 18 after his family's sushi restaurant faced financial difficulties. He is largely self-taught, rising without the conventional pathway through prestigious establishments. He served as

chef_bio.origin: Tokyo (Ginza flagship); Nagoya Owari location opened October 1, 2025 within the newly built Espacio Nagoya Castle luxury hotel, the first expansion of the Ginza Inaba group outside Tokyo.
chef_bio.background: Chef Masanobu Inaba was born in Tokyo in 1967 and entered professional Japanese cuisine at age 18 after his family's sushi restaurant faced financial difficulties. He is largely self-taught, rising without the conventional pathway through prestigious establishments. He served as vice head chef at Grand Hyatt Tokyo's Roppongi sushi counter, then as executive chef at Conrad Tokyo's Kazahana Japanese restaurant, and later led the kitchen at Aman Nemu luxury resort in Ise-Shima.
chef_bio.philosophy: 温故知新 — honoring tradition while refusing to be bound by convention. Inaba has stated he values 'rejecting outdated assumptions while treasuring heritage. ' He pays close attention to seasonal ingredients, appropriate dishware, timing, temperature, and each guest's comfort to create 'a special experience of blissful moments. '
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal omakase tempura: each piece fried individually with technique matched to the ingredient's characteristics, served at a 6-seat hinoki counter where guests watch the chef work. This is Inaba's first dedicated tempura counter.

anecdotes.text: The breakfast Inaba developed at Aman Nemu in Ise-Shima was recognized by US magazine Brides as one of 'The Best Hotel Breakfasts in the world' — an unusual international accolade for a Japanese inn-style morning meal.

Inaba established Ginza Inaba in 2021 in partnership with MUGEN's Naohiro Uchiyama during the COVID-19 pandemic, a deliberate rejection of caution that reflects his 'reject conventional wisdom' philosophy.

The Nagoya venue, 尾張 粋 稲葉, marks the group's first regional expansion and Inaba's first tempura-dedicated counter, housed inside Espacio Nagoya Castle, a luxury hotel that opened October 1, 2025 on the historic Nagoya Castle Hotel site.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura batter is made from wheat flour by definition; a dedicated GF fryer or wheat-free batter is not mentioned anywhere on the restaurant's site or in any review source found.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tentsuyu dipping sauce is standard dashi + mirin + soy sauce (wheat-containing); no tamari or wheat-free soy sauce alternative is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dipping broth (tentsuyu) is almost certainly bonito-dashi based, the standard for tempura restaurants. No plant-based dashi alternative is mentioned.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The official site states: 'Allergies and other dietary restrictions should be communicated in advance, as same-day substitutions are difficult to accommodate.' This implies some flexibility if flagged at booking, but no specifics are given.

positives.text: Completely reservation-only format with advance allergy disclosure requirement means the kitchen has time to prepare; a 6-seat counter gives chefs full control over each guest's experience and direct knowledge of individual needs.

cultural_comfort.note: All materials and reservations are in Japanese; staff may have limited English, and the formal omakase setting assumes familiarity with traditional tempura counter etiquette.

places.name: 馬力屋 (Barikiya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free claims anywhere. Standard Japanese soy sauce (contains wheat) is almost certainly used in the tare and marinades. The stamina teppan uses hacho miso, which may also contain wheat. No allergen menu, no dedicated fryer information, and no staff allergy-handling protocol documented. Celiac diners should call ahead and approach with caution.
places.vegan_detail: This is a meat-only yakiniku and horumon specialist. Every dish centres on beef offal, pork, or other animal proteins. There are no plant-based mains, and the kitchen has no documented vegan provisions.
places.neighborhood: 西区新道 (浅間町駅徒歩7分)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Barikiya has been serving the working-class Shindo neighbourhood of Nagoya's Nishi Ward since around 1970, making it one of the older surviving horumon-yaki joints in the area. The shop's blackened exterior, red lanterns, and perpetual haze of charcoal smoke mark it as a relic of

chef_bio.background: Barikiya has been serving the working-class Shindo neighbourhood of Nagoya's Nishi Ward since around 1970, making it one of the older surviving horumon-yaki joints in the area. The shop's blackened exterior, red lanterns, and perpetual haze of charcoal smoke mark it as a relic of Showa-era downtown Nagoya.
chef_bio.philosophy: Honest, unglamorous yakiniku at neighbourhood prices: fresh offal sourced daily, served in a no-frills room where the meat does the talking.
chef_bio.specialty: Ninniku Sagari — thick-cut beef hanging tender blanketed in raw grated garlic, grilled table-side until the garlic mellows and caramelises on the hot grate.

anecdotes.text: Regulars describe the interior fans running at full blast to cope with the smoke from flaring horumon — small intestine famously ignites on the griddle, filling the compact room with a satisfying char.

The finishing move is the garlic fried rice, built on the same punchy garlic base as the sagari and described by one blogger as wholly different from any Chinese-style fried rice — a distinctly Barikiya creation.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No fryer information is documented. The kitchen handles marinated meats and offal; cross-contamination risk from shared equipment is unknown.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No allergen menu exists. Standard Japanese soy sauce containing wheat is the expected baseline for yakiniku tare and marinades at this type of establishment — no wheat-free soy sauce or tamari is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is an all-meat establishment; every surface and grill is in contact with animal products. No plant-based preparation area exists.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling protocol has been documented for this restaurant. Phone reservations are accepted and calling ahead (052-551-0598) in Japanese is the only known channel for allergy enquiries.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, smoky neighbourhood spot with a decades-long local following; no English signage or menu, phone reservations in Japanese only.

places.name: ププキッチン (Pupu Kitchen)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or certification. The menu is rice-based (enzyme brown rice) and naturally avoids many wheat sources, but standard soy sauce containing wheat is likely used in seasoning. No dedicated gluten-free fryer or kitchen separation confirmed. Celiac diners should ask specifically about soy sauce and any wheat-containing condiments before ordering. The macrobiotic framework does not inherently exclude wheat.
places.vegan_detail: The daily lunch set and curry lunch are fully plant-based (no meat, fish, eggs, or dairy). The desserts — including a soy-milk soft-serve parfait — are also egg-, dairy-, and refined-sugar-free. Standard soy sauce (containing wheat) is likely used in cooking; no tamari-specific claim confirmed. Dishes use macrobiotically sourced organic vegetables, whole grains (enzyme brown rice), and plant proteins such as takakibi (sorghum) and soy-based substitutes. Hidden animal products such as bonito dashi are not part of the macrobiotic philosophy, but explicit confirmation that all broth is vegan has not been found.
places.neighborhood: 城西 / Josai, Nishi Ward
places.cuisine: マクロビオティックカフェ / Macrobiotic Organic Cafe
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Healing Ai was founded in 1991 by health consultant Katoh Hiroko, who turned to macrobiotics and natural therapy after recovering from her own illness caused by nutritional excess. She passed the business to current owner Itoh Kazushige, who has continued her mission for over two

chef_bio.background: Healing Ai was founded in 1991 by health consultant Katoh Hiroko, who turned to macrobiotics and natural therapy after recovering from her own illness caused by nutritional excess. She passed the business to current owner Itoh Kazushige, who has continued her mission for over two decades — writing a monthly newsletter for customers across 500 manuscript pages, later compiled into a published book titled '20 Years Later, Me. ' The café won the 3rd Japan Vegetarian Award (Corporate Category) in 2018 for its contribution to plant-based lifestyle promotion.
chef_bio.philosophy: Three macrobiotic principles guide every dish: 身土不二 (body and soil as one — eat local and seasonal), 一物全体 (use the whole ingredient), and 陰陽調和 (yin-yang balance). The café aims to 'correct imbalanced nutrition and refresh daily fatigue' through Japanese traditional food, and stocks only products the owners personally believe in.
chef_bio.specialty: 毎日替わりプープーランチ (daily macrobiotic lunch set) — a single-plate meal featuring enzyme brown rice, miso soup, and rotating sides such as takakibi hamburger (made from sorghum, not meat), tofu terrine, hijiki marinade, and root vegetable potage

anecdotes.text: Founder Katoh Hiroko developed her health philosophy after personal illness revealed the dangers of nutritional excess; her recovery through macrobiotics became the founding story of Healing Ai in 1991.
The establishment won the 3rd Japan Vegetarian Award in the Corporate Category in 2018, recognizing its long-running commitment to plant-based lifestyle content.
Owner Itoh has written a monthly 'Healing Ai Letter' for over 20 years — a total of 500 manuscript pages — which was compiled and published as a book.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free kitchen or fryer confirmed. The menu is naturally low in wheat due to its macrobiotic, rice-based focus, but cross-contamination from shared surfaces or utensils cannot be ruled out.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard soy sauce (which contains wheat) is likely used in seasoning, as no tamari or gluten-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere in available sources. Celiac diners must ask specifically about soy sauce used in cooking.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The café philosophy is fully plant-based — no meat, fish, eggs, or dairy are used in the main kitchen — so vegan cross-contact is very unlikely in normal operation.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff are trained macrobiotic practitioners knowledgeable about ingredients sourced from the adjacent natural foods store; however, no formal allergy protocol or allergen-menu documentation has been confirmed.

positives.text: All standard desserts are made without eggs, dairy, or refined sugar; soy-milk soft-serve is used throughout the sweets menu.
The daily lunch uses organic, pesticide-free vegetables and whole grains (enzyme brown rice); no animal products appear in the main menu.

cultural_comfort.note: An English-language menu is available, but the setting is a small local macrobiotic cafe embedded in a Japanese natural foods store — some familiarity with Japanese dining customs and basic phrases will make the visit smoother.

places.name: 龍園 (Ryuen)
places.gf_detail: Standard Japanese shoyu (soy sauce) is brewed with wheat and is the defining flavour of this bowl. No gluten-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere. The kitchen is a tiny counter operation with no dedicated allergen protocols on record. This shop is not safe for anyone with celiac disease or serious wheat allergy.
places.vegan_detail: The signature shoyu ramen is built on an animal-based broth (chicken and/or pork bone stock), and the soy sauce tare almost certainly contains wheat. No plant-based or vegan options are listed or described in any source. Vegans have no workable choice here.
places.hours_raw: 月火水金土 11:30-13:30、木・日曜定休
places.neighborhood: 浄心（西区）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Standard Japanese shoyu (soy sauce) is brewed with wheat and is the defining flavour of this bowl. No gluten-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere. The kitchen is a tiny counter operation with no dedicated allergen protocols on record. This shop is not safe for anyone

chef_bio.philosophy: Reviewers describe the broth as 'gentle and additive-free' — a quietly principled approach to keeping the soup honest in an era of flavour boosters.
chef_bio.specialty: Shoyu ramen — a light, clear, old-school soy-sauce broth bowl priced around ¥700, prepared without chemical seasoning (無化調). Takana (pickled mustard greens) variations and a ramen-plus-fried-rice lunch set are also available.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information available on cross-contamination controls. The kitchen is a small counter operation and no allergen protocols are documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard wheat-brewed shoyu is the defining ingredient of the ramen. No wheat-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned in any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broth is animal-based (chicken and/or pork bone); no plant-only preparation exists at this shop.

cultural_comfort.note: A compact 10-seat counter shop with no English menu or signage; ordering is straightforward but best managed by pointing at the menu or using a translation app.

places.name: 矢場とん 名古屋城金シャチ横丁店 (Yabaton Nagoya Castle Kinshachi Yokocho)
places.gf_detail: Miso katsu is inherently wheat-heavy: the pork cutlet is dredged in wheat flour and coated with panko breadcrumbs before deep-frying. The proprietary miso sauce (aged 18 months, simmered with pork) is soybean-based, but the breading alone makes the core product unsafe for anyone avoiding gluten. No gluten-free preparation, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free menu option has been identified at Yabaton. Celiac diners should not eat here.
places.vegan_detail: Yabaton specializes entirely in breaded pork cutlet (tonkatsu). The core menu is pork-centric with no documented vegan alternatives. Side vegetables and cabbage are present but cooked in the same environment as meat and served alongside animal products. The signature miso sauce is simmered with pork. No vegan menu or plant-based options have been identified at this or any Yabaton location.
places.hours_raw: 10:30〜17:30（L.O. 17:00）、名古屋城の定休日に準ずる
places.neighborhood: 中区三の丸（金シャチ横丁・義直ゾーン）
places.notes: Yabaton was founded in 1947 by Shigeo Suzuki as 'Yaba no Tonkatsu,' a small shop in front of Minami-Otsu-dori tram stop in postwar Nagoya. Suzuki reportedly discovered the miso katsu concept when he dipped a skewered cutlet into a dote-nabe (miso-braised offal) pot at a street st

chef_bio.background: Yabaton was founded in 1947 by Shigeo Suzuki as 'Yaba no Tonkatsu,' a small shop in front of Minami-Otsu-dori tram stop in postwar Nagoya. Suzuki reportedly discovered the miso katsu concept when he dipped a skewered cutlet into a dote-nabe (miso-braised offal) pot at a street stall and was struck by how good it tasted — he then developed his own proprietary miso sauce and built it into a full restaurant. The company became a limited company in 1953, expanded to a flagship Yabacho store in 1970, and passed to second-generation leader Takayuki Suzuki in 1980. Today the chain operates 28 domestic locations and has international outposts including Bangkok. This branch opened inside the Kinshachi Yokocho Yoshinao Zone food district, a curated dining complex built adjacent to Nagoya Castle's main gate.
chef_bio.philosophy: Yabaton insists on dipping the cutlet into miso sauce rather than simply pouring it on top — maintaining the founder's original technique against the modern trend of sauce-on-top service.
chef_bio.specialty: Miso katsu — breaded pork cutlet (loin or fillet) dipped in or plated with a deeply savory red soybean miso sauce aged 18 months in cask under stone weights. The signature 'waraji' (sandal-sized) cutlet is the crowd-draw.

anecdotes.text: The miso katsu concept was born when founder Shigeo Suzuki dipped a kushikatsu (skewered cutlet) into a dote-nabe miso pot at a postwar street stall and realized the combination was extraordinary — leading him to develop his now-famous proprietary sauce.

A longtime customer recalled: 'During postwar recovery, miso katsu was truly extravagant. We worked hard and enjoyed it only once every few months as our sole pleasure.' It was considered a luxury item in the 1940s and 1950s.
Since 2008, Yabaton has built five elementary schools in Cambodia through donations, and in 2017 established the 'Yabaton Children's Fund' — unusual community philanthropy for a tonkatsu chain.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All katsu items are deep-fried in shared fryers — seafood (scallop, shrimp, oyster) and vegetable kushikatsu are fried alongside pork cutlets. No dedicated fryer for any item has been documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature dish is coated in wheat flour and panko breadcrumbs before frying — this is the core preparation and cannot be omitted. The miso sauce itself is soybean-based, but cross-contact with wheat throughout the kitchen is guaranteed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All cooking occurs in a pork-focused kitchen. The miso sauce is simmered with pork. No plant-only preparation zone or vegan menu items have been identified.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No specific allergen handling protocols or allergy communication procedures have been documented for this location. The HotPepper listing notes English menus are available but does not mention allergen cards or staff training.

positives.text: Credit cards and multiple e-money formats accepted; English menus available; located in a well-lit tourist-facing complex with clear signage — easier navigation for foreign visitors than a typical street restaurant.

cultural_comfort.note: English menus available; located inside a tourist-facing food complex at Nagoya Castle, so staff are accustomed to international visitors.

places.name: motsu・sen (Motsu Sen)
places.gf_detail: The signature motsusuki is a sukiyaki-style broth almost certainly based on soy sauce (which contains wheat) and mirin. No gluten-free claims or accommodations are mentioned anywhere. Champon noodles (〆) contain wheat. This venue is not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Entirely meat/offal-focused. Every dish — sashimi of heart and tripe, grilled hormonyaki, the signature motsusuki, champon finish — is built around beef offal. No plant-based or vegan options exist on the menu.
places.neighborhood: 那古野・円頓寺横丁 (Nakono / Endoji Yokocho)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in May 2023 on the second floor of Endoji Yokocho (円頓寺横丁), a refurbished alley of small restaurants in Nagoya's Nishi Ward. The concept was produced by a well-regarded hormonyaki specialist from Shibuya, Tokyo, with the aim of bringing motsu sukiyaki — a style nearly absen

chef_bio.background: Opened in May 2023 on the second floor of Endoji Yokocho (円頓寺横丁), a refurbished alley of small restaurants in Nagoya's Nishi Ward. The concept was produced by a well-regarded hormonyaki specialist from Shibuya, Tokyo, with the aim of bringing motsu sukiyaki — a style nearly absent from Nagoya — to the city in a stylish, accessible setting.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner/producer insists on daily-fresh offal delivery, personally crafts the original sauces, and sources Cambodian pepper directly — a level of ingredient control unusual for a 16-seat neighbourhood spot. Staff cook tableside to maintain the correct heat and technique throughout the course.
chef_bio.specialty: もつすき (motsusuki): beef offal cooked sukiyaki-style in a soy-based sauce, evolving through three stages from seared offal to full hotpot, finishing with champon noodles dusted with Cambodian pepper sourced directly by the owner.

anecdotes.text: The motsu sashimi — sliced raw heart and tripe served as course openers — was cited by multiple Retty reviewers as unexpectedly refined, changing their assumptions about offal cuisine.

A ladies-only abbreviated course (6 dishes, ¥3,630) was introduced in early 2024, reflecting the owner's intent to make the venue approachable for diners who might be intimidated by offal-focused omakase.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information available on cross-contamination controls. The kitchen handles wheat-containing soy sauce, champon noodles, and likely other wheat-based ingredients throughout service.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Motsusuki is a sukiyaki-style dish whose sauce is almost certainly soy-sauce-based (containing wheat). No gluten-free soy sauce (tamari) alternative is mentioned anywhere in listings or reviews.
The finish course is champon noodles, which contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Entire menu is built around beef offal — no plant-based dishes exist; vegan cross-contact is moot as there is nothing vegan to order.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling policy or allergen menu is referenced in any source. Diners with dietary restrictions should call 052-571-2919 before visiting.

cultural_comfort.note: No English menu or signage mentioned; reservations and communication are in Japanese, though the intimate counter format and staff attentiveness noted in reviews suggest a welcoming environment for those who can gesture or use translation apps.

places.name: 長命うどん 弁天通店 (Chomei Udon Bentendori)
places.gf_detail: Udon is the core product — wheat noodles are unavoidable. Dashi is almost certainly fish-based (bonito/katsuo). Soy sauce in the broth contains wheat. No GF options exist and cross-contamination from wheat is inherent to every dish. Celiac diners cannot eat here safely.
places.vegan_detail: Broth is fish-based dashi (bonito); tempura toppings include seafood. No plant-based or vegan options are offered or noted anywhere in available sources.
places.hours_raw: 月〜金 11:00〜16:00頃（売り切れ次第終了）、土・日・祝 11:00〜14:00頃（売り切れ次第終了）、無休
places.neighborhood: 浄心（西区）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 長命うどん (Chomei Udon) traces its roots to 1913 (Taisho 2), when a Nagoya noodle-stall family was chosen to make the ceremonial first crossing of the newly built Naya Bridge. The city mayor marked the occasion by bestowing the name 'Chomei' (longevity) on the shop. The Bentendori br

chef_bio.origin: Nagoya (Nakamura Ward main shop, est. 1913; Bentendori branch in Nishi Ward)
chef_bio.background: 長命うどん (Chomei Udon) traces its roots to 1913 (Taisho 2), when a Nagoya noodle-stall family was chosen to make the ceremonial first crossing of the newly built Naya Bridge. The city mayor marked the occasion by bestowing the name 'Chomei' (longevity) on the shop. The Bentendori branch is one of several noren-wake (apprentice-spun-off) locations; only trainees who complete an apprenticeship at the main Nakamura-ward shop and are recognised as masters earn the right to open an affiliated branch.
chef_bio.philosophy: Deliver honest, affordable noodles hand-crafted with the same sincerity as the original 1913 shop; slight branch-to-branch variation is accepted as proof of handmade craft.
chef_bio.specialty: Classic Nagoya market-style udon with interchangeable noodle types (udon, soba, kishimen, chuka soba) and mix-and-match combinations — house-made noodles, light savory broth, kakiage tempura; the branch is known for not serving kishimen.

anecdotes.text: The name 'Chomei' (long life) was personally granted by the Nagoya city mayor at the Naya Bridge opening ceremony in the Taisho era — an origin story the chain still highlights as its founding legend.

The Bentendori location is housed in the ground floor of an apartment building on the north side of Nagoya Castle's perimeter, with a small rear car park — an incongruous setting that adds to its local-secret feel.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat is the primary ingredient (udon noodles); cross-contamination is inherent and unavoidable throughout the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broth is seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce alternatives are mentioned in any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish-based dashi is used in the broth; tempura includes seafood toppings. No plant-based broth option is available.

cultural_comfort.note: A no-frills cash-only counter shop with no English menu or signage; basic Japanese is helpful for ordering, though the short menu and pointing at photos or the menu board is manageable.

places.name: もつ藤 (Motsu Fuji)
places.gf_detail: もつ鍋のスープには自家製醤油ベースの酢だれが使用されており、醤油には通常小麦が含まれる。〆の麺（ちゃんぽん・ごまラーメン）にも小麦が含まれる。グルテンフリー対応の明示はなく、食物アレルギーへの対応については事前に電話で確認が必要。専用フライヤーの有無も不明。
places.vegan_detail: もつ鍋（内臓肉）が看板メニューの肉料理専門店。植物性のみの選択肢はなく、ビーガン対応は不可。
places.neighborhood: 中区丸の内 / 市役所・久屋大通駅圏
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 愛知県一宮市出身。会社員を辞めて調理師専門学校へ進み、中華料理店での修業を経て大阪の餃子専門店でも腕を磨いた。その後、福岡の名門もつ鍋店が大阪に出した支店で約1年半の修業を積み、2007年に暖簾分けの形で名古屋・丸の内に「もつ藤」を開いた。

chef_bio.origin: 福岡・博多の名門もつ鍋から暖簾分け（2007年開業）
chef_bio.background: 愛知県一宮市出身。 会社員を辞めて調理師専門学校へ進み、中華料理店での修業を経て大阪の餃子専門店でも腕を磨いた。 その後、福岡の名門もつ鍋店が大阪に出した支店で約1年半の修業を積み、2007年に暖簾分けの形で名古屋・丸の内に「もつ藤」を開いた。
chef_bio.philosophy: 国産の新鮮な食材と九州の本場調味料にこだわり、くどさのないすっきりとした味わいを追求。 カウンターでは焼き物を目の前で仕上げるライブ感も楽しめる。
chef_bio.specialty: 博多水炊き風もつ鍋。 丁寧に下茹で・脂抜きした国産モツを、自家製醤油酢だれとゆずこしょうで食べるあっさりとした一鍋。

anecdotes.text: もつ鍋の上に餃子の皮を蓋のようにのせ、そのまま鍋に溶かし込むのが看板の食べ方。〆はちゃんぽんかごまラーメンで、テーブルごしに大量のごまをかけるパフォーマンスも名物のひとつ。
伊藤シェフは「餃子を作るとき、普通の水の代わりに熱湯を使うと生地の温度が保たれてパリッと仕上がる」と語る。

gf_cross_contamination.text: グルテンフリー対応や交差汚染への取り組みについて公式・口コミ上での言及はなく、詳細は不明。事前の電話確認が必須。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: もつ鍋の自家製たれに醤油ベースの酢だれを使用。一般的な醤油には小麦が含まれるため、セリアック病や小麦アレルギーの方は要注意。グルテンフリー醤油使用の記載なし。

vegan_cross_contact.text: もつ鍋専門店であり、全メニューが動物性食材を使用。ビーガン・ベジタリアン向けの選択肢および交差汚染への配慮についての情報はない。

staff_allergy_handling.text: アレルギー対応や食事制限への配慮に関する記載はウェブ上に見当たらない。来店前に電話（052-961-9525）で確認することを強く推奨。

cultural_comfort.note: 地元客中心の小さな居酒屋で、英語メニューや英語対応スタッフの記載はなく、日本語でのやり取りが基本となる。

places.name: 晴快荘 (Seikaisou)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free claims or allergen information published. Wheat risk is high on multiple fronts: soy sauce (tamari not mentioned) is standard in Japanese izakaya cooking and almost certainly used in simmered fish and dipping sauces; the lunch menu explicitly features フライ定食 (fried dishes), meaning wheat-battered items are fried in the kitchen; dashi-based broths may contain wheat-containing seasonings. No dedicated fryer. The restaurant has not addressed cross-contamination. Celiac diners should avoid.
places.vegan_detail: A seafood-specialist izakaya built around dashi-based broths (the signature mozuku tako shabu uses seafood stock), fish sashimi, simmered fish, and seasonal fugu courses. No plant-based or vegan options are indicated anywhere on the menu or website. Hidden dashi (bonito/seafood) is present in virtually every dish.
places.neighborhood: 丸の内・名古屋城駅徒歩5分
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 晴快荘は愛知県日間賀島で旅館「ホテル晴快荘」を営む一家が名古屋に開いた海鮮料理店。「島の幸を気軽に楽しんでほしい」という思いのもと、日間賀島産のとらふぐやタコ、季節の魚介を産地直送で提供する。本店は愛知県水産会館の地下1階という漁業ゆかりの場所に構え、1970年代から地元ビジネスパーソンや食通に愛され続けている。

chef_bio.background: 晴快荘は愛知県日間賀島で旅館「ホテル晴快荘」を営む一家が名古屋に開いた海鮮料理店。 「島の幸を気軽に楽しんでほしい」という思いのもと、日間賀島産のとらふぐやタコ、季節の魚介を産地直送で提供する。 本店は愛知県水産会館の地下1階という漁業ゆかりの場所に構え、1970年代から地元ビジネスパーソンや食通に愛され続けている。
chef_bio.philosophy: 日間賀島から直送した旬の食材を「お値打ちな価格でくつろいだ雰囲気で」提供することをモットーとする。
chef_bio.specialty: もずくたこしゃぶ（日間賀島産タコを香り高い出汁ともずく・ワカメと合わせる名物しゃぶしゃぶ）、季節のとらふぐコース（10月〜3月）

anecdotes.text: レビュアーは女将を「気さくなおばちゃん」と評し、カジュアルで温かみのある接客が常連客を引きつけていると記している。
ランチは週替わりの魚定食が人気で、開店前から行列ができることもあるほど地元の支持を集めている。

gf_cross_contamination.text: ランチメニューにフライ定食（揚げ物）が含まれており、同一キッチンで小麦を使用した揚げ物が調理されている。専用フライヤーの有無は不明だが、小麦との接触汚染リスクは高い。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: 醤油ベースの味付けが標準的な居酒屋・海鮮料理店であり、煮魚・タレ類に小麦を含む通常の醤油が使用されていると考えられる。グルテンフリー醤油（タマリ等）の使用は確認されていない。

vegan_cross_contact.text: 全メニューが魚介類・海産物を中心に構成されており、植物性食材のみの調理は現実的に不可能な環境。もずくたこしゃぶの出汁は魚介ベース。

staff_allergy_handling.text: アレルギー対応に関する情報はウェブサイト・口コミ・グルナビのいずれにも掲載なし。食物アレルギーや食事制限がある場合は事前に電話（052-971-3091）で直接確認することを推奨する。

cultural_comfort.note: Established izakaya catering to local Japanese clientele; no English-language menu or signage found — Japanese fluency or a translation app recommended.

places.name: めん処　幸の家 (Mendokoro Yukino-ya)
places.gf_detail: The core product is wheat noodles (udon and kishimen) and the restaurant has not documented any gluten-free alternatives. Soy sauce, miso, and wheat noodles are fundamental to every dish on the menu. No dedicated fryer or allergen accommodation is mentioned on any source. This is not a safe choice for anyone with celiac disease or wheat allergy.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built around miso-based noodle broths, pork cutlet (miso katsu), chicken dishes, tempura, and fried items. No plant-based or dashi-free options are documented anywhere. Traditional katsuobushi-based or pork-based dashi is standard in this style of cooking; assume nothing is vegan.
places.neighborhood: 西区数寄屋町 (Joshin / Nishi Ward)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The core product is wheat noodles (udon and kishimen) and the restaurant has not documented any gluten-free alternatives. Soy sauce, miso, and wheat noodles are fundamental to every dish on the menu. No dedicated fryer or allergen accommodation is mentioned on any source. This is

chef_bio.specialty: Miso-nikomi kishimen — flat Nagoya-style wheat noodles simmered in a rich, concentrated miso broth served piping hot in an earthenware pot

anecdotes.text: Multiple food writers describe the proprietor as a warm, motherly figure whose welcoming manner is as much a draw as the food itself.

A local food blog singles out the restaurant's proximity to Nagoya Castle and calls the miso-nikomi kishimen an authentic example of the city's flat-noodle tradition — a rarer find than miso-nikomi udon.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: All noodle dishes use wheat-based udon or kishimen noodles; the miso broth almost certainly contains wheat-based miso and standard soy sauce. No wheat-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth bases are traditional Japanese style; katsuobushi (bonito) or pork-derived dashi is standard in miso-nikomi preparations. No plant-based broth option is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional neighborhood noodle shop with no English menu or signage; the atmosphere is warm and the proprietor is described as welcoming, but Japanese is the working language.

places.name: そば処 甚家 (Sobadokoro Jinya)
places.gf_detail: Soba is buckwheat-based but standard Japanese soba shops almost universally use wheat-containing soy sauce in their tsuyu dashi broth, and tempura on the menu means wheat flour is present in the kitchen with high cross-contamination risk. No dedicated gluten-free preparation or labeling has been noted in any source. Celiac diners should ask before ordering; safe GF eating here is unlikely without direct confirmation from the kitchen.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centres on domburi bowls (oyakodon, butadon, tendon) and soba served in fish-based broth. Katsuobushi (bonito) dashi is strongly implied by the classic tsuyu style described by reviewers. No plant-based or vegan options were identified.
places.neighborhood: 浅間町駅周辺（名古屋市西区新道）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Soba is buckwheat-based but standard Japanese soba shops almost universally use wheat-containing soy sauce in their tsuyu dashi broth, and tempura on the menu means wheat flour is present in the kitchen with high cross-contamination risk. No dedicated gluten-free preparation or l

chef_bio.specialty: 梅おろしそば (plum daikon soba) and a legendary-value service set — onigiri plus dashimaki tamago for around ¥200 — that regulars order on every visit

anecdotes.text: One reviewer visited 10 times between June 2021 and July 2024, working through the whole menu from tanuki soba to tsukimi soba, calling the broth rustic, slightly sweet, and faintly acidic in the best old-school way.
The Showa-era retro interior and famously energetic, warm hospitality make it a reliable lunch stop for locals near Asamacho Station.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura is on the menu, meaning wheat flour is actively used in the kitchen. No source mentions a dedicated fryer or separate prep area for allergen-sensitive orders.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No source confirms wheat-free soy sauce. Standard Japanese tsuyu (the dipping/broth base for soba) contains wheat-based soy sauce; this shop has given no indication it deviates from that norm.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish-based dashi is implied by the classic broth flavor described by reviewers; pork and chicken bowls are also served, making a fully plant-based meal extremely unlikely.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling information appears in any reviewed source. With no English menu and a small cash-only operation, allergy communication will require Japanese or a translation aid.

positives.text: Soba noodles are buckwheat-based, which is inherently gluten-free before any broth or coating is applied — a useful starting point for a conversation with staff.

cultural_comfort.note: A small Showa-era retro shop where ordering is done in Japanese with no English menu observed; staff are noted as warm and welcoming to solo diners.

places.name: ザ・カワブン・ナゴヤ (The Kawabun Nagoya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or dedicated protocols confirmed. Italian courses include pasta, bread, and wheat-based sauces. Kitchen is not reported as celiac-aware. Wheat-containing soy sauce unlikely (Italian cuisine), but cross-contamination from pasta cooking is a real risk. Must inquire directly at reservation — no reliable basis for celiac diners to assume safety.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu. Courses are Italian-style multi-course using animal proteins (meat, seafood, dairy, eggs) as central ingredients. The Kenzo Estate wine partner page notes staff can accommodate guests with food restrictions, but no documented plant-based courses exist. Ask well in advance when booking.
places.neighborhood: 丸の内・市役所
places.notes: THE KAWABUN NAGOYA opened in 2008 on the site of Ryotei Kawabun, documented as Nagoya's oldest traditional restaurant, which served the Owari Tokugawa clan as a purveyor during the Edo period. The four-storey building incorporates Taisho-era (1912–1926) architectural elements — c

chef_bio.origin: 名古屋市中区丸の内 — built on the grounds of Ryotei Kawabun, Nagoya's oldest ryotei
chef_bio.background: THE KAWABUN NAGOYA opened in 2008 on the site of Ryotei Kawabun, documented as Nagoya's oldest traditional restaurant, which served the Owari Tokugawa clan as a purveyor during the Edo period. The four-storey building incorporates Taisho-era (1912–1926) architectural elements — carved wooden transoms, tiled roofs, and stone water basins — alongside a 400-year-old shiitake tree planted when Nagoya Castle was constructed. The venue is operated by Plan Do See Co. , Ltd. , a Tokyo-based hospitality group.
chef_bio.philosophy: Honor 400 years of Kawabun's hospitality legacy while pursuing modern Italian cuisine through simple cooking methods that let premium seasonal ingredients speak for themselves.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal Italian course dining (tasting courses) with ingredients personally sourced by the chef from producers across Japan, served against a backdrop of traditional Japanese garden views and open kitchen theatre.

anecdotes.text: The original Ryotei Kawabun hosted luminaries including Ito Hirobumi, and a 400-year-old shiitake tree on the grounds is said to have been planted as a landmark during the construction of Nagoya Castle.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Italian courses feature fresh pasta and wheat-based preparations; no separate gluten-free cooking line or dedicated equipment has been documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Italian kitchen is unlikely to rely on wheat-containing soy sauce, but dressings, sauces, and desserts may contain wheat; no ingredient list is publicly available.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Courses are built around meat, seafood, and dairy; shared kitchen surfaces and cookware make vegan cross-contact likely without prior arrangement.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The Kenzo Estate partner listing notes the restaurant can accommodate guests with food restrictions and international guests, but no formal allergy protocol is documented publicly.

positives.text: Course-based dining with advance reservations creates an opportunity to communicate dietary needs directly with the kitchen before arrival.

cultural_comfort.note: Operated by Plan Do See, a major hospitality group; staff experienced with international guests and formal dining etiquette; online reservations available in English via TableCheck.

places.name: みちのり弁当 (Michinori Bento)
places.gf_detail: Tokai region's first dedicated gluten-free bento shop. Wheat, barley, rye, and oats are excluded from all ingredients including seasonings and condiments. Gluten-free soy sauce and miso are sourced specifically. Contamination prevention is structural: no grain products are permitted on premises, all kitchen equipment is newly purchased (not leased), and staff uniforms are washed exclusively on-site. The founder (who has a wheat allergy) designed protocols to address even trace-protein reactions.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu. Standard bento boxes use fish, eggs, and/or meat (grilled mackerel, chicken karaage, chicken nanban, chikuwa). Some vegetable-based sides (kinpira gobo, potato salad, leafy greens) may be plant-based but shared kitchen preparation with animal products. Tabelog notes "vegetarian menu available" — inquire directly for current vegan-safe options.
places.neighborhood: Nishi-ku, Joshin
places.notes: Naco developed a wheat allergy as an adult and experienced first-hand the isolation of being unable to share meals with friends and family. Determined that no one should have to give up the pleasure of eating together, she trained as a gluten-free advisor and founded 株式会社テマトジカン (

chef_bio.background: Naco developed a wheat allergy as an adult and experienced first-hand the isolation of being unable to share meals with friends and family. Determined that no one should have to give up the pleasure of eating together, she trained as a gluten-free advisor and founded 株式会社テマトジカン (Tematojikan Co. , Ltd. ). みちのり弁当 opened January 27, 2023, in a lovingly repurposed 60-year-old wooden building in the Joshin neighborhood — chosen to reduce demolition waste, with reclaimed wood and a transparent glass front that lets passersby see into the kitchen.
chef_bio.philosophy: 「小麦アレルギーの人も、そうでない人も、一緒に食卓を囲み同じ食事で『おいしいね』を分かち合う」— People with and without wheat allergies can sit at the same table and share the joy of 'this is delicious. '
chef_bio.specialty: Rice-flour karaage bento — crispy chicken fried in rice flour batter, entirely wheat-free from coating to seasoning

anecdotes.text: Rather than lease kitchen equipment that might have contacted gluten, Naco purchased every piece of equipment new — a significant upfront cost that she absorbed to guarantee contamination-free preparation from day one.

The shop building itself is a sustainability statement: a 60-year-old wooden structure was renovated instead of demolished, with reclaimed wood used for interior fittings and glass walls installed so the kitchen is fully visible to customers.

みちのり弁当 was the first dedicated gluten-free bento shop in the entire Tokai region when it opened in January 2023, filling a gap Naco had experienced personally as a wheat-allergy sufferer living in Nagoya.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No grain products (wheat, barley, rye, oats) are permitted to enter the premises at all, eliminating the primary cross-contamination vector.
All kitchen equipment was purchased new rather than leased, ensuring no residual gluten from prior use.
Staff uniforms are washed exclusively on-site to prevent outside gluten introduction.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Gluten-free soy sauce and miso are specifically sourced; standard wheat-containing soy sauce and miso are not used in any preparation.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is not vegan-dedicated. Bento boxes regularly contain fish, chicken, and eggs; plant-based sides are prepared in the same kitchen environment.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The shop advises contacting them directly for allergen accommodation requests beyond gluten: 「食物アレルギー対応につきましてはお問い合わせください」(Please inquire about food allergy accommodations).
Protocols account for even trace-protein quantities sufficient to trigger allergic reactions, reflecting the founder's personal experience with wheat allergy.

positives.text: Tokai region's first dedicated gluten-free bento shop — the entire menu is safe for wheat allergy; no need to ask or modify orders.
The founder is herself a wheat-allergy sufferer and certified gluten-free advisor, bringing lived-experience credibility to every safety decision.

cultural_comfort.note: Listed on Tabelog's English site, accepts major international credit cards (Visa, Mastercard, JCB, AMEX), and offers delivery via Uber Eats and Wolt — accessible for non-Japanese speakers, though phone and in-person reservations are in Japanese.

places.name: WOK (WOK)
places.gf_detail: スリランカ料理はライスベースが基本でグルテンを含む食材が少ない傾向にあるが、醤油・小麦含有調味料の使用有無は不明。グルテンフリー対応の明示はなく、パパダム（豆粉製）は通常GF対応だが揚げ油の共用リスクは確認不可。セリアック等の方は必ず事前に電話（070-3810-4231）で確認を。
places.vegan_detail: メニューにベジタブルカレーが存在し、スリランカ料理はもともと豆・野菜ベースのカレーが豊富。ただしヴィーガン対応を明示した情報はなく、魚・鶏・豚カレーも提供。来店前に個別確認が必要。
places.neighborhood: 西区城北町（庄内通駅徒歩約11分）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 2026年6月21日オープン。スリランカ出身のオーナーが名古屋市西区城北町の目立たない路地裏（ハートイン城北町1C）で営む一軒。相場の半値ほどという圧倒的なコストパフォーマンスと、本場の味がそのまま皿に上る「ガチなスリランカ料理」として注目を集め始めている。

chef_bio.background: 2026年6月21日オープン。 スリランカ出身のオーナーが名古屋市西区城北町の目立たない路地裏（ハートイン城北町1C）で営む一軒。 相場の半値ほどという圧倒的なコストパフォーマンスと、本場の味がそのまま皿に上る「ガチなスリランカ料理」として注目を集め始めている。
chef_bio.philosophy: 本場の味をそのままに、手頃な価格で提供することをコンセプトとしている模様。
chef_bio.specialty: フィッシュカレー・チキンカレー・ベジタブルカレーなど本格スリランカカレー、ファルーダ（デザート）

positives.text: スリランカ料理はライス（米）を主食とし、豆・野菜・スパイスが主体のカレーが多いため、グルテンフリーに親和性がある料理文化といえる。

cultural_comfort.note: スリランカ人オーナーの個人経営店で英語対応の可能性あり。日本語が基本だが、身振りや指差しでの注文も通じると思われる。

places.name: ヴェジタブルカフェ&シーフードバー サイエン (Vegetable Cafe & Seafood Bar Saien)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free labeling or certification found on any platform. Menu includes soy-based dressings (standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat), burgers with buns, grain rice bowls, fried items (soft-shell crab burger, fried shrimp), and dessert shortcakes — all high-risk for gluten. Shared kitchen environment with fried items means cross-contamination risk. No dedicated fryer information available. Those with celiac disease or serious gluten sensitivity should inquire carefully with staff before ordering; safe options are not guaranteed.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is vegetable-forward at lunch with NYC-style chopped salads (romaine, red cabbage, spinach with dressing choices including soy-based and sesame-ginger), but the restaurant's core identity is an omnivore dining bar. Evening focus is on raw oysters, wagyu beef, and seafood. No dishes are labeled vegan. Soy-based dressings are on offer alongside other options. Hidden animal-based ingredients (bonito dashi, oyster sauce, etc.) in cooked items cannot be ruled out — ask staff before ordering.
places.neighborhood: 市役所駅・中区二の丸（名古屋城東側金シャチ横丁宗春ゾーン）
places.cuisine: オーガニック野菜・シーフード・牡蠣・ダイニングバー
places.notes: Saien opened on March 29, 2018, as part of the Soharu Zone of Kinshachi Yokocho, a food-and-culture complex built along the eastern edge of Nagoya Castle. The Soharu Zone was conceived to showcase ambitious, creative restaurateurs bringing fresh ideas to Nagoya, and Saien was one

chef_bio.background: Saien opened on March 29, 2018, as part of the Soharu Zone of Kinshachi Yokocho, a food-and-culture complex built along the eastern edge of Nagoya Castle. The Soharu Zone was conceived to showcase ambitious, creative restaurateurs bringing fresh ideas to Nagoya, and Saien was one of its founding tenants.
chef_bio.philosophy: The restaurant's founding concept centered on bringing New York-style salad culture to Nagoya: self-cultivated organic vegetables paired with daily market-sourced seafood, aiming for cuisine described as 'beautiful, delicious, and body-friendly. ' Over time the concept evolved into a broader dining bar with a strong oyster and wagyu focus in the evenings while maintaining the fresh-vegetable-and-seafood identity at lunch.
chef_bio.specialty: Soft-shell crab burger — a whole soft-shell crab tucked between buns — is the venue's most talked-about item and a recurring centerpiece of visitor photos and reviews.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's signature 'five-minute shelf-life' seasonal shortcake — a dessert intentionally marketed as perishable to signal ultra-fresh cream — became a social media talking point among Kinshachi Yokocho visitors.
The soft-shell crab burger, priced around ¥980, features a whole crab and drew consistent praise in visitor blogs as an unusual and photogenic item not found at typical Nagoya spots.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Fried items (soft-shell crab burger, fried shrimp, miso cutlet) are on the menu and likely share kitchen fryers with other dishes. No dedicated GF fryer information is publicly available.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy-based dressings are offered on salads, and soy sauce is a standard ingredient in Japanese cuisine — standard Japanese soy sauce (醤油) contains wheat. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternatives are mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen prepares raw oysters, wagyu beef, seafood, and fried items alongside vegetable dishes. No separation between vegan and omnivore prep is indicated.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy handling protocol is mentioned on any official or third-party platform. Phone reservations are accepted at 052-212-8553, which would be the best avenue to inquire about specific dietary needs before visiting.

positives.text: The restaurant's concept emphasizes self-cultivated organic vegetables and daily fresh market seafood, suggesting ingredient quality awareness even if formal allergy protocols are not advertised.

cultural_comfort.note: Located inside the tourist-oriented Kinshachi Yokocho complex at Nagoya Castle — an English-friendly environment designed to welcome international visitors, with multilingual signage common in the area.

places.name: 喫茶ニューポピー (Kissa New Poppy)
places.gf_detail: The entire menu is built around wheat: thick-cut toast, spaghetti, and bread rolls are the core offerings. No gluten-free alternatives exist. Soy sauce likely appears in the Napolitan and other savory dishes. No dedicated fryer information available, but there is no indication of fryer use. This venue is not appropriate for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is a classic Nagoya kissaten lineup: toast with red bean paste and butter, teppan spaghetti (Napolitan), curry rice, and morning sets with egg and salad. No vegan-designated dishes exist. Hidden animal products are the norm here — butter in toast, egg in morning sets, likely milk in coffee drinks. Coffee drinks (black) would be the only reliably vegan option. Ask (in Japanese) if a specific item can be modified.
places.hours_raw: 月～水・日 8:00～18:00、金・土 8:00～22:00、木曜定休（第2水曜も休みの場合あり）
places.neighborhood: 那古野・四間道 (Nagono / Shikemichi historic district), Nishi-ku —
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] New Poppy is the second-generation revival of 喫茶ポピー, the coffee house Masashi Bito's parents opened two years before his birth and ran in the Meieki district from 1977 until December 2010, when building deterioration forced its closure after 34 years. Masashi worked alongside his

roles: Owner / roaster — 尾藤雅士 (Masashi Bito)
Floor staff — 尾藤孝子 (Takako Bito), Masashi's mother
chef_bio.origin: Nagoya, Aichi — historic Shikemichi (四間道) district, Nishi-ku
chef_bio.background: New Poppy is the second-generation revival of 喫茶ポピー, the coffee house Masashi Bito's parents opened two years before his birth and ran in the Meieki district from 1977 until December 2010, when building deterioration forced its closure after 34 years. Masashi worked alongside his parents during the shop's final three years, catching the coffee bug. He went on to establish BEANS BITOU, a specialty roasting and wholesale company, and ran 喫茶神戸館 in Fushimi before opening 喫茶ニューポピー in January 2019 in the historic Shikemichi preservation district of Nagono — a neighborhood with deep personal resonance: his great-grandfather once lived near Gojo Bridge there, and his father is buried in the area. His mother Takako (尾藤孝子) works alongside him, making this a literal mother-and-son continuation of a family story.
chef_bio.philosophy: Masashi chose the word 'New' deliberately: to inherit tradition while creating something genuinely new. He wanted a space where people could sit without urgency — his study of kissaten across Japan convinced him that a shop's history is its most irreplaceable asset. He offers 'Story Coffee' (物語珈琲), custom wedding-favor blends built from couples' personal narratives.
chef_bio.specialty: Teppan Ogura Toast (鉄板小倉トースト) — thick-cut bread delivered on a sizzling iron plate, spread with black sesame paste and sweet red bean, crowned with melting vanilla ice cream. House-roasted Poppy Blend and New Poppy Blend coffees are also a draw.

anecdotes.text: Masashi's great-grandfather once lived near Gojo Bridge in Nagono, and his father is buried in the same neighborhood — connections he describes as feeling 'invited' to bring the family's kissaten back to this exact block of the city.

The original 喫茶ポピー opened in 1977, two years before Masashi was born; he grew up in and around the shop, then watched his mother run it alone after his father's early death before taking over himself at age 28.

New Poppy's Masu Presso collaboration pairs direct-fire-roasted coffee with 枡 (traditional wooden measuring boxes) from Ohashi Ryoki, a century-old Ogaki craftsman — described by the shop as 'the world's first marriage' of the two.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Wheat is the central ingredient across the entire menu — thick-cut toast, spaghetti, and bread rolls are the core offerings. No source mentions gluten-free soy sauce or wheat-free alternatives.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Morning set includes boiled egg and toast with butter; coffee drinks are served with milk by default. No plant-based milk alternative is mentioned in any source.

cultural_comfort.note: A beloved local kissaten in a historic district — menus are in Japanese with no English translation; staff may have limited English, but the ordering process is simple and the atmosphere is warm and welcoming.

places.name: らーめん 柊 (Ramen Hiiragi)
places.gf_detail: Standard ramen shop: the shoyu (soy sauce) broth contains wheat via conventional soy sauce. Noodles are wheat-based. No tamari or GF alternatives noted anywhere. No allergen menu, no staff allergy-handling protocol found. This place is not safe for celiac or serious wheat-sensitive diners.
places.vegan_detail: Both ramen varieties use a pork-bone and seafood-blended broth; the signature chashu pork is the headline topping. No plant-based broth option exists. Vegan diners have nothing suitable here.
places.neighborhood: 浅間町（西区）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened on January 8, 2018 in a quiet residential side street a short walk from Asama-cho Station. The space is styled like a cozy hideout — warm wooden counter, intimate atmosphere — and has built a loyal neighbourhood following since opening.

chef_bio.background: Opened on January 8, 2018 in a quiet residential side street a short walk from Asama-cho Station. The space is styled like a cozy hideout — warm wooden counter, intimate atmosphere — and has built a loyal neighbourhood following since opening.
chef_bio.philosophy: A stripped-back, solo-diner-friendly spot that keeps the menu tight and the quality high.
chef_bio.specialty: Low-temperature slow-cooked chashu pork, praised across reviews as 'melt-in-your-mouth. ' Offered in both shoyu (soy sauce) and shio (salt) broths built on a pork-bone and seafood base.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers consistently single out the chashu as the star of the bowl, with one describing it as 'exquisite, melt-in-your-mouth' — the result of precise low-temperature cooking.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Standard ramen kitchen with no GF protocols noted; wheat noodles cooked in shared water, wheat-based soy sauce used in broth.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Shoyu (soy sauce) variety uses conventional soy sauce which contains wheat. No tamari or wheat-free soy sauce alternative mentioned on any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Entire menu is built around pork-bone and seafood broth; no plant-based option exists, so cross-contact is total and unavoidable.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 14-seat counter shop with no English signage or staff language noted; ordering is straightforward (point at menu items), but expect a fully Japanese-language environment.

places.name: アンティキ (Antichi)
places.gf_detail: グルテンフリー対応の明示はなし。コースにはパスタ（小麦粉）が含まれており、タリアテッレ等が定番メニューに登場する。醤油使用の記載はないがイタリアンベースなので醤油・味噌は少ないと思われるが確認が必要。パン・パスタを除いた対応については予約時に直接相談することを強く推奨。セリアック病の方は専用厨房・グルテンフリー保証がないため注意が必要。
places.vegan_detail: 完全なヴィーガンメニューは設定されていない。コースは魚介・肉類が主体で、バター・チーズ・生クリームを多用するイタリア料理。契約農家の無農薬野菜を豊富に使用しており、予約時にリクエストすれば対応を検討してもらえる可能性はあるが（「苦手な食材があれば申し付けるよう」との案内あり）、ヴィーガン対応を明示的に保証する情報はない。
places.neighborhood: 東区上竪杉町（白壁地区）
places.notes: 明治時代（約130〜140年前）に建てられた古民家を改装した隠れ家イタリアン。名古屋・東区の白壁地区に位置し、アンティークな調度品と年代物の建築が醸し出す「時間が止まったような」空間が特徴。オーナーが北イタリアのレストランで歴史的建築とモダンな料理が融合するスタイルに触れ、名古屋でも同様のコンセプトを実現した。

chef_bio.origin: 名古屋市東区上竪杉町（白壁地区）
chef_bio.background: 明治時代（約130〜140年前）に建てられた古民家を改装した隠れ家イタリアン。 名古屋・東区の白壁地区に位置し、アンティークな調度品と年代物の建築が醸し出す「時間が止まったような」空間が特徴。 オーナーが北イタリアのレストランで歴史的建築とモダンな料理が融合するスタイルに触れ、名古屋でも同様のコンセプトを実現した。
chef_bio.philosophy: 「サービス、料理、空間のすべてに幸せを感じてもらいたい」（支配人・尾関慎二氏）。 日本ならではの旬の素材に徹底的にこだわり、契約農家から届く無農薬野菜、厳選された国産食材を四季に合わせて使用。 イタリアワインはソムリエが選んだ60種以上を用意。
chef_bio.specialty: 旬の食材を活かしたイタリアンコース料理。 ジビエ（若鳩・山鴫・鶉・キジ・鴨）、三河一色産のハモ、五島列島のアワビ、国産黒毛和牛、日本のタイガープロウンなど、日本の旬食材をイタリアン技法で仕上げる。

anecdotes.text: 建物は明治時代築約130〜140年の古民家を改装。ワインセラーも改装した内装に組み込まれており、「時間が止まったような」雰囲気と評されている。
二階には1日1組限定で利用できる個室があり、プロポーズや記念日のシーンに利用されることで知られる。
支配人の尾関慎二氏は「お客様が何を好み、何が苦手かを事前に把握し、おいしく食べていただけるように工夫します」と語っており、コース内容の柔軟な対応を示唆している。

gf_cross_contamination.text: グルテンフリー専用の調理環境・フライヤーについての情報はなし。コースにパスタ（タリアテッレ等）が含まれるため厨房での小麦粉の使用は常態的と考えられる。セリアック病レベルの対応確認は予約時に直接必要。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: メニューに醤油の記載なし。イタリアン主体の調理のため醤油・味噌の使用は限定的と推測されるが、和食材（ハモ、和牛、ウニ等）使用時の調味料については確認が必要。

vegan_cross_contact.text: バター・チーズ・生クリームが標準的に使用されるイタリアンコース構成。動物性食材の排除については予約時のリクエストが必要。

staff_allergy_handling.text: 予約時にアレルギーや苦手な食材をコメント欄で伝えるよう案内されており、スタッフは対応を検討してくれる姿勢がある（「おいしく食べていただけるように工夫します」）。ただし書面によるアレルゲン対応保証の情報はない。

positives.text: 契約農家からの無農薬野菜を多用しており、野菜の質と鮮度へのこだわりは高い。コース内に豊富な野菜料理が含まれる。

cultural_comfort.note: 高級古民家イタリアン。日本語主体の環境だが、コース料理の流れは国際的で、英語対応の可否は不明。予約時に英語でのコミュニケーションは確認が必要。

places.name: 鳥開総本家 金シャチ横丁店 (Torikai Sohonke Kinshachi Yokocho)
places.gf_detail: The restaurant uses standard wheat-containing soy sauce in both the tebasaki wing sauce and the oyakodon tare. The tebasaki is deep-fried (battered/coated) — wheat is present in the breading. The oyakodon dashi contains bonito; the soy-based sauce is not tamari. No gluten-free options, dedicated fryer, or allergen-substitution program are documented anywhere. Celiac-unsafe; avoid.
places.vegan_detail: Entirely a chicken-specialist restaurant. Every dish centres on Nagoya Cochin or domestic chicken. The dashi base is made from Kyushu bonito flakes (katsuo), and the signature oyakodon uses chicken and egg. No plant-based or vegan options are offered or documented anywhere on the official site or third-party sources.
places.hours_raw: 10:30–17:30 (last order 17:00) daily; closed on days Nagoya Castle is closed (irregular)
places.neighborhood: 中区・名古屋城 (Nakaku, Nagoya Castle area — Kinshachi Yokocho, Gis
places.cuisine: 鶏料理・親子丼・手羽先
