places.notes: Nishimura Kazunori grew up in the Ise-Shima fishing community with a fisherman father. Despite those coastal roots, it was chicken — specifically the joy of biting into wings at childhood Christmas dinners — that became his culinary north star. In 2000 he opened a small 10-tsubo

chef_bio.origin: Nagoya, Aichi — founded at Nagoya Station West Exit 2000; Kinshachi Yokocho branch opened in the Gishoku (Yoshinao) Zone of the castle precinct.
chef_bio.background: Nishimura Kazunori grew up in the Ise-Shima fishing community with a fisherman father. Despite those coastal roots, it was chicken — specifically the joy of biting into wings at childhood Christmas dinners — that became his culinary north star. In 2000 he opened a small 10-tsubo izakaya called Torikai at Nagoya Station's west exit. The turning point came at the 2005 Aichi Expo, where he ran a booth selling both branded Nagoya Cochin and unbranded chicken side by side: despite a price difference of nearly 10x, diners overwhelmingly chose the Nagoya Cochin. That moment convinced him that quality provenance could command loyalty. In 2007 he incorporated as Progre Corp. and rebranded the operation as Torikai Sohonke, pivoting the menu to centre on oyakodon and tebasaki made exclusively with Nagoya Cochin. The Kinshachi Yokocho branch, opened inside the newly developed tourism precinct at Nagoya Castle's main gate, carries that flagship identity into one of the city's most historically resonant settings.
chef_bio.philosophy: 鳥開総本家's guiding principle is: 'a chicken specialist shop that has consistently prioritized authenticity since its founding' (創業当時から本物にこだわり続けた鶏専門店). This means sourcing only Nagoya Cochin raised for 120+ days — more than double the broiler standard — to achieve the breed's characteristic firm texture and depth of flavour. The wing sauce is a proprietary tare replenished gradually in its original pot, so that each new batch absorbs the complexity of every batch before it.
chef_bio.specialty: 特撰名古屋コーチン親子丼 (premium Nagoya Cochin oyakodon) and 名古屋コーチン手羽先唐揚げ (Nagoya Cochin tebasaki fried wings)

anecdotes.text: At the 2005 Aichi World Expo, Nishimura sold Nagoya Cochin and unbranded chicken from the same booth. The Nagoya Cochin — priced nearly 10 times higher — outsold the generic product decisively. The experience convinced him to rebuild the brand entirely around premium provenance.

Nishimura traces the oyakodon's slightly sweet, egg-rich flavour profile to a childhood memory: eating sukiyaki leftovers the morning after, stirring the sweet broth into rice and raw egg. That combination became the emotional blueprint for the restaurant's signature dish.

The tebasaki wing sauce is kept in a traditional tare pot that is never fully emptied — new sauce is added to the residue of previous batches, building cumulative depth. The restaurant has won the Japan Karaage Association Grand Prix (tebasaki division) three consecutive years at the highest gold level, and nine gold awards at the National Donburi Grand Prix (oyakodon division).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tebasaki (fried chicken wings) are coated and deep-fried. No dedicated fryer for any allergen-free preparation is documented. Cross-contamination with wheat from frying is a realistic risk.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The cooking philosophy page confirms the use of 'Central Japan soy sauce marinade' (中日本しょうゆダレ) in the karaage and wing preparations. Standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat; no tamari or wheat-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere on the official site or third-party sources.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is built on chicken and chicken-derived ingredients (egg, bonito dashi). There is no plant-based preparation at this restaurant; vegan cross-contact is unavoidable throughout.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen declaration, staff allergy protocol, or dietary accommodation program is documented on the official site, Tabelog listing, Kinshachi Yokocho operator page, or any third-party source reviewed. Diners with serious allergies should contact the restaurant directly before visiting.

cultural_comfort.note: Located inside a purpose-built tourist precinct at Nagoya Castle, this shop is well-accustomed to international visitors; signage and menus are available in multiple languages, and staff at Kinshachi Yokocho venues regularly handle non-Japanese guests.

places.name: 山本屋総本家 金シャチ横丁店 (Yamamotoya Souhonke Kinshachi Yokocho)
places.gf_detail: Noodles are made from 100% domestic wheat flour — wheat is the central ingredient in every dish. The dashi and miso tare both contain soy sauce (wheat). Buckwheat flour is used as dusting powder on the noodles, adding a soba cross-contact risk on top of direct gluten. No gluten-free noodle substitution is offered. This restaurant is not suitable for anyone avoiding gluten.
places.vegan_detail: All udon and kishimen dishes are simmered in a broth made from katsuobushi (bonito), mackerel, sardine, kombu, and shiitake. The miso base blends Kakukyu Hacho miso with white miso and uses soy sauce; no plant-based broth option exists. Side dishes include beef tendon, oden with fish products, and chicken. There is no vegan pathway on this menu.
places.neighborhood: 中区三の丸、名古屋城駅直近（金シャチ横丁義直ゾーン）
places.cuisine: 味噌煮込みうどん・きしめん
places.notes: 山本屋総本家は大正14年（1925年）に創業し、2025年に創業100周年を迎えた名古屋を代表する味噌煮込みうどんの老舗。現在は町田福之氏が代表を務め、両親から受け継いだ暖簾を守る。

roles: 老舗チェーン（名古屋市内5店舗）
名古屋めし観光スポット代表格
金シャチ横丁の主要テナント
chef_bio.background: 山本屋総本家は大正14年（1925年）に創業し、2025年に創業100周年を迎えた名古屋を代表する味噌煮込みうどんの老舗。 現在は町田福之氏が代表を務め、両親から受け継いだ暖簾を守る。
chef_bio.philosophy: 「毎年、小麦の品質も味噌の品質も変わる。 それでも変わらぬ味を守るため、毎年ブレンドや配合を調整し続けている」（町田福之代表）。 創業時の味と食べ方をそのまま守り続けることを最優先としている。
chef_bio.specialty: 味噌煮込みうどん：愛知県岡崎産カクキューの八丁味噌と白味噌をブレンドしたコクのある味噌だしに、国産小麦100%の生麺を土鍋のままグツグツと煮込む。 蓋を取り皿として使う独自の食べ方も創業当時から変わらない。

anecdotes.text: 麺には「コシ・太さ・鮮度・打ち粉・素材」の五つのこだわりがあり、毎日製造した生麺のみを使用。冷蔵保存は一切行わない。
名古屋城の金シャチにちなんで考案した「金シャチ煮込うどん」（¥2,420）は、エビの天ぷらを金シャチに見立てたこの店舗限定の一品。
麺の打ち粉にそば粉を使用しており、そばアレルギーの顧客からの問い合わせを受けて成分表示を明示している。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Noodles are made entirely from wheat flour; wheat is inseparable from the dish. No gluten-free alternative exists.
Buckwheat (soba) flour is used as dusting powder on all udon noodles, creating an additional cross-contact risk for both gluten-sensitive and soba-allergic diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The miso tare contains soy sauce (shoyu), which contains wheat. Allergen labeling for Yamamotoya products explicitly lists wheat (小麦) as a declared allergen across noodles, miso blend, and broth components.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is made from katsuobushi (bonito), mackerel, mackerel pike, and sardine. The miso blend contains bonito and mackerel. No plant-only broth option is available.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No information found about staff allergy handling procedures at the restaurant. Given the allergen complexity (wheat in noodles and broth, soba dusting powder, multiple fish), diners with serious allergies should contact the restaurant directly before visiting.

cultural_comfort.note: Tourist-area location adjacent to Nagoya Castle with international visitors in mind, but menus and staff interaction are primarily in Japanese; some English menus available given the Kinshachi Yokocho tourist setting.

places.name: sourire食堂 (Sourire Shokudo)
places.gf_detail: This is a pasta-focused Italian restaurant; wheat pasta is the core product. No gluten-free pasta option or GF labeling has been identified. Cross-contamination risk is high in a pasta kitchen. Celiacs should not assume safety here and would need to call ahead (052-325-5615) to ask directly.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on pasta sets with soup, salad, focaccia, and quiche — all components likely contain dairy or egg. No vegan-specific labeling or menu found. Diner should ask about individual dishes; some oil-based pasta may be adaptable.
places.neighborhood: 浄心駅 徒歩8分（西区城西）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] sourire食堂 opened in December 2023 at a corner spot in the residential Johsai neighborhood of Nagoya's Nishi Ward, taking over a space that previously housed the izakaya Kushiyoshi. The new kitchen kept alive the pasta that regulars had loved at the former occupant, reframing it w

chef_bio.background: sourire食堂 opened in December 2023 at a corner spot in the residential Johsai neighborhood of Nagoya's Nishi Ward, taking over a space that previously housed the izakaya Kushiyoshi. The new kitchen kept alive the pasta that regulars had loved at the former occupant, reframing it within a relaxed shokudo format.
chef_bio.specialty: Daily-rotating pasta sets — generous portions of seasonal pasta (tomato, oil-based with ingredients like firefly squid) accompanied by salad, soup, and focaccia bread.

anecdotes.text: A diner who visited early on noted the interior was 'シャレオツやん' (quite sophisticated/chic) and rated the value as '超抜群' (outstanding), saying both pasta dishes came with abundant fresh ingredients at a very reasonable price.

The restaurant shifts character across the day — opening as a morning café from 7:30 AM, pivoting to a lunch set spot by midday, then transforming into an Italian bar-style dinner venue in the evening.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a pasta-specialist kitchen; wheat flour is the primary ingredient in virtually every dish. Cross-contamination risk for celiac diners is high and has not been addressed in any public source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pasta sets include soup, bread (focaccia), and quiche, all of which likely contain dairy or egg. No plant-based alternatives have been noted in any review or listing.

cultural_comfort.note: Casual neighborhood Italian café in a residential area; menus and signage are in Japanese, but the approachable shokudo format means pointing at daily specials boards works fine.

places.name: 〇〇処 こっこや (Marumaru-dokoro Kokkoya)
places.gf_detail: Gluten is pervasive: all lunch sets feature deep-fried items in wheat breading (karaage, tonkatsu, fry sets), conventional wheat-containing soy sauce is used throughout, and there is no dedicated fryer or GF menu. This is not a safe venue for anyone with celiac disease or serious wheat allergy.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu revolves around chicken, beef, seafood, and pork. No plant-based dishes are listed, and no vegan accommodation is mentioned on any platform. Dashi and animal stocks are standard in izakaya cooking; this place is no exception.
places.neighborhood: 丸の内 / 久屋大通
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Gluten is pervasive: all lunch sets feature deep-fried items in wheat breading (karaage, tonkatsu, fry sets), conventional wheat-containing soy sauce is used throughout, and there is no dedicated fryer or GF menu. This is not a safe venue for anyone with celiac disease or serious

chef_bio.specialty: 丸鳥の丸焼き (whole roasted chicken)

anecdotes.text: Reviewers describe the interior as having a deeply nostalgic Showa-era atmosphere — the kind of izakaya that feels like it hasn't changed in decades, drawing salary workers and neighbourhood regulars who treat it as their go-to local spot.
The whole roasted chicken (丸鳥の丸焼き) at ¥1,680 is the crowd-pleaser, and draft beer at ¥390 keeps the cost-performance ratio firmly in regulars' favour.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All lunch sets are deep-fried in wheat breading in a shared kitchen; no information about fryer separation is available.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard wheat-containing soy sauce is a core ingredient across the menu — house tare, miso preparations, and grilled chicken sauces all involve conventional shoyu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: There are no plant-based dishes on the menu; animal proteins are present in every listed item.

cultural_comfort.note: A classic salaryman izakaya with a Showa-era feel — predominantly Japanese-speaking clientele, no English menus, and a lived-in neighbourhood-pub atmosphere where regulars are at home.

places.name: 彩膳中華　杏ズ (Saizen Chuka Anzu)
places.gf_detail: The restaurant advertises gluten-free adaptation (グルテンフリー対応) and a TripAdvisor reviewer reported a six-course vegan gluten-free dinner. However, the signature vegan dandan noodles use egg-free domestic wheat noodles — not gluten-free. No tamari or wheat-free soy sauce is specifically confirmed in any source; standard Chinese soy sauce contains wheat. No dedicated GF fryer or preparation area confirmed. Cross-contamination protocols not documented. Celiacs should communicate needs clearly in advance; ask specifically about soy sauce and noodle alternatives before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: Several dishes are marked with a "V" symbol and can be made vegan for an additional ¥200 (e.g., oil-fried chicken with soy meat, yuba fried rice, dandan noodles, mapo tofu, sweet and sour pork with soy meat). The restaurant also accommodates five-allium-free (五葷抜き) Buddhist dietary requests. It is not a fully vegan restaurant — omnivore dishes share the kitchen. Hidden animal products: no dashi or katsuo mentioned in vegan dishes; house-made soy sauce koji and salt koji used, brown sugar for sweetness.
places.neighborhood: 中区 丸の内 (Naka Ward, Marunouchi)
places.notes: Owner-chef Ai Hiroshi holds international certification as a medicinal cooking instructor (国際薬膳調理師) and previously served as head chef at a renowned hotel before opening this intimate 15-seat spot in Nagoya's Marunouchi district. The restaurant is a husband-and-wife operation; th

roles: international medicinal cooking instructor (国際薬膳調理師)
culinary instructor at Nagoya Tsuji Culinary Professional School
chef_bio.background: Owner-chef Ai Hiroshi holds international certification as a medicinal cooking instructor (国際薬膳調理師) and previously served as head chef at a renowned hotel before opening this intimate 15-seat spot in Nagoya's Marunouchi district. The restaurant is a husband-and-wife operation; the wife prepares desserts including refined-sugar-free tarts and whole cakes available for special occasions.
chef_bio.philosophy: Anzu builds Chinese cuisine on a foundation of traditional Chinese medicinal dietary principles (薬膳), using no chemical seasonings or refined white sugar. Oils are rice oil and non-GMO soy oil; sweetness comes from unrefined brown sugar (粗糖). The kitchen makes its own salt koji, soy sauce koji, and hon-mirin seasonally. The chef actively visits local producers — sourcing lotus root from 田島蓮園 and strawberries from かとう農園 — and holds cooking classes five times a year aligned with the 24 solar terms. The stated goal is that every diner, regardless of dietary restriction, receives the same quality meal rather than a stripped-down substitute.
chef_bio.specialty: Vegan dandan noodles (ベジ担々麺) and soy-meat sweet and sour pork with black vinegar are signature dishes, alongside mapo tofu and seasonal medicinal lunch sets.

anecdotes.text: A TripAdvisor reviewer described bringing one vegan and three omnivores and receiving a six-course meal that was simultaneously vegan and gluten-free: 'We all enjoyed this wonderful vegan gluten-free deliciousness! Highly recommend giving them a try!'

A note.com natural-food reviewer described the chef as bringing a rare approach: instead of simply removing ingredients, he redesigns dishes so the vegan version stands on its own merits — including a plant-based egg made from potato and pumpkin.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free preparation area or cross-contamination protocols are documented in any available source. The kitchen prepares wheat-based noodles (the vegan dandan noodles use egg-free domestic wheat noodles) alongside adapted GF dishes.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The kitchen uses homemade soy sauce koji (醤油麹) as a core condiment. No source confirms wheat-free or tamari soy sauce is used. Standard soy sauce in Chinese cooking contains wheat; celiacs must ask explicitly whether a wheat-free alternative is available for their specific dishes.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Not a fully vegan restaurant — meat and seafood dishes are prepared in the same kitchen. No source documents whether dedicated cookware or utensils are used for vegan preparations.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Multiple reviewers confirm staff ask about dietary preferences and allergies at the table, and the owner-chef explains each dish personally — suggesting genuine attentiveness rather than a scripted allergy protocol.

positives.text: No chemical seasonings (MSG) or refined white sugar used anywhere in the kitchen.
Staff proactively ask about dietary restrictions; the chef personally brings dishes to the table and explains every ingredient, enabling real-time dialogue about allergen concerns.
English menu clearly marks vegan options, reducing communication friction for non-Japanese-speaking diners with dietary needs.

cultural_comfort.note: English menus are available with vegan options clearly marked; multiple foreign reviewers confirm the chef proactively explains each dish and staff ask about dietary restrictions at the table.

places.name: 鮨旬美 西川 (Sushi Shunbi Nishikawa)
places.gf_detail: Standard Japanese soy sauce (containing wheat) is used throughout the nigiri service. Dashi is made from fish bones. No gluten-free protocols or dedicated preparation areas are documented. The restaurant's own policy explicitly turns away guests with severe allergies or broad food restrictions, making it incompatible with celiac requirements.
places.vegan_detail: Pure seafood omakase — every course centres on fish, shellfish, and roe. There is no plant-based alternative and the restaurant explicitly declines reservations from guests with extensive ingredient restrictions.
places.neighborhood: 名古屋城近く・浅間町（エスパシオ ナゴヤキャッスル 4F）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Chef Nishikawa spent over 20 years honing his craft in Japanese cuisine before opening this restaurant, choosing sushi specifically because the counter format lets him interact directly with each guest — a style he felt matched his personality. The restaurant originally opened in

chef_bio.origin: Nagoya, Aichi (originally Shimizu; relocated to Meieki 2018; current Espacio location from October 2025)
chef_bio.background: Chef Nishikawa spent over 20 years honing his craft in Japanese cuisine before opening this restaurant, choosing sushi specifically because the counter format lets him interact directly with each guest — a style he felt matched his personality. The restaurant originally opened in Shimizu before relocating to the Meieki area in 2018. In October 2025 it moved again to its current home on the fourth floor of the newly opened luxury hotel Espacio Nagoya Castle, just steps from the castle keep.
chef_bio.philosophy: "To provide enjoyable dining experiences — that is our only goal. " Nishikawa sources ingredients each morning only for that day's confirmed guests, and applies what he calls a 'golden ratio' of salt and aging time tailored to each fish variety to concentrate umami.
chef_bio.specialty: Kuruma ebi (tiger prawn) sourced from Mikawa Isshiki, each large shrimp yielding two contrasting preparations — one chilled immediately after boiling for a snappy texture, the other fully cooked to draw out natural sweetness.

anecdotes.text: When offered a television appearance, Nishikawa declined — explaining that he could only give passing grades to his own dishes, reflecting standards too high to broadcast.
Diners at the Espacio counter look directly onto the floodlit silhouette of Nagoya Castle, making the backdrop as much a part of the experience as the food.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free preparation area or protocol is documented. In a small 8-seat counter kitchen handling multiple courses simultaneously, cross-contamination from soy sauce and other wheat-containing condiments is essentially certain.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) is used in nigiri preparation. No mention of tamari or gluten-free soy sauce alternatives in any reviewed source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every course involves seafood; fish-bone dashi is used for appetiser preparations. There is no plant-based track and no separation of animal products.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant's official policy is to decline reservations from guests with severe allergies or broad ingredient restrictions, citing incompatibility with the omakase format.

cultural_comfort.note: Counter-only omakase with direct chef interaction; the chef is known for being warm and conversational, but the experience is conducted almost entirely in Japanese with no documented English service.

places.name: 名古屋市市政資料館 喫茶室 (Nagoya City Archives Kissashitsu)
places.gf_detail: Signature items are toast and sandwiches made on wheat bread — gluten is central to the menu. No gluten-free alternatives are advertised. Standard kissaten soy sauce is likely wheat-based. No mention of a dedicated fryer (cafe does not serve fried food). Those with celiac disease should be aware that wheat-based bread is the main food offering and cross-contamination is not addressed anywhere in available sources.
places.vegan_detail: Menu is simple kissaten fare: mixed sandwiches (egg, ham, potato salad fillings), ogura toast, and coffee. No dedicated vegan options; most sandwiches contain egg and/or ham. Beverages (drip coffee, lemonade, cream soda) are likely plant-based, but fillings should be verified on arrival. No explicit vegan labeling noted on any source.
places.hours_raw: 火〜日 9:00〜16:45（LO 16:15）、月曜定休・毎月第3木曜定休（祝日の場合は翌日または第4木曜）、12/29〜1/3休館
places.neighborhood: 東大手・白壁（東区）
places.cuisine: 喫茶・軽食・トースト
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The kissashitsu opened in 2003 inside the Nagoya City Archives building (名古屋市市政資料館), a striking Neo-Baroque brick structure completed in 1922 as the Nagoya Court of Appeals and District Court. The building served judicial functions until 1979, when it was repurposed as a civic ar

chef_bio.background: The kissashitsu opened in 2003 inside the Nagoya City Archives building (名古屋市市政資料館), a striking Neo-Baroque brick structure completed in 1922 as the Nagoya Court of Appeals and District Court. The building served judicial functions until 1979, when it was repurposed as a civic archives and designated an Important Cultural Property. The cafe occupies a former courtroom on the second floor, its original wooden furniture and high ceilings preserved largely intact.
chef_bio.philosophy: Unhurried, inexpensive refreshment in a space that doubles as living history — visitors rest among marble staircases, stained-glass windows depicting scales of justice, and the quiet hum of an archive. The cafe makes no effort to modernize; its retro character is the point.
chef_bio.specialty: ミックスサンド（混合サンドイッチ）and 小倉トースト — the mixed sandwich (¥600) pairs cucumber, tomato, and potato salad, while ogura toast (¥350) nods to Nagoya's red-bean toast tradition; coffee is hand-drip nel-brewed at ¥300.

anecdotes.text: The central staircase inside the archives features two stained-glass windows — a sun symbolizing fair judicial proceedings above, and scales of justice at the entrance. Over 1,000 couples have held wedding ceremonies on these stairs, and cosplayers regularly use the corridors as a backdrop.
NHK's 2024 drama 'Tora ni Tsubasa' used the archives building as a primary filming location, renewing public interest in the kissashitsu and its former-courtroom setting.
Reviewers note the cafe has almost no exterior signage — finding it feels like discovering a neighborhood secret tucked inside a grand civic monument.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information about gluten cross-contamination precautions is available from any source. The kitchen prepares wheat-bread sandwiches and toast as its core menu items, making a gluten-safe environment unlikely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No soy sauce is listed as an ingredient in menu items. The menu is limited to sandwiches, toast, and beverages, so soy sauce exposure risk is minimal, but no explicit allergen information is provided.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Sandwiches contain egg and ham. No vegan-separated prep area or dedicated utensils are mentioned. Beverages appear plant-based but are not explicitly labeled vegan.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, allergy protocol, or staff allergy-handling practice has been documented in any available source. Phone ahead (052-953-0051) to inquire directly before visiting with a serious allergy.

positives.text: Menu is very short and simple — sandwiches, toast, and drinks — so ingredient questions are straightforward to ask. Staff are consistently described as friendly and approachable.
Entirely non-smoking environment inside a civic building.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet, old-school kissaten with no English menu; staff are described as friendly, but expect Japanese-only communication.

places.name: キューオーエルコーヒー (Q.O.L. COFFEE)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or allergen information published. The kitchen produces sandwiches on standard wheat bread and baked goods. Black coffee and other straight coffee drinks are naturally GF, but shared equipment and a small kitchen mean cross-contamination risk for any food item. Dashi-maki tamago sandwich uses dashi (broth) whose wheat content is unknown. Patrons with celiac should ask staff specifically about ingredients and preparation; safe to drink black coffee but food options are risky without direct confirmation.
places.vegan_detail: No declared vegan menu. Coffee drinks are plant-milk-adaptable in principle, but no confirmation available. The signature food item is a dashi-seasoned rolled egg sandwich with mayo — neither vegan nor likely GF. Pastries and baked goods contain dairy/eggs. Ask staff about milk alternatives and whether any items can be made without animal products.
places.neighborhood: 丸の内・市役所（中区）
places.cuisine: カフェ・スペシャルティコーヒー・サンドウィッチ
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Shima Yuya grew up in Nagoya dreaming of running his own kissaten. After a serious run at competitive basketball through high school, he pivoted back to that dream, enrolling in culinary school and then spending years in Italian restaurants before eight years at a respected Nagoy

roles: オーナー・ロースター・バリスタ
chef_bio.background: Shima Yuya grew up in Nagoya dreaming of running his own kissaten. After a serious run at competitive basketball through high school, he pivoted back to that dream, enrolling in culinary school and then spending years in Italian restaurants before eight years at a respected Nagoya coffee shop in Higashi ward. He then trained at a Gifu roastery to master the craft of roasting, and at age 30+ relocated to Melbourne, Australia — the epicentre of specialty coffee culture — working as a barista for several months before returning to open Q. O. L. COFFEE in June 2017.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name Q. O. L. stands for Quality of Life — the belief that one well-made cup of coffee can meaningfully improve a person's day. Shima sources beans directly from farms around the world, roasts them on a GIESEN W6A machine used in international competitions, hand-picks for defects, and measures roast colour numerically to guarantee consistency. Every cup comes with a card naming the bean and its origin.
chef_bio.specialty: 自家焙煎スペシャルティコーヒー（シングルオリジン・ブレンド）とだし巻き玉子サンド

anecdotes.text: Shima holds a Q-Grader licence — an internationally recognised coffee quality-grading certification held by only around 300 people in Japan — as well as Coffee Meister and Coffee Instructor credentials. He has served as a judge at Cup of Excellence international competitions and at Japan's national hand-drip championship.

On the shop's 7th anniversary in June 2024, Shima released a limited blend called '7th Anniversary Blend FURUMAI' using Colombian and Salvadoran Geisha beans, reflecting on seven years that 'passed in a flash despite various equipment troubles along the way.'
A second location, Q.O.L. COFFEE BREWERS, opened in April 2022 inside Nagoya PARCO's west building, bringing the brand to a younger, commercial-district audience.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No allergen or cross-contamination information is published. The café bakes pastries and prepares sandwiches on wheat bread in a small two-floor space; shared preparation surfaces are likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature dashi-maki tamago sandwich is seasoned with dashi broth; the soy sauce or dashi type (and any wheat content) is not disclosed in any published source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All food items reviewed contain eggs, dairy, or both. No vegan-designated preparation area is indicated.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling policy is publicly documented. The café does offer an English menu, which suggests some capacity to communicate with non-Japanese speakers about ingredients.

positives.text: Straight specialty coffee drinks (black coffee, cold brew, flat white without cow's milk) are naturally gluten-free and vegan-adaptable, and represent the core offering of the shop.

cultural_comfort.note: English menu available; staff are accustomed to solo international visitors; relaxed specialty-coffee shop atmosphere with no pressure.

places.name: 久坊 (Kyu-bo)
places.gf_detail: グルテンフリー対応の明示なし。鉄板焼きコースには醤油ベースのタレや、小麦を含む調味料が標準的に使用される。専用フライヤーはなし（鉄板調理）。コース内容はゲスト個別の予算・要望に応じて相談可能とのことで、アレルギー等は予約時に問い合わせが必須。
places.vegan_detail: メニューはA5和牛・活魚介を主軸とした完全なオムニコース構成。ヴィーガン・ベジタリアン対応の記載なし。オーナーが自家栽培した野菜を使用するなど素材へのこだわりはあるが、動物性食材を除いたコース提供は確認できない。予約時に個別相談の余地はある可能性がある。
places.neighborhood: 中区丸の内、地下鉄丸の内駅1分
places.cuisine: 鉄板焼き・A5黒毛和牛・活魚介
places.notes: 名古屋・丸の内のビジネス街に静かに構える鉄板料理の隠れ家。薄暗い路地を抜けた先にあるカウンターと個室は、接待や記念日のための特別な空間として地元の常連に知られる。オーナーは自ら野菜を栽培し、客の顔ぶれや予算に合わせた仕入れを行うという徹底したこだわりを持つ。

roles: オーナー（野菜栽培・食材選定）
chef_bio.background: 名古屋・丸の内のビジネス街に静かに構える鉄板料理の隠れ家。 薄暗い路地を抜けた先にあるカウンターと個室は、接待や記念日のための特別な空間として地元の常連に知られる。
chef_bio.philosophy: 「最高級の食材で最上級のおもてなしを」。 完全予約制を採ることで一組一組に向き合い、食材は予約ごとに個別調達する。
chef_bio.specialty: A5ランク黒毛和牛の鉄板焼き（サーロイン・フィレ・シャトーブリアン）、活アワビ・伊勢海老

anecdotes.text: 「隠れ家的」な雰囲気で、薄暗い通路を抜けたU字カウンターが特徴的。レビュアーはオーナーが食事中にスタッフへワインサービスを指導し始めたことをユニークな体験として記録している。

gf_cross_contamination.text: 鉄板焼きでは同一の調理面を複数の食材・ソースに使用するため、小麦を含む醤油ベースのソース等との接触リスクがある。グルテンへの配慮に関する記載は公式サイト・予約サイトのいずれにも見当たらない。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: 鉄板料理のタレや仕上げには醤油（小麦含有）が一般的に使われる。グルテンフリー醤油（丸大豆醤油等）の使用に関する明示はない。

vegan_cross_contact.text: 全コースが和牛・魚介を中心に構成されており、同一の鉄板面での交差接触は避けられない構造。ヴィーガン向けの分離調理に関する案内は確認できない。

staff_allergy_handling.text: 公式サイトは「ご予算・ご要望に合わせてコースをご用意」と記載しており、アレルギー等の事前申告は予約時に電話で確認することを推奨。英語対応は未確認。

positives.text: 完全予約制かつ少人数対応の個室あり。食材は予約ごとに個別調達するスタイルで、事前に詳細な要望を伝えやすい環境。

cultural_comfort.note: 完全予約制の高級鉄板焼き店で、英語メニューや英語対応スタッフの情報は確認できず。日本語での予約・来店が前提と思われる。

places.name: 焼鳥 かん田 (Yakitori Kanda)
places.gf_detail: Yakitori tare almost certainly contains wheat-based soy sauce (regular shoyu). No GF accommodations, dedicated fryer, or allergen information published. Course-only format leaves no room for substitutions. Not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Yakitori course built around grilled chicken; no plant-based alternatives noted anywhere. Standard yakitori tare (basting sauce) contains mirin and soy sauce. Not a viable option for vegans.
places.hours_raw: 18:00〜22:00 (reported across multiple aggregators; exact per-day schedule and closed days not publicly confirmed)
places.neighborhood: 東区 泉 (Higashi-ku, Izumi) — nearest subway: 市役所駅 / 東大手駅
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened around early 2018 in a discreet residential building in Nagoya's Higashi-ku Izumi district. The restaurant has no signage, no public phone number, and no official website — access is through returning regulars only.

chef_bio.background: Opened around early 2018 in a discreet residential building in Nagoya's Higashi-ku Izumi district. The restaurant has no signage, no public phone number, and no official website — access is through returning regulars only.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner-chef reportedly devotes virtually all waking hours to the craft, sourcing and preparing ingredients with an intensity that reviewers describe as singular even by yakitori standards.
chef_bio.specialty: Omakase yakitori course featuring premium chicken parts, showcased daily in a wooden box arrangement reminiscent of an osechi tray before grilling begins.

anecdotes.text: A blogger visiting a pre-opening event in late 2017 noted that every element of the course — from appetizers through grilled skewers to palate cleansers — bore the stamp of the chef's personal obsession.

Celebrity blogger Kato Chika wrote that the chef presents the day's raw ingredients arranged in a beautiful wooden box before service, and closes each course with handmade chocolate — a detail that stuck with regulars.
One commenter noted the restaurant's owner previously discouraged even naming the place on social media, though that policy softened over time.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No cross-contamination data available. The kitchen is a tiny owner-operated counter; no allergen protocol has been documented anywhere.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Yakitori tare (the basting glaze applied during grilling) standardly contains wheat-brewed soy sauce. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternative has been reported.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Entire menu is animal-based; vegan cross-contact is not a meaningful concept here as no plant-based dishes are offered.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling information published. The course-only, regulars-only format means the kitchen does not adapt to dietary restrictions for new guests.

cultural_comfort.note: Entirely in Japanese; no English spoken; access is limited to returning regulars — first-time visitors cannot make a reservation through any public channel.

places.name: 炭火やきとり ごまめ泉 (Sumibiyakitori Gomame Izumi)
places.gf_detail: Standard yakitori tare (dipping sauce) almost certainly contains wheat-based soy sauce (shoyu). Salt-grilled (shio) skewers are likely wheat-free on the skewer itself, but shared grill and kitchen cross-contamination are unconfirmed. No allergen menu or gluten-free declaration found anywhere. Must ask the kitchen specifically about tare ingredients and shared preparation surfaces before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: Entirely meat-focused yakitori with chicken as the centerpiece (Satsuma Jidori, Tanba Akadori). Seafood items include horse sashimi. No plant-based options noted anywhere. All skewers are animal-derived; there is no known vegan-friendly menu path.
places.neighborhood: 久屋大通・泉 (Hisaya-odori / Izumi, Higashi Ward)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened February 21, 2025 in the Izumi 1-chome district of Nagoya's Higashi Ward, a 3-minute walk from Kuyamachi Station. The shop occupies the third floor of the Hasegawa Building, offering views over the Nagoya nightscape from its counter and table seats.

chef_bio.background: Opened February 21, 2025 in the Izumi 1-chome district of Nagoya's Higashi Ward, a 3-minute walk from Kuyamachi Station. The shop occupies the third floor of the Hasegawa Building, offering views over the Nagoya nightscape from its counter and table seats.
chef_bio.philosophy: Authentic binchotan technique in a refined, non-smoking space that suits both casual evenings and business hospitality.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled yakitori using Wakayama Kishu binchotan charcoal, which generates far-infrared heat that seals in umami. Signature skewers draw from aged thigh meat (jukusei momo) sourced from two premium breeds — Satsuma Jidori and Tanba Akadori — letting diners compare flavour profiles side by side.

anecdotes.text: The counter faces an open kitchen, so guests can watch the master tend skewers over glowing charcoal — a deliberate design choice that turns grilling into part of the dining experience.
Jukusei momo (aged thigh) is offered in two breed variants on the same visit, a tasting-flight approach unusual for a neighbourhood yakitori-ya.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information on shared grilling surfaces or kitchen cross-contamination practices. The charcoal grill is almost certainly shared across all skewer types.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tare (yakitori dipping sauce) is standard in Japanese yakitori and typically contains wheat-based soy sauce. No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce alternative has been mentioned by any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All cooking surfaces are used exclusively for meat and seafood; there is no plant-based section and no vegan cross-contact protocol documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy menu, allergy declaration, or staff allergy-handling procedure has been documented by any source checked. Phone inquiry (052-212-5595) before visiting is strongly advised for any dietary restriction.

positives.text: Shio (salt) grilled skewers avoid tare entirely and may be the safest ordering path for wheat-avoiders, though shared-grill cross-contamination remains unconfirmed.

cultural_comfort.note: A stylish but neighborhood-local izakaya; menus and staff communication will be in Japanese, though the modern setting and card-payment system make it navigable with basic Japanese or a translation app.

places.name: オステリア オルカドーロ (Osteria Orca d'Oro)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergen protocol is documented anywhere. Fresh handmade wheat pasta is a signature, and bread (focaccia) is served routinely. Cross-contamination from flour is a realistic concern in this kitchen. Guests with celiac disease should contact the restaurant directly before visiting; safe options are not assured without custom arrangement.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or explicit accommodation listed. The kitchen works à la carte with daily market seafood, meat, and handmade wheat pasta. The chef is noted for adapting to guest requests, so vegetable-only plates may be possible if arranged in advance, but hidden animal-derived ingredients (fish stock, butter, bonito dashi) are standard in Italian cooking here and cannot be guaranteed absent without direct inquiry.
places.hours_raw: 火〜日 ランチ 11:30〜13:30、ディナー 17:30〜22:00。月曜定休（月曜が祝日の場合は翌火曜休）。
places.neighborhood: 西区花の木 (浄心駅徒歩3分)
places.notes: Born in Shizuoka Prefecture in January 1976. Yokoyama is an Italian cuisine specialist who opened Osteria Orca d'Oro in Nagoya's Nishi Ward, operating it as owner-chef.

chef_bio.origin: Nagoya, Aichi (opened as an independent osteria in Nishi Ward)
chef_bio.background: Born in Shizuoka Prefecture in January 1976. Yokoyama is an Italian cuisine specialist who opened Osteria Orca d'Oro in Nagoya's Nishi Ward, operating it as owner-chef.
chef_bio.philosophy: Yokoyama centres the menu on ingredients rather than a fixed list: he visits Yanagibashi Market every morning to hand-pick fresh seafood and vegetables, then builds the day's dishes around what is finest. He describes 'deliciousness as the foundation' while aiming for visual beauty in every plate, and expresses that he is grateful to each guest who chooses his restaurant from among the many options in the city.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal à la carte Italian: house-made fresh pasta (tagliatelle, pappardelle, tagliolini), acqua pazza (whole fish simmered in light broth), and charcoal-grilled Mikawa beef bistecca alla fiorentina. The menu changes daily based on what the chef selects at Yanagibashi Market each morning.

anecdotes.text: The chef visits Yanagibashi Market every morning to personally select that day's seafood and vegetables, then composes dishes around what he finds — the chalkboard specials change daily as a result.
The restaurant offers more than 200 Italian wines, positioning itself as a wine destination as much as a food destination in a residential neighbourhood not far from Nagoya Castle.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Fresh handmade wheat pasta is a core menu item and flour is in active use throughout service; cross-contamination cannot be ruled out without direct confirmation from the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No soy sauce-based preparations are documented for this Italian kitchen, but standard wheat-containing Italian ingredients (pasta dough, bread, flour-dusted proteins) are present throughout the menu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Butter, cream, fish stock, and cured meats are standard components of an Italian osteria kitchen; vegan cross-contact from shared surfaces and cookware is expected unless the kitchen is asked to accommodate a specific order separately.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen protocol, allergy menu, or staff allergy-handling procedure has been documented in any public source reviewed. The restaurant does note it will try to accommodate guest requests, but this is framed around taste preferences, not food safety.

positives.text: Chef adapts dishes to guest preferences on request — guests with dietary needs who call ahead may be able to arrange a custom plate, as the menu is inherently flexible and not bound to a printed list.

cultural_comfort.note: Owner-operated neighbourhood osteria near Joshin Station; no English menu or signage noted, and ordering centres on a chalkboard of daily specials — a working knowledge of Japanese is helpful.

places.name: フジヤマ55 金シャチ横丁店 (Fujiyama 55 Kinshachi Yokocho)
places.gf_detail: Noodles are house-made daily from Aichi-brand wheat flour (きぬあかり/ゆめあかり) plus kansui and potato starch — wheat is the primary ingredient. Broths contain fish-based dashi (bonito, mackerel) which typically include soy sauce with wheat. No gluten-free noodle substitute or GF accommodation is listed anywhere. Celiac diners should avoid.
places.vegan_detail: All broths are built on pork bone (tonkotsu) and whole chicken stock, combined with bonito, horse mackerel, and mackerel dashi. No plant-based broth or vegan menu items are offered. The Taiwan mazesoba also uses meat-based seasoning.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–15:00 (LO 14:30), 17:00–22:00 (LO 21:00); hours may shorten on certain dates — verify with venue
places.neighborhood: 名古屋城, 金シャチ横丁宗春ゾーン, 市役所駅
places.cuisine: ラーメン / つけめん
places.notes: Ryuichiro Sawa was born in Nagoya in 1971. After five years in the corporate sector, he pursued his passion for ramen by opening Chuuka Soba Tsurumaiikkokkuya, which drew lines from its very first month. While researching ramen across Japan, he encountered tsukemen — then a Tokyo

chef_bio.origin: 名古屋市大須 (Nagoya, Osu district), 2009
chef_bio.background: Ryuichiro Sawa was born in Nagoya in 1971. After five years in the corporate sector, he pursued his passion for ramen by opening Chuuka Soba Tsurumaiikkokkuya, which drew lines from its very first month. While researching ramen across Japan, he encountered tsukemen — then a Tokyo phenomenon — and set out to bring it to Nagoya. He founded Fujiyama55 in the Osu district in 2009, widely credited with igniting Nagoya's concentrated-tsukemen boom and dispelling the city's reputation as a ramen backwater.
chef_bio.philosophy: Sawa's guiding idea: 'I want to do fun things with my team — work itself is the highest form of entertainment. ' The brand's three pillars are people development, flavor creation, and store excellence. Noodles are hand-crafted each morning using only Aichi-brand wheat (きぬあかり/ゆめあかり), kansui, and potato starch — no preservatives or additives — with the ratio adjusted daily for temperature and humidity.
chef_bio.specialty: 魚介濃厚つけ麺 — thick house-made wheat noodles served alongside a two-day-simmered tonkotsu-torigara broth enriched with bonito, horse mackerel, and mackerel dashi; the Kinshachi Yokocho branch also features Nagoya miso ramen and Taiwan mazesoba

anecdotes.text: Sawa consumes over 500 bowls of ramen per year as part of his ongoing research into flavor.

The Fujiyama55 name reflects a twin ambition: Mount Fuji-inspired generous portions and the 'go-go' energy of relentless expansion — the chain now operates 59+ locations across Japan, Taiwan, China, Hong Kong, Thailand, Indonesia, and France.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire kitchen is built around wheat-based noodles made fresh on-site daily; cross-contamination with gluten is certain and unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broths combine pork bone, chicken, and fish dashi; standard Japanese soy sauce (containing wheat) is almost certainly used in seasoning tare — no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every broth on the menu contains pork bone, chicken stock, and/or fish dashi (bonito, mackerel, horse mackerel). There is no plant-based alternative and shared cooking equipment makes vegan cross-contact unavoidable.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy menu, allergy statement, or staff allergy protocol is published by the restaurant or venue. Diners with serious allergies should call ahead: 052-212-7375.

positives.text: Noodles are made with only three ingredients (wheat flour, kansui, potato starch) and zero preservatives or additives, which simplifies the ingredient picture even if it does not remove wheat.

cultural_comfort.note: Counter-heavy ramen shop inside a tourist-oriented food hall next to Nagoya Castle; staff interaction is minimal and ordering is straightforward, but menus and signage are primarily in Japanese.

places.name: 贅食家千壽 丸の内店 (Zeishokuya Senju Marunouchi)
places.gf_detail: Standard wheat-containing soy sauce (醤油) is used for sushi and sashimi — the default in Japanese sushi restaurants. Dashi is seafood-based. No gluten-free soy sauce, tamari, or dedicated GF preparations were found or advertised. Tempura appears on the parent location's menu, indicating shared-kitchen wheat use. Cross-contamination risk is real. Diners with celiac disease should contact the restaurant directly before visiting; do not assume safety.
places.vegan_detail: This is a traditional sushi and seafood izakaya. Miso soup uses seafood-based dashi (海鮮出汁). Fish, shellfish, and animal-derived broths are core to the menu. No plant-based or vegan alternatives were found.
places.hours_raw: ランチ 11:00-15:00、ディナー 17:00-20:00（毎日）。定休日不明（不定休の可能性あり）。
places.neighborhood: 中区丸の内（丸の内駅徒歩約6分）
places.notes: 贅食家千壽は名古屋市北区黒川に本店を構える寿司居酒屋で、丸の内店は2026年4月5日に新規オープンした支店。本店では店主の亀田俊之氏が北部市場で丸ごと一本の魚を自ら仕入れ、試食して品質を確認するスタイルを貫いている。

chef_bio.chef_name: 亀田俊之 (Kameda Toshiyuki) — main location
chef_bio.origin: 名古屋市北区（本店）、丸の内店は2026年4月開業
chef_bio.background: 贅食家千壽は名古屋市北区黒川に本店を構える寿司居酒屋で、丸の内店は2026年4月5日に新規オープンした支店。 本店では店主の亀田俊之氏が北部市場で丸ごと一本の魚を自ら仕入れ、試食して品質を確認するスタイルを貫いている。
chef_bio.philosophy: 「贅沢をお値打ちに」——高品質な寿司を手の届く価格で提供することを使命とし、値上げをせずに赤字覚悟で運営を続ける姿勢が話題を呼んでいる。
chef_bio.specialty: にぎり寿司（1貫160円からの圧倒的コスパ）、あたりまえランチ（¥1,990で10貫のにぎりとA5飛騨牛）

anecdotes.text: 本店はテレビ愛知で特集放送後に売上が約2倍に。店主は設備増強のために最大限の融資を受けたにもかかわらず、価格を据え置き続けている。

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information on dedicated fryers or separation of wheat-containing preparations. The parent menu includes tempura, indicating wheat flour is used in the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) is the default at sushi restaurants. No gluten-free soy sauce or tamari was mentioned anywhere in available sources.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Seafood-based dashi (海鮮出汁) is used in miso soup. Fish and shellfish are prepared throughout the kitchen. No plant-based separation is available.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy menu, allergen labeling, or staff allergy-handling protocol was found in any source. Celiac diners should call ahead: 052-201-3655.

positives.text: Counter-style seating means you can communicate directly with the chef about what goes into each piece, which may allow some customization.

cultural_comfort.note: The restaurant is a casual sushi counter with no English-language presence found; basic Japanese will go a long way, though counter sushi shops are generally navigable by pointing at items.

places.name: あんかけスパゲティ NANAYA (Ankake Spaghetti NANAYA)
places.gf_detail: The core dish is wheat spaghetti — gluten is structural, not incidental. The sauce may also contain soy sauce (wheat-bearing) as a seasoning. No gluten-free pasta or allergen-accommodation is mentioned in any source. This restaurant is not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Ankake spaghetti is a wheat-pasta dish fried in lard with a thick tomato-pepper sauce; standard toppings include wieners, bacon, and egg. No plant-based or vegan options are indicated anywhere on the menu or in reviews.
places.neighborhood: 丸の内／名古屋市中区
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] NANAYA opened on 24 October 2022 in a ground-floor space at Sun Mansion Marunouchi, a short walk from Marunouchi Station. Reviews note it is a compact, counter-led shop described as run by a kind couple, though no further owner details are publicly documented. A Tabelog review me

chef_bio.background: NANAYA opened on 24 October 2022 in a ground-floor space at Sun Mansion Marunouchi, a short walk from Marunouchi Station. Reviews note it is a compact, counter-led shop described as run by a kind couple, though no further owner details are publicly documented.
chef_bio.specialty: Ankake spaghetti — the classic Nagoya-born dish of thick wheat noodles stir-fried in lard and blanketed in a spicy, starch-thickened tomato-pepper sauce. NANAYA's version is praised for its punchy, peppery sauce and satisfying chew. Popular variations include the Mirakan (topped with a fried egg) and seasonal specials such as a Menchi Katsu (breaded minced-meat cutlet) topping in spring.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The restaurant's core product is wheat spaghetti; the kitchen works exclusively with wheat pasta and there is no indication of any gluten-free preparation area or protocols.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Ankake spaghetti sauce may contain soy sauce, which in Japan standardly contains wheat. No source confirms NANAYA uses tamari or any gluten-free soy sauce alternative.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Noodles are fried in lard and standard toppings include wieners and egg; no plant-only preparation line exists.

cultural_comfort.note: Small 18-seat counter-focused shop with no English menu noted; ordering is manageable by pointing at the menu, but staff are unlikely to speak English.

places.name: 創作串揚げ つだ 金シャチ横丁店 (Sosaku Kushiage Tsuda Kinshachi Yokocho)
places.gf_detail: Kushiage is deep-fried in wheat-flour batter (パン粉 breadcrumbs over a flour-egg-based coat) — standard wheat throughout. Frying oil is a lard-and-vegetable-oil blend used for all items; there is no dedicated GF fryer. Dipping options include a blended soy sauce (合わせ醤油) that almost certainly contains wheat, plus salt, lemon, and sansho salt which are likely safe. The restaurant states food-allergy accommodation is available (フードアレルギー対応可), so it is worth calling ahead, but celiac-safe dining cannot be assumed without direct confirmation.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu. Courses are heavily meat- and seafood-focused (wagyu, live prawn, hamo, scallop, salmon roe). Vegetable skewers do appear in courses (asparagus, green pea croquette, ginkgo nuts), but they share oil with meat/seafood, the batter very likely contains eggs, and dipping sauces include a blended soy sauce and a house sauce whose exact ingredients are unlisted. Hidden dashi (katsuo/bonito) is possible in sauces. Vegan guests should call ahead and ask explicitly — ad-hoc accommodation is noted as possible (フードアレルギー対応可) but a fully vegan course is not offered.
places.neighborhood: 中区二の丸（金シャチ横丁・宗春ゾーン）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Tsuda Takeshi graduated from an Osaka culinary school and spent five years mastering French cuisine before a single meal at Rokakutei — a Michelin one-star kushikatsu specialist with locations in Osaka and Tokyo — changed his direction entirely. He joined Rokakutei and spent a de

chef_bio.background: Tsuda Takeshi graduated from an Osaka culinary school and spent five years mastering French cuisine before a single meal at Rokakutei — a Michelin one-star kushikatsu specialist with locations in Osaka and Tokyo — changed his direction entirely. He joined Rokakutei and spent a decade there as its lead fryer, absorbing the techniques that would earn the restaurant its star. In March 2013, after 25 years in the craft, he opened his own flagship in Nagoya's Higashi Ward. The Kinshachi Yokocho branch opened on 29 March 2018, the same month Nagoya Castle's gourmet street debuted, and a Tokyo outpost at Toranomon Hills followed in 2024.
chef_bio.philosophy: Tsuda's guiding principle is that the ingredient is the star, not the batter. His coating is deliberately as thin as possible — built up with meringue and milk for lightness, finished with very fine breadcrumbs — and fried slowly at 170°C to coax natural flavour forward. He treats kushiage as washoku, using only sushi-grade ingredients that could in principle be eaten raw, and serves each skewer at the moment it is perfect.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal omakase kushiage course — live tiger prawn, Omi beef Tomosankaku, Aomori scallop, hamo with cream cheese, and salmon-with-ikura ('parent-and-child skewer') are signature items

anecdotes.text: Tsuda has said he was shocked when he first ate at Rokakutei: he had assumed kushiage was simply 'put batter on, fry, dip in sauce' — the experience upended that assumption and sent him on a 25-year journey to elevate the form.
A secret menu item — fried-rice-cracker tea rice — became so popular with regulars it was eventually added to the standard course.
Tsuda has publicly stated his next ambitions: Tokyo recognition by 2026 and a potential New York outpost by age 64, positioning creative kushiage as a global fine-dining form.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All kushiage items share the same lard-and-vegetable-oil frying blend; there is no separate dedicated GF fryer. Wheat from standard batter will be present in the oil throughout service.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The house dipping options include a blended soy sauce (合わせ醤油, described as 30+ ingredient base) which in standard Japanese practice contains wheat. Salt, sansho salt, lemon, and mustard options exist and are likely wheat-free, but confirmation is required.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All skewers are fried in the same shared oil (lard-and-vegetable blend). Lard is animal-derived. Even vegetable skewers are cooked in lard-containing oil alongside meat and seafood items.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Hitosara lists フードアレルギー対応可 (food-allergy accommodation available) for this location. The nature and extent of accommodation is not further specified; direct inquiry before visiting is strongly recommended.

positives.text: The restaurant uses exclusively domestic, sushi-grade ingredients and communicates openly about provenance — the counter format allows direct conversation with the chef about what is in each skewer.

cultural_comfort.note: Counter-style omakase dining with course service; English is not widely spoken, but online reservations are available and the format is intuitive for non-Japanese speakers.

places.name: 名古屋飯店 (Nagoya Hanten)
places.gf_detail: Standard machi-chuka kitchen — soy sauce (wheat-containing) is a baseline ingredient across virtually every dish including the signature ketchup-rice omurice. No gluten-free options, dedicated fryer, or allergen accommodation mentioned on Tabelog, the official Instagram, or any reviewed source. Wheat exposure is near-certain; not safe for celiac or serious gluten intolerance.
places.vegan_detail: Signature omurice is built on eggs and likely pork-based fried rice; other menu items include pork, meat-based broths, and lard-fried dishes typical of machi-chuka style. No plant-based options identified.
places.neighborhood: Kaminagoya, Nishi Ward — 4-minute walk north of Joshin Stati
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Noma-san spent his working life as an electrical equipment salesman before pivoting to professional cooking at age 40 — a late-start story that became a local talking point. He trained at several Chinese restaurants, including running Taka-hanten in the Endonji shotengai, before

chef_bio.background: Noma-san spent his working life as an electrical equipment salesman before pivoting to professional cooking at age 40 — a late-start story that became a local talking point. He trained at several Chinese restaurants, including running Taka-hanten in the Endonji shotengai, before opening Nagoya Hanten in December 2025.
chef_bio.philosophy: Noma's stated goal was to make an omurice that could only come from a Chinese restaurant — combining the technique of wok-fried rice with the silky egg work of tenshin-don. He refuses to compromise on ingredients despite a cost ratio above 50%.
chef_bio.specialty: 町中華本気のオムライス (machi-chuka honki no omurice) — ketchup fried rice sautéed in a wok, blanketed in a soft, barely-set sheet of premium Miyazaki 康卵 eggs at roughly ¥100 per egg, five per plate.

anecdotes.text: Despite each premium Miyazaki egg costing around ¥100, Noma uses over 200 eggs a day and doesn't blink at the food-cost math — 'without the right egg, it just doesn't taste right,' he has said.
The rice is Akita Komachi from Akita Prefecture, chosen for its firmness and mild sweetness when stir-fried — Noma considers it non-negotiable for both the fried rice and the omurice base.
The shop drew daily queues almost from its first week of trading, less than two months after opening in December 2025.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information on cross-contamination controls available; standard machi-chuka kitchen implies shared woks and utensils used across all dishes.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Wheat-containing soy sauce is a foundational ingredient in Chinese restaurant cooking and almost certainly used across sauces, marinades, and stir-fry bases here; no tamari or GF soy sauce alternative mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Shared wok cooking with meat, pork mince (Taiwan dishes), and animal-based stocks makes vegan cross-contact unavoidable.

cultural_comfort.note: QR-code ordering system for meals; Japanese-only menu and signage, but the ordering flow is straightforward and staff interaction is minimal.

places.name: ひつまぶし名古屋備長 金シャチ横丁店 (Hitsumabushi Nagoya Bincho Kinshachi Yokocho)
places.gf_detail: The house tare (sauce) is made with tamari soy sauce, mirin, and sugar. Traditional tamari contains little or no wheat compared to standard koikuchi soy sauce, but Bincho has not publicly confirmed their tamari is wheat-free or certified gluten-free. The eel itself is grilled over binchotan charcoal with no breading. The ochazuke broth (dashi) does not contain wheat but is fish-based. No dedicated GF menu or official allergy accommodation policy is published. Celiac diners should contact the restaurant directly before visiting.
places.vegan_detail: Eel is the sole focus of the menu. The signature ochazuke eating method uses fish-based dashi broth. No plant-based alternatives exist. Not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: 平日 10:30–17:30（LO 16:00）、土日祝 10:30–15:30（LO 15:00）・16:30–19:00（LO 18:30）。月曜定休（月曜祝日の場合は翌日）。名古屋城の休城日に準ずる。うなぎ売り切れ次第終了。
places.neighborhood: 中区三の丸 (金シャチ横丁)
places.notes: Bincho Co., Ltd. was founded in September 1992 by Hiroshi Suzuki, a native of Aichi Prefecture. After completing a decade-long apprenticeship at Unagi no Nishimoto, a longstanding Nagoya eel establishment, Suzuki opened the first Bincho location. The company name derives from bin

chef_bio.origin: Aichi Prefecture, Japan (founded 1992; now 12 locations across Aichi, Tokyo, Osaka, and Fukuoka)
chef_bio.background: Bincho Co. , Ltd. was founded in September 1992 by Hiroshi Suzuki, a native of Aichi Prefecture. After completing a decade-long apprenticeship at Unagi no Nishimoto, a longstanding Nagoya eel establishment, Suzuki opened the first Bincho location. The company name derives from binchotan (備長炭), the white oak charcoal central to the restaurant's cooking method; the logo's orange color represents glowing charcoal.
chef_bio.philosophy: Freshly grilled eel (焼きたて) paired with freshly cooked rice (炊きたて): eel is grilled only after each order, and rice is cooked in batches of no more than 20 portions to guarantee peak texture. The brand's mission is preserving and disseminating the traditional culture of eel cuisine (鰻の伝統文化の伝承).
chef_bio.specialty: Hitsumabushi — charcoal-grilled eel (unagi) served over rice in a wooden ohitsu vessel, eaten three ways: plain, with condiments (wasabi, negi, nori), and poured with dashi broth as ochazuke.

anecdotes.text: Suzuki learned Nagoya's distinctive jiyaki (地焼き) technique — direct charcoal grilling without the steaming step used in Kanto-style eel — during his 10-year apprenticeship at Unagi no Nishimoto. The method renders fat from the skin, yielding the restaurant's signature crispy exterior and tender interior.

The Kinshachi Yokocho branch sits in the Yoshinao Zone of the castle-side food district, and the menu includes a location-exclusive 金シャチひつまぶし (Gold Shachi Hitsumabushi) using 1.2 portions of eel — a nod to the golden shachihoko (dolphin-tiger) ornaments atop Nagoya Castle.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information is published about shared cooking surfaces or cross-contamination protocols. The open charcoal grill environment means multiple items may share preparation space.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The house tare uses tamari soy sauce (たまり醤油), which is traditionally brewed with little or no wheat — unlike standard koikuchi soy sauce. However, Bincho has not publicly confirmed the specific tamari is wheat-free or carries any GF certification. Celiac diners must verify directly.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Not applicable — no vegan dishes on the menu.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy menu or published accommodation policy found across the official website, Kinshachi Yokocho listing, Nagoya tourism info, or third-party reservation platforms. Phone reservations are available (052-223-5777) and contacting the restaurant in advance is the only recommended path for allergy concerns.

positives.text: Core protein is plain eel, charcoal-grilled with no breading or tempura coating — minimal ingredient complexity compared to multi-component Japanese dishes.

cultural_comfort.note: Located in the tourist-oriented Kinshachi Yokocho district next to Nagoya Castle; staff are accustomed to international visitors but the menu and ordering experience remain primarily Japanese-language.

places.name: 焼肉 だいいち (Yakiniku Daiichi)
places.gf_detail: Standard yakiniku tare (dipping sauce) and marinades in Japan typically contain wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free menu or allergen information is listed anywhere on Tabelog or the restaurant's Instagram. Dedicated GF accommodation is unconfirmed — those with celiac disease should enquire before booking about whether tamari or wheat-free alternatives are available. Cross-contamination risk from shared grilling surfaces is a further consideration.
places.vegan_detail: This is a meat-focused yakiniku and horumon (offal) restaurant with course menus built around black wagyu beef. No plant-based or vegan options are offered.
places.neighborhood: Josai, Nishi Ward — approx. 500m southwest of Nagoya Castle
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in October 2025 in a compact 16-seat space on the first floor of Heights SS building in Josai, a quiet residential street a short walk southwest of Nagoya Castle. The restaurant operates on a fully reservation-only basis with online booking only.

chef_bio.background: Opened in October 2025 in a compact 16-seat space on the first floor of Heights SS building in Josai, a quiet residential street a short walk southwest of Nagoya Castle. The restaurant operates on a fully reservation-only basis with online booking only.
chef_bio.specialty: Signature wagyu course centred on black wagyu beef (黒毛和牛); the restaurant claims to be the originator of miso-tan (みそたん発祥の店), a miso-marinated beef tongue preparation.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's Instagram identifies it as the birthplace of miso-tan (みそたん発祥の店), a distinctive preparation of beef tongue finished with miso.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Shared charcoal grills are standard at yakiniku restaurants; no information about dedicated or separated grilling equipment for allergen-free cooking.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Yakiniku tare (dipping sauces) and marinades in Japan are typically made with wheat-containing soy sauce. No gluten-free soy sauce (tamari) alternative is mentioned anywhere in the restaurant's listings.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Entirely meat-focused menu with no plant-based dishes; vegan cross-contact is not applicable as vegan dining is not possible here.

cultural_comfort.note: Fully reservation-only with online booking only (no phone calls accepted); signage and communication are in Japanese, so comfort with Japanese-language booking platforms is needed.

places.name: 天ぷら かき揚げ 光村 (Tempura Kakiage Mitsumura)
places.gf_detail: Traditional tempura is made with wheat-flour batter; no gluten-free batter or dedicated fryer documented. Soy-based dipping sauce (tentsuyu) almost certainly contains wheat-based soy sauce. Sesame oil is used for frying, but the frying oil is shared across all tempura and kakiage. No allergen menu, no staff allergy protocol, and no tamari or GF alternatives are mentioned on the official site or any reviewed source. Celiac diners should avoid.
places.vegan_detail: The kitchen centers on shrimp-heavy kakiage and other seafood tempura. Even vegetable tempura items are fried in the same oil as shrimp and fish. Dipping sauce (tentsuyu) is typically made with dashi from bonito/fish. No vegan-safe option or plant-based dashi confirmed. Vegans cannot safely eat here without direct confirmation from staff, which the restaurant does not facilitate (no English phone support confirmed).
places.neighborhood: 北区清水（清水駅近く）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 光村は1973年（昭和48年）創業の天ぷら専門店。名古屋市北区清水の住宅街に根を張り、半世紀以上にわたって地元客に愛されてきた老舗。2019年ミシュランガイド愛知・岐阜・三重版でミシュランプレートを獲得し、2025年には食べログ 天ぷら百名店にも選出されている。

chef_bio.origin: 名古屋市北区清水（1973年創業）
chef_bio.background: 光村は1973年（昭和48年）創業の天ぷら専門店。 名古屋市北区清水の住宅街に根を張り、半世紀以上にわたって地元客に愛されてきた老舗。
chef_bio.philosophy: 食材がなくなり次第終了という営業スタイルが示すとおり、鮮度と素材本位を重視する姿勢を一貫している。
chef_bio.specialty: かき揚げ丼。 ぷりぷりの小海老をふんだんに使ったかき揚げは丼から溢れんばかりの肉厚さで、甘辛のたれとごま油の香りが特徴。 特上かき揚げ丼は海老の量が約2倍。

anecdotes.text: 昼の開店前から行列ができるほどの人気店で、テーブルの順番ではなく注文順に料理が提供されるという独自のシステムを採用している。
2026年7月1日より原材料の急激なコスト上昇を理由に価格改定を予定していることを公式サイトで告知しており、長年据え置いてきた価格への真摯な姿勢が伝わる。

gf_cross_contamination.text: All tempura and kakiage — including vegetable items — are fried in the same sesame oil. No dedicated fryer for any allergen category is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tentsuyu dipping sauce and bowl tare are standard Japanese preparations that almost certainly contain wheat-based soy sauce; no tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetable tempura items share the frying oil with shrimp and fish tempura; no separation of cooking surfaces or oil confirmed.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, written allergy protocol, or English-language inquiry pathway has been documented on the official site or any third-party source reviewed.

positives.text: Sesame oil is used for frying (not blended vegetable shortening), which some diners prefer for its distinct flavour profile and lower prevalence of certain seed allergens.

cultural_comfort.note: The restaurant is a local neighbourhood institution; an English menu download is available on the official site, but the setting, ordering process, and staff interaction are primarily in Japanese.

places.name: 郷土素材料理 山人昔楼 (Sanjin Mukashiro)
places.gf_detail: Wheat is pervasive: soy sauce and miso appear throughout, tempura batter is used in multiple dishes, and rice/noodle courses include soy-glazed preparations. No gluten-free menu or dedicated GF accommodation is advertised. The restaurant states allergies can be addressed with advance notice, but there is no evidence of celiac-safe protocols or a dedicated fryer. Celiacs should contact the restaurant directly before booking and ask specifically about wheat-free alternatives to soy sauce (tamari) and tempura dishes.
places.vegan_detail: Standard kappo menu built around meat, fish, and seafood. Nearly all dishes incorporate fish-based dashi, soy sauce, or miso. No dedicated vegan options are listed. Allergy accommodation is possible with advance notice, but vegan requests would require explicit communication at booking time — not guaranteed.
places.neighborhood: 東区白壁・尼ケ坂
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A 70-year-old traditional private residence adjacent to a small park in Nagoya's Shirakabe district was carefully renovated and opened as a restaurant in November 2005. The building underwent a further major renovation and reopened in October 2025. The location is a 1-minute walk

chef_bio.background: A 70-year-old traditional private residence adjacent to a small park in Nagoya's Shirakabe district was carefully renovated and opened as a restaurant in November 2005. The building underwent a further major renovation and reopened in October 2025. The location is a 1-minute walk from Amagasaka Station, tucked into a quiet residential streetscape.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen's guiding principle is simplicity in service of the ingredient: boiling, grilling, and steaming are used to bring out natural flavors, with ancient salt and aged soy sauce as seasonings, and bincho charcoal for all grilled items. The motto is to let carefully sourced ingredients speak for themselves.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled seasonal ingredients: single-night-dried fish sourced from around Japan, three-variety Japanese heritage chicken (Akita Hinai, Nagoya Cochin, Satsuma Jidori), Kumamoto horse-meat sashimi, and wagyu loin grilled tableside over bincho charcoal.

anecdotes.text: Head chef Fukute Haruhiko works at the open counter, grilling directly-sourced overnight-dried fish over live bincho charcoal within view of guests — a deliberate choice that makes the craft of the kitchen part of the dining experience.

The dining room is designed so that every seat — counter, private room, and upstairs — faces the Japanese garden. A glass floor section in one room has garden stones embedded beneath it, blurring the line between interior and exterior.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura appears in multiple set courses and à la carte dishes. No information on a dedicated fryer or separation from other fried items.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Aged soy sauce (熟成醤油) is a stated house seasoning used across grilled, simmered, and rice dishes. Miso-based preparations also appear throughout the menu. Both contain wheat.
Charcoal-grilled rice balls with shoyu glaze and soy-glazed bamboo shoots are among listed dishes — standard wheat-containing soy sauce is used.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish-based dashi is a foundational ingredient in Japanese kappo cooking and is almost certainly present in soups and simmered dishes even where not explicitly listed.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Multiple sources report that the restaurant accommodates allergy requests when notified in advance. No formal allergy protocol or written allergen menu is documented.

positives.text: The kitchen uses high-quality ancient salt (古代塩) as a primary seasoning alongside soy sauce, meaning some simply-salted grilled items may be free of soy sauce and wheat — worth asking about specifically.

cultural_comfort.note: Traditional kappo setting with tatami-style private rooms; staff are hospitable but the environment is formal Japanese dining — no English menus confirmed, and advance reservations are required.

places.name: 花いち (Hanaichi)
places.gf_detail: Wheat is present throughout: tempura batter (signature tenmusu), soy sauce (used in seasoning), kitsune udon (wheat noodles), and dashimaki tamago. No gluten-free menu or substitution system is documented anywhere. Given the reservation-only, fully-booked format, diners with celiac should contact the restaurant far in advance — though no evidence exists that GF accommodations have been made.
places.vegan_detail: Traditional kappo cuisine built on fish-based dashi (the chef intentionally avoids kombu dashi, preferring fish stock) and seasonal seafood as central ingredients. No vegan alternatives are offered. Signature dishes include shrimp tempura tenmusu and dashimaki tamago.
places.neighborhood: 西区児玉 (浄心駅徒歩約8〜10分)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Born in Hekinan, Aichi, Ginzo Hanaichi left home at 16 for Osaka to apprentice in kappo and sushi restaurants. At 29 he returned to Nagoya and in 1983 opened 花いち in a quiet residential street in Nishi Ward — a converted traditional house with no exterior signage. Over 40-plus yea

chef_bio.origin: 愛知県碧南市 (Hekinan, Aichi Prefecture)
chef_bio.background: Born in Hekinan, Aichi, Ginzo Hanaichi left home at 16 for Osaka to apprentice in kappo and sushi restaurants. At 29 he returned to Nagoya and in 1983 opened 花いち in a quiet residential street in Nishi Ward — a converted traditional house with no exterior signage. Over 40-plus years he has maintained the kappo format unchanged, cooking entirely from scratch before guests each evening.
chef_bio.philosophy: The chef coined the phrase 不触不味 — roughly, 'do not touch what you would ruin by touching' — meaning the less you manipulate an ingredient the truer its flavor. He avoids kombu dashi entirely, believing it overwhelms seasonal produce. Nothing is made in advance; every dish is cooked to order.
chef_bio.specialty: Tenmusu (shrimp tempura rice ball) and freshly fried hanpen (fish cake with burdock) — dishes that distill the chef's obsession with serving food at the precise moment it is done.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant has no sign on the door. A single wooden board bearing the brushed characters 不触不味 hangs under the eaves — that is the only outward mark of one of Nagoya's most-awarded restaurants.

Regulars joke that the chef's voice is heard so rarely it is practically a menu item: '会話なら料理として' — 'if you want conversation, order it as a dish.' His wife Toshie manages all hospitality while he focuses entirely on the counter.

The daily handwritten menu is numbered by the restaurant's operating days — one visitor noted they were on day 9,603. After 40 years, the counter seats and the menu board look exactly as they did when 花いち opened in 1983.
As of the time of writing, new reservations are not being accepted; the restaurant is reportedly booked more than two years ahead.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura (shrimp, squid, dried potato) is fried on the counter in front of guests — a shared cooking environment where wheat-containing tempura batter is in active use throughout service.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard wheat-containing soy sauce is used in seasoning. No tamari or GF soy sauce substitution is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish-based dashi underpins most preparations. The chef explicitly avoids kombu dashi in favour of fish stock, making an all-plant meal structurally incompatible with the kitchen's approach.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, substitution policy, or allergy-handling protocol is documented in any reviewed source. Given the reservation-only format booked years in advance, allergen requests would need to be raised at the time of booking.

positives.text: Every dish is cooked entirely from scratch to order — no advance preparation or pre-made sauces — which means the chef has full visibility of what goes into each dish at each moment of service.

cultural_comfort.note: No English signage or menu; the chef is famously quiet and interaction flows primarily through his wife who manages hospitality — comfortable for Japanese-speaking diners, challenging for non-Japanese speakers.

places.name: 焼鳥 hanauta (Yakitori Hanauta)
places.gf_detail: Standard yakitori tare is made with wheat-containing soy sauce. Salt-grilled (shio) skewers are the lower-risk option but the same grill handles tare-glazed pieces, creating cross-contamination risk. No dedicated gluten-free facility or certified GF menu was found. Celiac diners should call ahead; the intimate 8-seat counter format may allow chef accommodation, but nothing is confirmed.
places.vegan_detail: Entirely chicken-focused omakase. Every course centers on binchotan-grilled heritage chicken skewers. No plant-based alternative course is offered.
places.neighborhood: 国際センター・那古野（円頓寺商店街）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in June 2023 on the second floor of a building in the historic Entoji shopping street (円頓寺商店街) district of Nagoya's Nishi Ward. The restaurant occupies a small but stylish space, part of the Kinsyachi Shoutengai merchant collective.

chef_bio.background: Opened in June 2023 on the second floor of a building in the historic Entoji shopping street (円頓寺商店街) district of Nagoya's Nishi Ward. The restaurant occupies a small but stylish space, part of the Kinsyachi Shoutengai merchant collective.
chef_bio.philosophy: Precision over quantity: an intimate omakase-style course built around the quality of the bird, the exactness of the heat, and the seasoning balance. Natural wines and local sake are chosen to complement, not overshadow, the grill.
chef_bio.specialty: Binchotan charcoal-grilled heritage chicken skewers — the kitchen centers on premium female Omi chicken (淡海地鶏メス) along with Amakusa Daio (天草大王), sourced for flavor and breed integrity.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers note the chef's focus on female Omi chicken — a deliberate sourcing decision that sets the skewers apart from standard yakitori. One regular wrote that the heat and salt balance was exactly right across every skewer.
The shop fills almost entirely with repeat customers, a sign of how quickly a loyal clientele formed around the 8-seat counter since its June 2023 opening.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tare-glazed and shio (salt) skewers share the same binchotan grill. Cross-contamination from wheat-based tare drips or brush contact is likely for celiac-risk diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Yakitori tare is traditionally made with soy sauce (containing wheat). No gluten-free soy sauce alternative was mentioned anywhere in available sources.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is built around grilled chicken; there is no plant-based course and vegan cross-contact is near-certain.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling policy or staff training information is publicly documented. The 8-seat counter format does allow direct communication with the chef, which may make individual requests possible — call ahead.

positives.text: Shio (salt-only) skewers are available and may avoid direct tare/wheat contact for lower-sensitivity guests, though shared grill risk remains.

cultural_comfort.note: An intimate 8-seat counter where conversation with the chef is part of the experience; basic Japanese will help, though the warm, approachable atmosphere is noted by regulars.

places.name: スパイシーカレー クスクス 白壁店 (Spicy Curry Kusukusu Shirakabe)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free designation anywhere. Naan is wheat-based and the default bread; basmati rice is available as an alternative and is naturally GF. Indian/Nepalese-style curry sauces are not typically thickened with wheat flour, but cross-contamination from naan baking in a shared kitchen is a realistic risk. No dedicated fryer or allergen protocol is documented. Celiac diners should call ahead and confirm rice-only ordering, shared cooking surfaces, and any wheat-containing marinades or sauces.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan designation on the menu. Curries are likely made with ghee or cream in some preparations (Malai Tikka, butter-based sauces). A rice-based set without dairy toppings may be achievable but would require direct confirmation with staff. The spice-fried chicken and kebabs contain meat; no plant-based curry option is advertised.
places.neighborhood: 東大手・白壁 (Higashi Ward)
places.cuisine: インド・ネパールカレー
