80–90% of vegetables used are certified organic or pesticide-free, sourced directly from named farms; antibiotic-free meats and wild-caught seafood used throughout.

cultural_comfort.note: French-style fine dining operated by a Japanese chef-couple; menu and communication are in Japanese, but the Western dining format is familiar and the staff are experienced handling reservation-time allergy notifications.

places.name: 和風ラザニア専門店 Y (Wafu Razania Senmonten Wai (Japanese-Style L)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-pasta lasagna specialty shop — gluten is the core ingredient. All lasagna varieties are built on standard wheat pasta sheets. Soy sauce (wheat-based) is incorporated directly into the sauce blend alongside Hatchomiso. No gluten-free pasta alternative is mentioned anywhere. No dedicated fryer information found. Not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options documented. The signature dish is lasagna layered with cheese-based béchamel (white sauce), tomato sauce, and Japanese condiments including Hatchomiso and soy sauce — dairy is integral. Menu also includes items like shrimp fry doria and beef tongue lasagna. No plant-based or dairy-free alternatives are mentioned on the official site, Tabelog listing, or any third-party reviews.
places.neighborhood: 中区二の丸 金シャチ横丁宗春ゾーン
places.cuisine: イタリアン・和風フュージョン
places.notes: Chef Shinohara Hidekazu spent 18 years in Milan, where he trained under Nobuyuki Matsuhisa (NOBU) at NOBU Armani and went on to open Zero Milano — a creative sushi restaurant whose clientele included soccer legend Roberto Baggio. His Milan-era work earned him a place in Who's Who

chef_bio.background: Chef Shinohara Hidekazu spent 18 years in Milan, where he trained under Nobuyuki Matsuhisa (NOBU) at NOBU Armani and went on to open Zero Milano — a creative sushi restaurant whose clientele included soccer legend Roberto Baggio. His Milan-era work earned him a place in Who's Who: Chefs, a US publication that ranks the world's top 100 culinary professionals. He later reverse-imported his fusion concept to Japan with Zero Tokyo in Ginza (2016), before launching this lasagna-focused concept at Kinshachi Yokocho in July 2023.
chef_bio.philosophy: Shinohara built the concept on a conviction that Italian and Japanese culinary sensibilities share deep common ground — both prize seasonality, craftsmanship, and umami. The shop name Y encodes the fusion: 和 (wa, Japanese) + 伊 (i, Italian) = Y. He positions lasagna not as foreign import but as a form that Japanese ingredients inhabit naturally.
chef_bio.specialty: 和風ラザニア (Japanese-style lasagna) — six-layer wheat pasta sheets with tomato sauce, béchamel, Hatchomiso, and soy sauce, in rotating varieties such as Salmon & Avocado, Mixed Mushroom, Beef Tongue & Olive, and Whitebait & Lemon Pepper from Shikoji Island (Aichi Prefecture)

anecdotes.text: Roberto Baggio was among the celebrity regulars at Shinohara's Zero Milano in Italy, drawn by his Japanese-Italian fusion cuisine long before the concept returned to Japan.
The shop name Y is a visual shorthand for the fusion: combining the kanji 和 (wa = Japanese) and 伊 (i = Italian) produces the letter Y — a bilingual pun encoded in a single character.

Kinshachi Yokocho opened the Muneharuzone in 2019 adjacent to Nagoya Castle's Ninomaru grounds; 和風ラザニア専門店 Y joined the complex at its July 2023 opening, part of a wave of concept restaurants targeting castle-area tourists.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat pasta is the foundational ingredient of every dish; cross-contamination throughout the kitchen is near-certain. No separate preparation areas for allergen-free cooking are documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce (which contains wheat) is used directly in the lasagna sauce blend alongside Hatchomiso. Wheat is present in both the pasta layers and the sauce itself — there is no wheat-free version of any menu item.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dairy (béchamel/white sauce, cheese) and meat proteins are core to the menu; no vegan preparation track exists. Cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

cultural_comfort.note: English menus are available; the venue sits inside Kinshachi Yokocho, a tourist-oriented food complex adjacent to Nagoya Castle, designed for broad accessibility including international visitors.

places.name: 馳走菴 ひじり (Chisoan Hijiri)
places.gf_detail: Wheat-containing soy sauce (koikuchi shoyu) is a staple of Kyoto kaiseki and almost certainly present in marinades, braises, and dipping sauces. Tempura (wheat batter) appears on the à la carte menu. No gluten-free claims, tamari use, or allergen menu exist on the official site or review pages. Celiac diners should inquire directly by phone before booking; outcomes are unverified.
places.vegan_detail: Classic Kyoto kaiseki built around dashi (kombu and katsuobushi), seasonal fish, Nagoya Cochin poultry, and soft-shell turtle. Kyoto vegetables and obanzai-style simmered dishes feature prominently but are cooked in fish-based broths. No vegan course exists; advance inquiry is essential and outcomes are uncertain.
places.neighborhood: 東区 東片端町、高岳駅徒歩8分
places.notes: Chisoan Hijiri is an intimate Kyoto-style kaiseki restaurant in Nagoya's Higashi Ward, steps from the Higashikatahata intersection. The chef trained in Kyoto before opening here, bringing authentic techniques and a personal procurement network that sources Kyoto vegetables, seaso

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto culinary tradition transplanted to Nagoya
chef_bio.background: Chisoan Hijiri is an intimate Kyoto-style kaiseki restaurant in Nagoya's Higashi Ward, steps from the Higashikatahata intersection. The chef trained in Kyoto before opening here, bringing authentic techniques and a personal procurement network that sources Kyoto vegetables, seasonal fish, and specialty items directly.
chef_bio.philosophy: Seasonal, ingredient-led cooking in a calm atmosphere modelled on Kyoto aesthetics: the interior uses lattice woodwork imported from Kyoto. The menu changes daily based on procurement, and the chef occasionally offers surprise off-menu dishes to counter guests.
chef_bio.specialty: Soft-shell turtle (suppon) — available year-round in multiple preparations including sashimi, chawanmushi, karaage, and hot pot — alongside a rotating kaiseki menu built on whatever the chef sources each day.

anecdotes.text: Fellow chefs praise the restaurant's Kyoto training lineage and describe the plating as 'distinctively creative' (盛り付けがとてもきれいで独創性があり).
A food blogger describes sourcing wild ayu (sweetfish) from the Wara River in Gifu Prefecture, calling it 'Japan's finest ayu', reflecting the chef's reach beyond standard market channels.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information available on cross-contamination controls; the kitchen prepares wheat-battered tempura and standard soy-sauce-based dishes.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Wheat-based soy sauce is standard throughout Kyoto kaiseki cooking and appears inherent to the braises, simmered dishes, and seasoning at this restaurant; no tamari or gluten-free soy sauce alternative is noted anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dashi made from katsuobushi (bonito flakes) is the backbone of Kyoto kaiseki; fish and poultry are central to the menu, making vegan cross-contact virtually certain without specific advance arrangement.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, allergy-handling policy, or gluten-free accommodation is mentioned on the official site or any reviewed source; the restaurant takes reservations by phone (052-963-0073) and dietary needs should be communicated at the time of booking.

positives.text: Courses are prepared to order in small batches at a 7-seat counter, giving the chef direct visibility over every dish and some flexibility to accommodate requests if communicated well in advance.

cultural_comfort.note: Intimate counter-style kaiseki restaurant oriented toward Japanese business entertaining; the chef interacts with guests in Japanese, and the menu is entirely in Japanese with no English materials noted.

places.name: 肉うどん さんすけ (Niku Udon Sansuke)
places.gf_detail: The restaurant's core product is handmade wheat udon noodles. The broth uses Imokei tamari soy sauce (from Gifu Prefecture), which is often lower-gluten than standard shoyu, but the shop is entirely built around wheat noodles and there is no gluten-free alternative offered. Cross-contamination is certain. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Broth is made with pork bone, chicken oil (chiyu), and animal-based dashi — no plant-based option available. Toppings include chashu pork as the centerpiece. Nothing on the menu is suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.neighborhood: 丸の内
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Owner Katō spent years training in ramen rather than udon — he worked at the legendary Ramen Jiro Mita main branch, then ran the Ramen Jiro Ōmiya offshoot for eight years before pivoting to udon in Nagoya. He opened this counter in October 2017 at age 29, channeling his ramen ped

chef_bio.background: Owner Katō spent years training in ramen rather than udon — he worked at the legendary Ramen Jiro Mita main branch, then ran the Ramen Jiro Ōmiya offshoot for eight years before pivoting to udon in Nagoya. He opened this counter in October 2017 at age 29, channeling his ramen pedigree into a bowl that straddles both worlds.
chef_bio.philosophy: Always satisfy customers' appetites (お客様のお腹を常に満たす). Katō applies ramen-kitchen discipline — three-day marinading and low-temperature pork cooking — to an udon format, producing generous portions that reviewers describe as addictive despite their richness.
chef_bio.specialty: さんすけうどん — handmade thick flat noodles in a rich pork-and-tamari-soy broth topped with chashu, spinach, nori, and self-serve ginger and green onion

anecdotes.text: The shop's name, さんすけ, references Oda Nobunaga's childhood name (三介), a nod to the historical Nagoya connection — a playful local anchor for what is otherwise a very non-traditional udon.
A food blogger visiting in summer 2025 noted customers eating with smiles and laughter — a warm contrast to the tense, almost martial atmosphere of the Ramen Jiro style the owner came from.
The broth prompted one writer to ask 'Is this just Yokohama ie-kei ramen?' — the tamari-soy, pork-bone, chicken-oil combination reads unmistakably ramen to veteran noodle-hunters.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles are the restaurant's entire product; flour is present throughout the kitchen. No gluten-free alternative exists and cross-contamination is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth uses Imokei tamari soy sauce from Gifu Prefecture. Tamari is typically made with little or no wheat, but this has not been confirmed as certified gluten-free and is served in a wheat-noodle kitchen.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pork chashu, chicken oil, and animal dashi are core broth components. There is no vegan path through the menu.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff are noted as patient and thorough in explaining the menu to first-time visitors, but no allergy or dietary accommodation protocols have been documented by any source.

cultural_comfort.note: Cash-only counter shop with a local-regular feel; staff are described as attentive and happy to explain the menu, but all signage and interaction is in Japanese.

places.name: グラッツィエ・ディ・クオーレ (Grazie di Cuore)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is listed or advertised. All pizzas and fresh pasta are wheat-based; the restaurant's own website highlights Italian wheat flour (イタリア産小麦) as a point of pride in its pizza dough. There is no dedicated GF kitchen, no mention of GF flour alternatives, and no allergen menu. Cross-contamination from flour-heavy cooking is a real concern. The restaurant does accept allergy-substitution requests verbally, but suitability for celiac diners has not been established.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan designation. The menu is built around meat, cheese, seafood, and egg-enriched pasta (carbonara uses Nagoya Cochin egg). Organic vegetables appear throughout — salads, roasted vegetables, and sides — but no dish is explicitly vegan. The restaurant notes it accommodates ingredient-substitution requests at time of visit, so it is worth asking, but no vegan commitment is made.
places.neighborhood: 浄心駅 徒歩5分 (西区上名古屋)
places.notes: Born in Tokyo in 1982, Terasawa Takashi discovered a love of cooking during part-time work at a family restaurant chain, where he stayed for eight years. He then spent three years at a pizza specialty shop, followed by three years at an Italian restaurant, before being entrusted

chef_bio.origin: Opened November 2018 in Joshin, Nishi-ku, Nagoya.
chef_bio.background: Born in Tokyo in 1982, Terasawa Takashi discovered a love of cooking during part-time work at a family restaurant chain, where he stayed for eight years. He then spent three years at a pizza specialty shop, followed by three years at an Italian restaurant, before being entrusted with the kitchen at Grazie di Cuore.
chef_bio.philosophy: Simple, never-tiring cuisine — シンプルで飽きのこない料理 — adapted so that Japanese diners of all ages feel at home with Italian food.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-made pizza on Italian wheat dough rested for half a day, fired to a crisp exterior and chewy interior; house-made roast beef served alongside mashed potato.

anecdotes.text: Terasawa's pizzas became so sought-after by fellow restaurateurs that the shop launched a frozen-pizza wholesale line — now supplying Italian restaurants, cafes, izakayas, and bars across the region.

The chef's route into professional cooking began entirely through family-restaurant part-time work; his eight years there gave him the fundamentals before he moved to specialist pizza and Italian training.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen works extensively with Italian wheat flour for fresh pasta and pizza dough; no GF-separated preparation area or equipment is mentioned.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No soy sauce-based dishes are evident on the Italian menu, but standard wheat-containing Italian ingredients (pasta dough, pizza dough) are used throughout.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Cheese, prosciutto, and animal-based ingredients appear in nearly every menu section; no vegan-safe prep line is indicated.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant states it accepts ingredient-substitution requests for allergies or dietary preferences at the time of visit (要相談).

positives.text: Organic vegetables (有機野菜) are used throughout the menu in salads, pasta, and side dishes.

cultural_comfort.note: A neighborhood Italian with local regulars and no evident English signage or menu; basic Japanese phrases will get you far, and staff are described as friendly and welcoming.

places.name: 手打ちそば 創愿 (Teuchi Soba Sougen)
places.gf_detail: The restaurant specialises in 十割そば (juwari soba) — 100% buckwheat noodles, which contain no wheat flour. This is a genuine option for gluten-avoiders. However, the standard offering also includes 二八そば (niwari, 20% wheat) and 更科そば (sarashina, also wheat-blended), so you must explicitly request the 十割. The tsuyu is made with a 20-year-old kaeshi base using shoyu from Shinshu (Nagano) — standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. Cross-contamination risk exists because wheat-containing soba is kneaded and cut in the same kitchen. No dedicated gluten-free kitchen is claimed. Tempura is served, meaning wheat flour and shared frying oil are present. Suitable for gluten-preference only, not celiac safety.
places.vegan_detail: Soba noodles themselves can be ordered without animal products, but the tsuyu (dipping broth) is almost certainly based on katsuobushi (bonito) and/or conventional Japanese dashi — no vegan broth option is advertised. Signature dishes include kamo-nanban (duck broth) and pork dipping soba; tempura is on the menu. Vegans should call ahead to ask whether a plain cold soba with no dipping sauce or a substitute is possible.
places.neighborhood: 中区丸の内・久屋大通
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened on 27 March 2006 in a ground-floor space of the Nakabun Building in Nagoya's Marunouchi office district. The owner-operator hand-rolls every portion of soba each morning using stone-milled buckwheat ground fresh daily. The shop's kaeshi (soy-sauce reduction that forms the

chef_bio.background: Opened on 27 March 2006 in a ground-floor space of the Nakabun Building in Nagoya's Marunouchi office district. The owner-operator hand-rolls every portion of soba each morning using stone-milled buckwheat ground fresh daily. The shop's kaeshi (soy-sauce reduction that forms the tsuyu base) has been continuously replenished since opening and is now over 20 years old.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner selects buckwheat from multiple origins, balancing hulled and unhulled grain ratios and adjusting the stone-mill technique to suit each harvest's characteristics. The guiding idea is to coax the maximum aroma and flavour from the grain itself, with a tsuyu built on a single carefully chosen Shinshu shoyu that has not changed since the shop opened.
chef_bio.specialty: Juwari soba (十割そば) — 100% buckwheat noodles; also kamo-nanban (duck broth soba) and seasonal tempura courses

anecdotes.text: The shop's kaeshi has been continuously replenished since the 2006 opening and is now over 20 years old — a hallmark of a traditional soba-ya.
An early food-blog review noted that the 100% buckwheat soba was available as a small upcharge option even in the shop's early years, with pork and duck dipping broths as the signature warm options.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-containing niwari (80% buckwheat / 20% wheat) and sarashina soba are made in the same kitchen as the juwari (100% buckwheat) soba; shared surfaces and equipment are likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tsuyu is built on a 20-year-old kaeshi made with Shinshu shoyu — standard Japanese soy sauce brewed with wheat. All broth-based and dipping dishes will contain wheat-derived soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Tempura is on the menu, meaning wheat-batter frying takes place in the kitchen. Duck (kamo) and pork dipping broths are signature items, so animal products are central to the cooking environment.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy menu, allergy statement, or staff allergy-handling protocol has been published on any source reviewed. Guests with serious allergies should call 052-951-1226 before visiting.

positives.text: Juwari soba (十割そば, 100% buckwheat, no wheat flour in the noodle itself) is a standing menu option and represents a genuine wheat-free noodle choice for those avoiding gluten by preference.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet, polished soba-ya in a business district with no English signage noted; an English menu is reportedly available, but interaction will be primarily in Japanese.

places.name: 手打蕎麦 ふたば (Teuchi Soba Futaba)
places.gf_detail: The menu includes both 二八そば (nihachi: 80% buckwheat, 20% wheat — not gluten-free) and 十割そば (juwari: 100% buckwheat — wheat-free as a flour). However, this is a general soba kitchen with no dedicated gluten-free protocol confirmed. The dashi broth uses bonito (likely with standard soy sauce containing wheat). Tempura is also on the menu, introducing wheat batter and shared-fryer risk. Celiacs should call ahead; the juwari soba is the only potentially lower-wheat option and cross-contamination cannot be ruled out.
places.vegan_detail: The dashi stock is bonito-based (katsuo dashi), making the standard soba broth non-vegan. The tsuyu served with cold seiro soba is also fish-based. Duck dishes are on the menu. Vegetable tempura options exist but share the kitchen. No plant-based broth alternative is mentioned anywhere. Vegans should ask if a konbu-only dashi is available; assume not unless confirmed.
places.hours_raw: 月火木金土日 11:00-14:30 / 18:00-21:00、水曜定休、毎月第3木曜日休み
places.neighborhood: 浅間町 / 押切 (Nishi Ward)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The menu includes both 二八そば (nihachi: 80% buckwheat, 20% wheat — not gluten-free) and 十割そば (juwari: 100% buckwheat — wheat-free as a flour). However, this is a general soba kitchen with no dedicated gluten-free protocol confirmed. The dashi broth uses bonito (likely with standard

chef_bio.philosophy: A calm, unhurried neighborhood soba shop where the noodles are rolled fresh daily and the atmosphere encourages lingering over soba-yu at the end of the meal.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-rolled nihachi (80/20 buckwheat-wheat) and juwari (100% buckwheat) seiro soba; duck nanban seiro and tempura seiro are the standout dishes.

anecdotes.text: Despite being a small shop, diners line up at the door by the 6 pm dinner opening — a reliable sign of neighborhood loyalty.
A soba researcher visiting in 2024 noted the mild, pale-coloured broth and hand-rolled noodles approximately 130–140 g per serving, and left wishing they had asked about the buckwheat's origin.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free preparation area is documented. The kitchen handles both wheat-containing nihachi noodles and tempura batter alongside the 100% buckwheat juwari soba, making cross-contamination a realistic concern for celiacs.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tsuyu dipping broth and kakejiru hot broth are almost certainly made with standard soy sauce (which contains wheat) and bonito dashi — no wheat-free tamari alternative is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bonito-based dashi is the standard broth throughout; duck dishes are prepared in the same kitchen. No plant-based alternative broth has been confirmed.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen handling procedures or staff allergy awareness information has been published for this restaurant. Phone ahead (052-522-5660) for any dietary requirements.

positives.text: The juwari (十割) seiro uses 100% buckwheat flour with no wheat added to the noodle itself — the safest option on the menu for those avoiding wheat in the noodle strand, subject to kitchen cross-contamination caveats.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet neighborhood soba-ya with no English signage or menu noted; a few Japanese phrases for ordering or pointing at the menu is enough to get by.

places.name: 濃厚中華そば 佐とう (Noko Chuka Soba Sato)
places.gf_detail: The broth is built on tamari soy sauce — even tamari often contains trace wheat in Japanese commercial production. No gluten-free claims or allergen accommodations are mentioned anywhere. The noodles themselves contain wheat. No dedicated fryer information available as this is a noodle shop. Not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The broth is a pork bone and pork meat (豚骨・豚肉) base seasoned with tamari soy sauce. Chashu (braised pork belly) is the signature topping. No vegan or vegetarian options mentioned. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.neighborhood: 中区 丸の内 / 丸の内駅から徒歩3分
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in October 2018 in Nagoya's Marunouchi business district, Noko Chuka Soba Sato was born from a bold genre pivot: the owner had previously run a popular donburi (rice bowl) restaurant called Le Granbowl at or near the same location, building deep expertise in meat preparati

chef_bio.background: Opened in October 2018 in Nagoya's Marunouchi business district, Noko Chuka Soba Sato was born from a bold genre pivot: the owner had previously run a popular donburi (rice bowl) restaurant called Le Granbowl at or near the same location, building deep expertise in meat preparation before committing fully to ramen.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner insists that despite the dramatic visual of meat-piled bowls, 'the soup itself is the protagonist. ' The broth — a concentrated pork-based stock seasoned with tamari and finished with subtle garlic and sweet notes — is treated as the centerpiece, with toppings as supporting cast.
chef_bio.specialty: 濃厚チャーシュー中華そば — a rich tamari soy sauce broth loaded with roughly ten thin slices of precisely prepared pork belly arranged in a flower-like spread across the bowl; paper aprons are provided because the broth splashes.

anecdotes.text: The shop transformed from a donburi specialist (Le Granbowl) into a ramen destination, carrying over the owner's confident meat-butchery skill into the chashu preparation that now defines the bowl.
A table condiment of kombu vinegar (昆布酢) is provided to cut the richness of the broth and add umami — an unusual and praised touch that regulars use to shift the flavour mid-bowl.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free accommodations exist. Wheat noodles are the core product cooked in the same kitchen, making cross-contamination certain.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broth is seasoned with tamari soy sauce; tamari in Japanese commercial production frequently contains trace wheat, and no wheat-free tamari claim is made.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broth is pork-based; no plant-based or separate cooking line exists.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling protocols or allergen menus are mentioned on any source reviewed. Phone lines are closed during service hours.

cultural_comfort.note: Counter-style ramen shop serving a Japanese business-district crowd; no English menu or staff noted, but ordering is straightforward with a short menu.

places.name: 楽いち (Rakuichi)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free claims or accommodations found. The tare (seasoning sauce) in tonkotsu ramen is typically wheat-based soy sauce, making the broth unsafe for celiac diners. Noodles are standard wheat noodles. Gyoza and other sides contain wheat. Cross-contamination risk is high in this tiny open counter kitchen. Not suitable for gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The broth is tonkotsu (pork bone) and the tare almost certainly contains soy sauce. Chashu pork is a standard topping. No plant-based options are listed. This shop is not suitable for vegans.
places.neighborhood: 名城公園・西区上名古屋（名城公園駅から徒歩約8分）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, hidden-gem counter ramen shop tucked in a quiet residential street in Nishi Ward, close to Meijo Park. Reviewers describe it as a place that feels more like a neighbourhood izakaya than a ramen shop, with regulars ordering beer and appetisers alongside bowls.

chef_bio.background: A small, hidden-gem counter ramen shop tucked in a quiet residential street in Nishi Ward, close to Meijo Park. Reviewers describe it as a place that feels more like a neighbourhood izakaya than a ramen shop, with regulars ordering beer and appetisers alongside bowls.
chef_bio.philosophy: Unpretentious neighbourhood comfort — the worn exterior belies a welcoming, easy atmosphere where solo diners and locals linger.
chef_bio.specialty: Tonkotsu ramen with a light, aromatic-oil-enriched pork-bone broth; the house 'Rakuichi-aji' (楽いち味) and 'Chotteri-aji' (ちょってり味 — slightly rich) styles are the calling cards, served with a choice of thin or medium-thick noodles.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information available. Open counter kitchen in a 10-seat shop makes cross-contamination highly likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tonkotsu tare in Japanese ramen shops standardly contains wheat-based soy sauce; no wheat-free tare is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broth and toppings are pork-based; no plant-based lane exists.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy handling information found; the shop has no online menu disclosures and is cash-only with no reservation system, making advance allergy communication difficult.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 10-seat counter shop with a convivial but Japanese-language-only environment; the owner is friendly, but no English signage or menus are evident.

places.name: 焼鳥 あらい (Yakitori Arai)
places.gf_detail: Standard yakitori tare (sauce) almost universally contains wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free menu, tamari substitution, or allergy accommodation is documented anywhere across Tabelog, HotPepper, or review sources. Some plain salt-grilled (shio) skewers could theoretically be lower-risk, but cross-contamination on shared grills is unaddressed. Celiac diners should call ahead and confirm soy sauce type and grill sharing practices before visiting.
places.vegan_detail: A dedicated yakitori specialist built around charcoal-grilled chicken and meat skewers. No plant-based or vegan options are listed on any reviewed source. Hidden animal content (chicken fat, dashi) is likely throughout.
places.neighborhood: 浅間町 (Asama-cho), 西区, 名古屋
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Standard yakitori tare (sauce) almost universally contains wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free menu, tamari substitution, or allergy accommodation is documented anywhere across Tabelog, HotPepper, or review sources. Some plain salt-grilled (shio) skewers could theoretically be

chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled yakitori featuring Nagoya Cochin (名古屋コーチン), a prized heritage chicken breed local to Aichi Prefecture, paired with a carefully curated selection of Japanese sake.

anecdotes.text: Opened November 20, 2024, in the quiet Asama-cho neighbourhood just west of Nagoya Castle. Reviewers noted the quality-to-price ratio feels almost too generous, with one writing it is 'outstanding compared to other yakitori shops' — drawing early praise for chicken heart (hatsu) and tender breast preparations.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Shared charcoal grill used for all skewers; no documentation of separate grill sections or protocols for allergen separation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard yakitori tare typically contains wheat-based soy sauce; no tamari or gluten-free alternative is documented for this restaurant.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All documented menu items are meat-based; animal fat and drippings on a shared grill make vegan cross-contact a near-certainty.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No information found on staff training for allergy handling; no reviews mention dietary accommodation requests being made or fulfilled.

cultural_comfort.note: A neighbourhood yakitori bar with a counter atmosphere and Japanese sake focus; English menu or English-speaking staff are not documented, so some Japanese or translation help is advisable.

places.name: 中華そば 白壁 あおい (Chuka Soba Shirakabe Aoi)
places.gf_detail: Wheat is central to this restaurant: house-made thin wavy noodles contain wheat, and the shoyu tare almost certainly contains wheat (standard Japanese soy sauce). No gluten-free noodle alternative or allergen accommodation is offered or mentioned anywhere. The mazesoba and all other menu items similarly contain wheat noodles. Not safe for celiac or wheat allergy.
places.vegan_detail: The broth is built on animal stock — chicken carcass, pork bone, and beef — with bonito (katsuobushi) dashi also referenced by reviewers. Signature chashu pork is a standard topping. There are no plant-based options on the menu. Not suitable for vegans.
places.neighborhood: 東大手駅 / 白壁 / 名古屋城北東
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The shop was founded in 1997 by the Sawa family in the Shirakabe district northeast of Nagoya Castle. Ryuichiro Sawa inherited it from his parents and continues to run it today. He is also the founder of the Fujiyama 55 tsukemen chain, but this original family counter is where hi

chef_bio.origin: Hida-Takayama, Gifu Prefecture (culinary inspiration); shop located in Nagoya's Shirakabe neighbourhood since 1997
chef_bio.background: The shop was founded in 1997 by the Sawa family in the Shirakabe district northeast of Nagoya Castle. Ryuichiro Sawa inherited it from his parents and continues to run it today. He is also the founder of the Fujiyama 55 tsukemen chain, but this original family counter is where his roots lie.
chef_bio.philosophy: Faithfully reproducing the gentle, deeply savory ramen of Hida-Takayama in Gifu Prefecture. The owner made repeated pilgrimages to Takayama to study and perfect the recipe before and after taking over the shop.
chef_bio.specialty: Takayama-style shoyu ramen — a light, clear soy broth made from chicken, pork, and beef stocks with sweet aromatic vegetables, served with thin house-made wavy noodles made fresh each morning

anecdotes.text: The current owner visited Takayama multiple times — driven by a taste he could not forget — to study the local ramen style before committing it to his own kitchen. That obsessive field research is reflected in the broth's distinctive sweetness and depth.
Despite dark-coloured shoyu broth, the soup delivers a gentle, not harsh flavour — reviewers consistently note that the appearance is more assertive than the taste, which is soft and umami-rich.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (shoyu) contains wheat; this restaurant uses shoyu tare in its broth and no tamari or wheat-free alternative is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broth is animal-based (chicken, pork, beef, and bonito); no plant-based cooking zone exists in this tiny 8-seat counter kitchen.

cultural_comfort.note: Tiny 8-seat counter shop with no English signage or menu; ordering is straightforward but a point at the menu approach is needed for non-Japanese speakers.

places.name: オステリア オルモ (Osteria OLMO)
places.gf_detail: パスタ（グルテンを含む小麦粉）が料理の中核を占めるイタリアン。セモリナ粉フリット・ブカティーニ・ニョッキなど小麦使用品が多数。醤油・だし等の使用は不明。アレルギーは2日前までの申告で対応を検討してもらえると案内されているが、専用グルテンフリーメニューや交差汚染防止体制の情報はない。セリアックの方は事前に詳細を直接確認要。
places.vegan_detail: 完全なヴィーガンメニューはなし。肉・魚・乳製品を多用するイタリア料理が中心（ウナギ、ホロホロ鳥、ストラッチャテッラチーズなど）。食材の希望は2日前までに伝えれば対応を試みてもらえる可能性がある旨の記載あり（「苦手食材は2日前まで」）。ただし対応範囲はコース変更の都合による。
places.hours_raw: 火〜土 18:00〜23:00（LO 21:00）、日・月 定休。不定期で臨時休業・日月営業あり（インスタグラムで告知）。
places.neighborhood: 那古野（西区）/ 国際センター駅徒歩6分
places.notes: オーナーシェフの佐藤一也氏は、銀座のAroma Fresca（アロマフレスカ）などで研鑽を積んだのち、2014年11月に名古屋・那古野に当店を開いた。名古屋市西区の「しけ道」（式見道）沿いに建つ築1941年の町家を改装した空間で、木を基調とした温かみある雰囲気が特徴。

roles: オーナーシェフ（佐藤一也）
chef_bio.origin: 名古屋市西区那古野、2014年11月開業
chef_bio.background: オーナーシェフの佐藤一也氏は、銀座のAroma Fresca（アロマフレスカ）などで研鑽を積んだのち、2014年11月に名古屋・那古野に当店を開いた。 名古屋市西区の「しけ道」（式見道）沿いに建つ築1941年の町家を改装した空間で、木を基調とした温かみある雰囲気が特徴。
chef_bio.philosophy: 「日本の恵みを感じる一皿」を追求しつつ、あくまでイタリア料理の技法・エッセンスを損なわない料理づくりを信条とする。 単なる「和イタリアン」に陥らない独自性を意識している。
chef_bio.specialty: 日本の旬食材を用いたイタリア料理。 柳橋市場の鮮魚・肉、全国の農家から直接仕入れる自然栽培野菜を「惜しみなく」使い、香りと温度にこだわった一皿を提供する。

anecdotes.text: 店名「OLMO」はイタリア語で「ケヤキ（欅）」を意味し、那古野の土地に根ざした料理を表現するという意志が込められている。
ヒトサラに寄稿した推薦シェフは「カジュアルながらも非常に繊細で美味しい。関東の有名店で修業したシェフが作り出す季節ごとのメニューと、細部に至る丁寧な仕込みが随所に感じられる」と評している。

gf_cross_contamination.text: 専用グルテンフリー調理器具・フライヤーの分離使用に関する情報は公式サイト・各種レビューとも一切記載なし。パスタ・セモリナ粉・小麦フリットを多用するキッチンでの交差汚染リスクは高いと考えられる。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: 醤油・だし（小麦含有の可能性）の使用有無についての情報は公開されていない。イタリア料理が主体だが、日本の旬食材を多用するため隠し味として使用している可能性を排除できない。

vegan_cross_contact.text: 乳製品（ストラッチャテッラ、ゴートチーズ等）・魚介・肉を同一キッチンで扱う。ヴィーガン専用調理ラインの情報なし。

staff_allergy_handling.text: 「アレルギーや苦手食材は2日前までにお知らせいただければ対応します」と案内されている（食べログ情報）。対応の具体的範囲（代替食材の内容・除去の確実性等）は不明。セリアックや重篤なアレルギーの方は予約時に直接詳細を確認することを強く推奨。

positives.text: 完全予約制のため、事前にアレルギー・食材の希望を伝える機会が確保されている。少人数制（15席）で、シェフが一人ひとりの状況に配慮しやすい環境。

cultural_comfort.note: 完全予約制の高級イタリアン。日本語での事前コミュニケーション（アレルギー等の申告）が必須で、英語対応の確認情報はない。

places.name: 丸の内蕎麦 (Marunouchi Soba)
places.gf_detail: Traditional soba tsuyu is made with wheat-containing soy sauce (醤油). Tanuki soba — the signature menu item — is topped with tenkasu (tempura batter scraps), which are fried in oil shared with wheat-battered tempura. Buckwheat soba noodles can contain wheat depending on blend (no juwari/100% soba confirmed here). No GF accommodation or awareness indicated at this casual ticket-machine shop. Not appropriate for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The broth is described as 和風出汁 (Japanese-style dashi), which at a standard casual soba shop of this type is virtually always made with katsuobushi (bonito flakes). No vegan-friendly broth option is indicated anywhere. Menu items include meat soba (肉そば). This place is not suitable for vegans.
places.neighborhood: 丸の内 / 名古屋城周辺
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Traditional soba tsuyu is made with wheat-containing soy sauce (醤油). Tanuki soba — the signature menu item — is topped with tenkasu (tempura batter scraps), which are fried in oil shared with wheat-battered tempura. Buckwheat soba noodles can contain wheat depending on blend (no

chef_bio.specialty: たぬき蕎麦（天かす入り温かいそば）

anecdotes.text: シンプル イズ ベスト をコンセプトに、丸の内のビジネス街で働くランチ客に、しっかりとした和風出汁の温かいそばを500〜750円台で提供。券売機で食券を購入するスタイルが手軽さを演出している。

gf_cross_contamination.text: たぬき蕎麦のトッピングである天かす（揚げ玉）は小麦粉の天ぷらバッターと同じ油で揚げられており、グルテン交差汚染のリスクが高い。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: そばつゆには通常の醤油（小麦含有）が使用されており、GF対応の代替醤油の情報は確認されていない。

vegan_cross_contact.text: だしは和風出汁（鰹節ベースと推定）であり、植物性ブロスの提供は確認されていない。肉そばメニューも存在する。

cultural_comfort.note: 券売機で食券を購入する完全日本語運用の立ち食いスタイル；英語メニューや英語対応スタッフの存在は確認されていない。

places.name: La・麺喰亭 (La Menkuitei)
places.gf_detail: Standard ramen shop using wheat noodles. Soy sauce in the broth is almost certainly wheat-containing (standard Japanese shoyu). No gluten-free noodle alternatives, dedicated fryer, or allergen accommodation mentioned in any source. Gyoza wrappers are wheat-based. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: All broths are animal-based: chicken clear broth or tonkotsu (pork bone). No plant-based broth options found. Toppings include chicken chashu and pork chashu. Gyoza are also on the menu. No vegan options identified across any source reviewed.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 11:30–14:30, 18:00–23:00; Sun & holidays 11:30–15:00, 17:30–22:00. Closed days listed as irregular (不定休) on Tabelog; one source (nagoya-meshi.com) cited Wednesday closures but this may be outdated.
places.neighborhood: 東大手・東区 (Higashioote, Higashi Ward)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] La・麺喰亭 opened on June 5, 2013 in the quiet Shomokucho neighborhood of Nagoya's Higashi Ward, a short walk from Higashioote Station. It operates as a small independent counter ramen shop with approximately 20 seats.

chef_bio.background: La・麺喰亭 opened on June 5, 2013 in the quiet Shomokucho neighborhood of Nagoya's Higashi Ward, a short walk from Higashioote Station. It operates as a small independent counter ramen shop with approximately 20 seats.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen favors a health-conscious, low-oil approach: reviewers consistently note you can finish the soup without heaviness. Lunch sets include complimentary rice and pickles, and affordable add-ons (chashu rice bowls, gyoza) keep the experience accessible.
chef_bio.specialty: 鶏レモンラーメン (Chicken Lemon Ramen, ¥870) — a salt-based clear broth topped with sliced lemon, tender chicken chashu, and white scallion sprouts. The broth is intentionally low-oil, emphasizing clean chicken umami with a bright citrus lift. A second broth option — tonkotsu (pork bone) — is available for those preferring a richer bowl.

anecdotes.text: One reviewer described the lemon ramen as 'not just novelty — it was genuinely delicious,' noting that the acidity complements rather than overwhelms the simple broth, making it ramen you'd want to eat endlessly.
The shop is described by regulars as 'a cozy place where you can chat about ramen with the friendly owner,' signaling an engaged, hands-on proprietor running a tight counter operation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese shoyu (wheat-containing soy sauce) is used in ramen broths at this type of shop; no tamari or wheat-free alternative is mentioned in any source.

cultural_comfort.note: Counter-only ramen shop with Japanese-language menus displayed above the kitchen; no English signage noted, but solo dining is common and the ordering process is straightforward point-and-pay at the counter.

places.name: 焼鳥ごりょう (Yakitori Goryo)
places.gf_detail: No GF claims found anywhere. Standard yakitori tare (basting sauce) and shoyu-based seasonings almost certainly use wheat-containing soy sauce. The course includes a chicken dashi soup whose thickeners and seasonings are unknown. A yakitori kitchen grilling whole chickens over charcoal has no dedicated fryer risk, but cross-contact from shared utensils and sauces is a real concern. Guests with celiac disease must contact the restaurant directly before booking to ask about soy sauce type and tare ingredients.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is chicken-only omakase. Courses include chicken dashi soup, multiple grilled chicken cuts, and ikura (salmon roe) on the finishing rice dish. No plant-based alternatives exist and there is no indication the kitchen can accommodate vegan requests.
places.neighborhood: 那古野・四間道 (Nagono / Shikemichi), 国際センター駅2番出口より徒歩3分
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 焼鳥ごりょう opened in July 2024 in a renovated historic townhouse in Nagono (那古野), one of Nagoya's most atmospheric old districts near Shikemichi. It is operated by 合資会社宮治 (Miyaji Partnership), a Nagoya-based hospitality company. The 12-seat counter-only space evokes the intimacy of a

chef_bio.origin: 名古屋市西区那古野・四間道エリア
chef_bio.background: 焼鳥ごりょう opened in July 2024 in a renovated historic townhouse in Nagono (那古野), one of Nagoya's most atmospheric old districts near Shikemichi. It is operated by 合資会社宮治 (Miyaji Partnership), a Nagoya-based hospitality company. The 12-seat counter-only space evokes the intimacy of a high-end sushi bar, and the kitchen works exclusively with whole female Mikawa red chickens (三河赤鶏の雌) — butchering only as many birds as the day's reservations require.
chef_bio.philosophy: Live, waste-nothing whole-bird cookery: every seat is booked before a single chicken is ordered, ensuring absolute freshness. The open counter lets diners watch the entire process from raw bird to finished skewer over charcoal.
chef_bio.specialty: Omakase yakitori course built around whole-bird Mikawa red chicken, including rare cuts such as uchi-momo (inner thigh, one per bird), furisode (wing tip), and bonciri (boneless tail), finished with kinoko rice and ikura.

anecdotes.text: On Monday and Thursday evenings younger staff take the counter at a discounted ¥5,000 course — a deliberate training programme that regulars use to discover emerging talent.

Reviewers noted that staff come outside to greet arriving guests, and that some visits ended with a small souvenir from the kitchen — touches that give the place a hospitality ethos beyond mere grilling.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information on fryer use (no fried items seen in menus), but shared charcoal grills, tare basting sauce, and seasoning bottles are common cross-contact vectors in a yakitori kitchen. No GF protocol is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce type (tamari vs. wheat-containing) is not disclosed anywhere. Standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat; yakitori tare is typically soy-sauce-based. Assume wheat present until confirmed otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is animal-based (chicken, salmon roe, beer/wine). There is no vegan pathway and therefore no relevant cross-contact mitigation to assess.

staff_allergy_handling.text: A Tabelog review notes staff asked 'Is rare chicken (sasami) acceptable?' before serving, suggesting some attentiveness to individual needs, but no formal allergy protocol or allergen menu has been published.

positives.text: No deep-frying observed in the menu; whole-bird charcoal grilling with no batter or breading on skewers is the core technique, which limits some wheat exposure vectors compared to a mixed-menu izakaya.

cultural_comfort.note: Reservation-only 12-seat counter experience conducted entirely in Japanese; staff greet guests outside and the intimate omakase format assumes familiarity with the rhythm of a Japanese counter.

places.name: ウッタムカレー 丸の内店 (Uttam Curry Marunouchi)
places.gf_detail: Indian curry sauces at this type of restaurant are typically flour-free, but naan is wheat-based and baked in a shared tandoor oven. Rice is available as a naan substitute. No gluten-free labeling, dedicated procedures, or allergy handling information is published anywhere for this restaurant. Soy sauce is not a typical ingredient in Indian/Nepalese curry, but the kitchen's practices regarding cross-contact are unknown. Anyone with celiac disease should phone ahead before visiting.
places.vegan_detail: Several plant-based curry choices — vegetable curry, chickpea masala, bean curry, spinach-potato curry, eggplant dry curry — and a vegetable Indian fried rice. However, naan contains dairy (ghee/butter), and there is no stated vegan certification or confirmed dairy-free preparation. Curries appear to be prepared without fish-based dashi or bonito, consistent with South Asian cooking tradition, but no explicit allergen information is published and cross-contact with dairy in the tandoor oven cannot be ruled out. Ask staff to confirm before ordering.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-14:30 / 17:00-22:00 daily; no fixed holidays (年中無休)
places.neighborhood: 中区 丸の内
places.notes: Uttam Curry opened its Marunouchi branch in December 2013, bringing a compact eatery-style Indian and Nepalese curry shop to the office district just south of Nagoya Castle. It is part of a small multi-location operation — a predecessor Kokusai Center location predates it. Review

chef_bio.background: Uttam Curry opened its Marunouchi branch in December 2013, bringing a compact eatery-style Indian and Nepalese curry shop to the office district just south of Nagoya Castle. It is part of a small multi-location operation — a predecessor Kokusai Center location predates it.
chef_bio.philosophy: Spice levels are kept mild by default to suit Japanese palates, with customization available on request — the stated aim is that everyone from children to adults can enjoy curry.
chef_bio.specialty: Large hand-stretched naan baked to order in a tandoor oven, paired with a wide range of curries spanning chicken masala, mutton saag, and several vegetable options; the choice curry lunch set at ¥790 is the calling-card order for the weekday office crowd.

anecdotes.text: The original Uttam Curry location reportedly sold authentic chicken curry and naan for ¥500 at a community festival in Nishi Ward, which is how one long-time reviewer first discovered the restaurant before it expanded to Marunouchi.
Weekday lunch service is consistently full; a reviewer in 2025 noted the restaurant offers one free extra naan with the lunch set, a touch that has earned repeat visitors.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Naan is wheat-based and baked in a shared tandoor oven used for all breads and tandoori items; no separate GF baking surface or protocol is mentioned anywhere.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No soy sauce is listed in the menu descriptions; Indian and Nepalese curry recipes at this restaurant do not appear to use soy sauce, but no allergen statement is published and kitchen practices are unconfirmed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dairy (ghee/butter) is used in naan and likely in tandoori dishes; the tandoor oven is shared. Vegan curries are not labeled as prepared separately from dairy-containing items.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, allergy protocol, or dietary accommodation procedure is published. One reviewer noted some language barrier with staff. Phone inquiry (052-202-0177) before visiting is advised for any serious dietary restriction.

positives.text: Multiple vegetable curries are available daily — vegetable curry, chickpea masala, bean curry, spinach-potato, and eggplant dry curry — giving plant-based diners real choices without relying on meat-free adaptations.
Rice is available as a substitute for naan at no stated extra charge, providing a wheat-free carb option if the curry sauce itself is flour-free.

cultural_comfort.note: Nepalese-run restaurant with Japanese-language menus; staff are friendly though reviewers note some language barrier — pointing at the menu works fine, and service is welcoming to all diners.

places.name: あんかけ太郎 名古屋城金シャチ横丁店 (Ankake Taro Nagoya Castle Kinshachi Yokocho)
places.gf_detail: Ankake spaghetti is fundamentally a wheat-pasta dish — gluten is the core ingredient. The sauce also typically contains Worcestershire sauce, which includes wheat-derived vinegar and malt, and bouillon products that may carry gluten. No gluten-free pasta option is offered or mentioned anywhere on the official site or third-party listings. This spot is not suitable for anyone avoiding gluten.
places.vegan_detail: Ankake spaghetti as served here is not vegan. The signature sauce is built on Worcestershire sauce (which contains anchovies), and the noodles are traditionally coated in lard before saucing. Meat toppings (sausage, bacon, minced meat) are standard across most menu items. A "Japonaise" style listed on the broader menu uses soy-dashi seasoning, adding another non-vegan element. No plant-based alternatives are offered or advertised.
places.neighborhood: 名古屋城, 金シャチ横丁宗春ゾーン
places.cuisine: あんかけスパ / パスタ
places.notes: Ankake Taro is a Nagoya-born operator dedicated to a single dish: ankake spaghetti, the city's beloved comfort-food oddity in which fat spaghetti noodles are smothered in a thick, spiced tomato-Worcestershire gravy. The brand runs five locations across Naka Ward, and this outpost

chef_bio.origin: 名古屋市中区（名古屋城エリア）
chef_bio.background: Ankake Taro is a Nagoya-born operator dedicated to a single dish: ankake spaghetti, the city's beloved comfort-food oddity in which fat spaghetti noodles are smothered in a thick, spiced tomato-Worcestershire gravy. The brand runs five locations across Naka Ward, and this outpost — the fourth to open — sits inside the Muneharū Zone of Kinshachi Yokocho, the castle-gate dining precinct that opened to revitalize the area around Nagoya Castle's east gate.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner describes the Kinshachi Yokocho location as a responsibility: to be a place that locals who work nearby and tourists alike genuinely need — not just a souvenir stop but a real neighbourhood lunch counter that carries Nagoya's food culture forward.
chef_bio.specialty: あんかけスパゲティ（みらかん・ナポリタンなどのトッピング違いを含む）

anecdotes.text: The sauce is deliberately tuned down on spice compared to some rival ankake-spa shops, a deliberate choice to make it accessible to children and older diners — and to maximise the portion-size flexibility that runs from a 250 g 'small' all the way to a 1 kg 'mega'.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire menu is wheat-pasta based and the kitchen is not set up to avoid gluten cross-contact. There is no gluten-free pasta, no GF sauce variant, and no allergen management information published.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The Japonaise menu style uses soy-dashi seasoning; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No wheat-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All cooking stations handle lard-coated noodles and animal-based stocks simultaneously; there is no separation for plant-based preparation.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen chart or staff allergy-handling protocol is published on the official site or any listing reviewed. Callers may reach the restaurant at 052-212-5554 to ask directly.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual counter-and-table pasta spot geared toward local workers and Nagoya Castle tourists — English menus are not confirmed; basic pointing/ordering is manageable but staff likely Japanese-only.

places.name: 納屋天 (Nayaten)
places.gf_detail: Traditional fugu cuisine relies heavily on wheat-containing soy sauce for ponzu dipping and seasoning. Dashi is fish-based (fugu broth) so dairy-free but not wheat-free by default. No GF adaptations or tamari substitutions are mentioned anywhere in reviews or listings. Fried fugu (kara-age) involves wheat-flour batter. Staff allergy communication should be attempted before booking, but celiac safety cannot be assumed at this type of traditional specialist counter.
places.vegan_detail: Exclusively a fugu (blowfish) speciality restaurant. Every dish centres on pufferfish — sashimi (tessa), hot pot (tetchiri), fried fugu, grilled shirako (milt). There are no plant-based or vegan dishes.
places.hours_raw: Seasonal only: open from the autumn equinox (approx. late September) through the spring equinox (approx. late March). Dinner service only; exact nightly hours not published. Closed entirely June–September (off-season). Call 052-961-8222 for current schedule.
places.neighborhood: 白壁 (Shirakabe), 東区 (Higashi Ward) — 2-minute walk from Higas
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Nayaten is a tiny, counter-only fugu specialist tucked into the residential Shirakabe neighbourhood of Nagoya's Higashi Ward, a short walk from Higashiote Station and less than a kilometre from Nagoya Castle. It is run by an elderly husband-and-wife couple and operates strictly w

chef_bio.background: Nayaten is a tiny, counter-only fugu specialist tucked into the residential Shirakabe neighbourhood of Nagoya's Higashi Ward, a short walk from Higashiote Station and less than a kilometre from Nagoya Castle. It is run by an elderly husband-and-wife couple and operates strictly within the fugu season — from the autumn equinox through to the spring equinox — honouring the traditional view that fugu is at its finest in the cold months.
chef_bio.philosophy: Understated hospitality in a hidden-gem setting: the couple prepare everything themselves, engage guests warmly, and keep the menu tightly seasonal, letting premium fugu speak for itself.
chef_bio.specialty: Thick-cut fugu sashimi (tessa) — unusually generous slices that concentrate the fish's umami rather than the near-translucent cuts typical elsewhere; also highly regarded for grilled shirako (white milt), tetsu-chiri hot pot, and the proprietors' own deeply infused hire-zake (fin sake).

anecdotes.text: Regulars describe the counter as a place where you drink and eat happily alongside the chef and proprietress — a rare intimacy at this price point.

First-time visitors cannot simply call to reserve; the convention is to be introduced by an existing regular before a table is offered, making this one of Nagoya's genuinely hard-to-enter neighbourhood counters.
The restaurant is described as extremely difficult to find — a hidden gem tucked in a residential lane — yet beloved by its small circle of loyal regulars.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a traditional Japanese fugu counter. Wheat flour is used in fried fugu preparation (kara-age) and wheat-based soy sauce is standard in ponzu and seasoning throughout the menu. Cross-contamination risk in a tiny 8-seat counter kitchen with no separation of cooking surfaces must be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned in any review or listing. Traditional wheat-containing shoyu is the default for ponzu dipping and cooking.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every component of the menu involves fugu or fish-derived stock. There is no vegan menu path and no plant-based preparation area.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No information on allergy communication or accommodation has been published. Given this is a tiny counter run by two people with no English-language presence, allergy discussions would need to happen in Japanese at the time of reservation.

positives.text: The restaurant is non-smoking throughout (全席禁煙), and fugu itself — as a white fish served as sashimi or in broth — is naturally free of gluten when prepared without soy sauce or flour coating. Guests with specific needs should raise this at booking.

cultural_comfort.note: Phone reservation in Japanese required; first-time guests are typically introduced by a regular — this is a local regulars' counter with no English interface or tourist infrastructure.

places.name: 千力 (Senriki)
places.gf_detail: Tempura is the entire concept: wheat-flour batter is inseparable from every dish. The tsuyu (sauce) and dipping sauces also typically contain wheat-based soy sauce. There is no gluten-free preparation available and no indication the kitchen can accommodate wheat avoidance. Completely unsuitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built around seafood tempura — shrimp, scallops, and mixed kakiage — with no plant-based main options. Dashi is almost certainly katsuobushi-based. Nothing here works for vegans.
places.hours_raw: 火〜土 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30)、17:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30)；日曜・祝日定休。月曜については情報によって異なるため要確認。
places.neighborhood: 丸の内
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Senriki is a small, independently run tempura and donburi shop tucked into the office backstreets of Marunouchi, a five-minute walk from Marunouchi Station. It has built a loyal following among nearby office workers who queue before the doors open at lunch.

chef_bio.background: Senriki is a small, independently run tempura and donburi shop tucked into the office backstreets of Marunouchi, a five-minute walk from Marunouchi Station. It has built a loyal following among nearby office workers who queue before the doors open at lunch.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen leans into salt as a seasoning philosophy, letting the natural sweetness of the seafood and vegetables speak. Diners can choose salt, tsuyu sauce, or miso — but regulars overwhelmingly recommend salt.
chef_bio.specialty: Shio kakiage-don — a generous mixed fritter of small shrimp, mushrooms, and scallops served over rice with a clean salt seasoning rather than the heavy sweet sauce found at most tempura shops.

anecdotes.text: A blogger arrived before noon on a weekday to find a queue already formed. A fellow diner in line said unprompted: 'Rather than trying three varieties, go with one specialty — the salt kakiage is what this place is about.'

The restaurant offers a 'kurabehare' (comparison) set at around ¥1,100–¥1,300 that lets diners order two mini bowls side by side — a practical way to sample the salt versus miso preparations in one sitting.
A daily 'kigamayure-don' (whim bowl) rotates based on what the kitchen feels like — past examples have included Hida beef with tomato and crab with smoked salmon and yuzu pepper.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat flour batter is used for every dish — there is no separation of gluten-containing and gluten-free cooking at any stage.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tsuyu-based dipping sauce and seasoning sauces are standard Japanese preparations and almost certainly contain wheat-based soy sauce; no tamari or GF alternative is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All mains contain seafood (shrimp, scallops); dashi is presumed katsuobushi-based with no plant-based alternative mentioned.

cultural_comfort.note: A compact neighborhood lunch spot in an office district — no English menu or signage, but the photo menu and simple ordering process make it manageable for non-Japanese speakers.

places.name: 日本料理 丈 (Nihon Ryori Jo)
places.gf_detail: Standard kaiseki uses wheat-based soy sauce (shoyu) in sauces and marinades, and dashi may include wheat-containing ingredients. No gluten-free menu is offered. The restaurant's policy states allergen requests must be made at the time of booking — no post-arrival adjustments are guaranteed. There is no dedicated gluten-free program, no dedicated fryer disclosed, and no evidence of cross-contamination protocols. Celiac diners should contact the restaurant directly before reserving.
places.vegan_detail: Traditional kaiseki menus rely heavily on dashi (typically katsuobushi/bonito-based) and seafood courses. No vegan menu or plant-based alternative is advertised. The counter-style omakase format ("御誂え料理") allows some customization if discussed at time of reservation, but vegan accommodation is not confirmed and would require direct inquiry before booking.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sun 11:30–14:30, 18:00–22:00; closed Monday (or Tuesday if Monday is a public holiday)
places.neighborhood: 西区 樋の口町 / 浅間町駅から徒歩約10分
places.notes: Nagata Isao was born in 1972 in Gifu Prefecture and entered the culinary world at age 18, spending his formative years at the original Hotel Nagoya Castle and its sister property Castle Plaza. He became head chef at Castle Plaza in 2009, then head of Japanese cuisine at Hotel Nag

roles: Supervising Producer: 佐々木浩 (Sasaki Hiroshi) — proprietor of Gion Sasaki, Kyoto (3 Michelin stars)
chef_bio.origin: Opened October 1, 2025 as the flagship Japanese restaurant of Espacio Nagoya Castle, a new luxury hotel erected on the historic site of Hotel Nagoya Castle. The hotel's Japanese cuisine lineage stretches back decades, and 丈 is designed as its direct heir — with the same chef who cooked for dignitaries during the 2005 Aichi Expo now leading the kitchen.
chef_bio.background: Nagata Isao was born in 1972 in Gifu Prefecture and entered the culinary world at age 18, spending his formative years at the original Hotel Nagoya Castle and its sister property Castle Plaza. He became head chef at Castle Plaza in 2009, then head of Japanese cuisine at Hotel Nagoya Castle in 2013, and later served as Japanese cuisine chef at Nagoya Kanko Hotel's 'Gorotake' from 2020. When Espacio Nagoya Castle opened in October 2025 — built on the site of the former Hotel Nagoya Castle — he returned as head chef of 丈. He received the Aichi Prefectural Governor's Excellence Technician Award (愛知県知事優秀技能者表彰) in 2022, one of the prefecture's highest culinary honors.
chef_bio.philosophy: The restaurant operates under the motto 温古知新 — 'learn from the past to create the new' — a guiding principle of its supervising chef Hiroshi Sasaki of Gion Sasaki in Kyoto. The name 丈 (jo) is chosen deliberately: it carries the meaning of integrity and uprightness, and shares the same reading as 城 (castle), linking the restaurant's identity to Nagoya Castle directly outside its windows.
chef_bio.specialty: Counter-style kaiseki (カウンター割烹) featuring seasonal omakase courses — 御誂え料理 — timed and paced to each diner, with ingredients sourced nationwide for peak seasonal quality.

anecdotes.text: Nagata Isao catered to foreign dignitaries and VIP guests during the 2005 Aichi Expo while working at Hotel Nagoya Castle — a formative test of high-stakes hospitality that now informs his exacting standards at 丈.
The name 丈 shares its reading (じょう / jo) with 城 (castle), quietly embedding Nagoya Castle into the restaurant's very identity. Reviewers have noted the wordplay as intentional and considered.

Hiroshi Sasaki — the three-Michelin-star chef who oversees monthly menus at 丈 from his Kyoto base — is scheduled to personally cook a one-night special event at the restaurant in July 2026 for ¥40,000 per guest, offering diners a direct encounter with his technique.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free preparation area or cross-contamination protocols are disclosed. A kaiseki kitchen uses wheat-based shoyu and mirin routinely across multiple dishes; shared prep surfaces and equipment are the norm in this format.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese kaiseki relies on shoyu (soy sauce brewed with wheat) as a foundational seasoning in sauces, marinades, and finishing. No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere in available sources.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dashi (stock) in kaiseki is typically made with katsuobushi (bonito flakes) or niboshi (dried sardines) and is used throughout the menu — in soups, sauces, and seasonings. No plant-based dashi option is advertised.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The official site states that allergy and dietary concerns must be communicated at the time of reservation. No adjustments are guaranteed after arrival. Guests with serious allergies should call 052-908-8930 (10:00–18:30) before booking.

positives.text: The counter omakase format ('御誂え料理') allows the kitchen to pace and adjust individual courses in real time, which in principle creates more opportunity for dietary discussion than a fixed set menu — though this is subject to advance notice at booking.

cultural_comfort.note: A formal hotel kaiseki restaurant operating on simultaneous fixed seatings with a smart-casual dress code — English is unlikely to be spoken fluently at the counter, and interaction with the head chef is in Japanese.

places.name: 柳城 NAGOYA CASTLE (Ryujo Nagoya Castle)
places.gf_detail: Standard Cantonese kitchen with significant wheat exposure: soy sauce (shoyu) is used throughout savory dishes, dim sum wrappers contain wheat flour, and cornstarch-thickened sauces may be cross-contaminated. No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or GF soy sauce alternative is documented. The hotel notes allergy accommodations should be requested in advance and may be declined if safety cannot be guaranteed. Not safe for celiac without direct, detailed inquiry with the kitchen.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu exists. Cantonese cuisine here centers on live-tank seafood, dim sum (typically contain lard or egg wrappers), Nagoya cochin chicken, and braised pork belly. Hidden animal ingredients (oyster sauce, lard in pastry, dashi-equivalent stocks) are standard in this style. Vegan requests should be made well in advance; the kitchen may be able to accommodate with prior notice but this is not confirmed.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30–14:30 (L.O. 13:30) / Dinner 17:30–22:00 (L.O. 20:30); open daily, no regular closure days listed
places.neighborhood: 西区 (Nishi Ward) / 浅間町・名古屋城エリア
places.notes: 柳城 NAGOYA CASTLE opened on October 1, 2025, as the signature Chinese restaurant of Espacio Nagoya Castle, a luxury hotel that occupies a historic site roughly 330 meters from Nagoya Castle's walls. The restaurant carries on the 柳城 (Ryujo) brand, referencing the castle's old name.

roles: Head Chef: 内藤勝博 (Katsuhiro Naito)
chef_bio.background: 柳城 NAGOYA CASTLE opened on October 1, 2025, as the signature Chinese restaurant of Espacio Nagoya Castle, a luxury hotel that occupies a historic site roughly 330 meters from Nagoya Castle's walls. The restaurant carries on the 柳城 (Ryujo) brand, referencing the castle's old name.
chef_bio.philosophy: Head chef Katsuhiro Naito draws on traditional Cantonese technique while weaving in the umami of local Japanese ingredients — Nagoya cochin, Okazaki ouhan chicken — to create what he calls authentic Cantonese cuisine expressed through a Japanese ingredient lens. Beverage pairings (Chinese wines, Taiwanese teas) are curated by in-house sommeliers and tea masters.
chef_bio.specialty: Cantonese crispy pork (広東式クリスピーポーク) and hand-crafted dim sum by a dedicated master dim sum chef; live-tank seafood dishes prepared to order.

anecdotes.text: Chef Naito is the first Japanese chef to reach the top 10 in an international Cantonese cuisine competition, a distinction cited prominently in the hotel's opening press materials.

The restaurant overlooks Nagoya Castle and takes its name from the castle's historical designation 柳城 (Ryujo — Willow Castle), connecting the dining experience to the 400-year-old landmark visible through its windows.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information about cross-contamination controls is publicly documented. Standard Cantonese kitchen workflows involve shared woks, utensils, and prep surfaces with wheat-containing ingredients throughout service.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Wheat-containing soy sauce is a foundational ingredient in Cantonese cooking and used throughout the menu. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Lard is traditional in dim sum pastry, and oyster sauce and other animal-derived condiments are ubiquitous in Cantonese kitchens. No vegan cross-contact protocols are documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The hotel states that allergy accommodations require advance notice and may be declined if customer safety cannot be guaranteed; requests should be directed to the reservation desk (052-908-8930, 10:00–18:30).

positives.text: Luxury hotel setting with dedicated reservation staff, making advance allergy communication more feasible than at a typical independent restaurant.

cultural_comfort.note: Luxury hotel restaurant (Espacio Nagoya Castle) with international guest infrastructure; English-language reservation platforms (TableCheck, Ikyu) available and hotel staff accustomed to overseas visitors.

places.name: おばんざいあふろ (Obanzai Afuro)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free labelling or accommodation is mentioned anywhere. Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) is almost certainly used in dashi-maki tamago and braised dishes. Tempura-battered items may appear. Cross-contamination risk is real in a 13-seat kitchen with no dedicated GF protocol. Celiac diners should not assume safety; ask specifically about soy sauce and cooking oils before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: Traditional obanzai cooking built on dashi broth; reviewers cite fish-based dashi (鰹・魚介出汁) as central to the flavour. Dishes like braised pork (角煮) and mackerel (ほっけ) appear on the menu alongside vegetable small plates. No vegan options are advertised, but plant-forward dishes (tofu with greens, seasonal vegetables) do appear — ask staff what is prepared without dashi or animal protein. Hidden fish-based dashi is the main concern.
places.neighborhood: 中区丸の内（丸の内駅より徒歩2分）
places.cuisine: 居酒屋・おばんざい・和食
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small obanzai counter that opened in September 2025 in the Horie Building on Marunouchi-dori, a short walk from Marunouchi Station. Run by a female proprietor, the place seats just 13 at a counter and a compact private nook.

chef_bio.background: A small obanzai counter that opened in September 2025 in the Horie Building on Marunouchi-dori, a short walk from Marunouchi Station. Run by a female proprietor, the place seats just 13 at a counter and a compact private nook.
chef_bio.philosophy: Gentle, home-style flavours rooted in careful dashi technique; a welcoming spot for solo diners seeking a quiet neighbourhood counter.
chef_bio.specialty: Dashi-forward obanzai small plates — seasonal vegetable simmers, dashi egg roll (dashimaki tamago), and braised dishes paired with sake and shochu.

anecdotes.text: One early reviewer noted that, despite the name conjuring an image of an afro-wearing cook, the establishment is run by a warm female proprietor.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information available. The kitchen serves a full izakaya menu in a compact space; cross-contamination cannot be ruled out.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard wheat-containing soy sauce is expected in braised dishes (kakuni), egg roll, and simmered vegetable plates. No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dashi (fish stock) is central to the cooking style per multiple reviews; plant-based dishes are likely prepared in the same pots and with the same dashi as fish dishes.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy handling policy or staff training information has been reported. The restaurant is a solo-operator counter; direct verbal communication with the proprietor is the only available channel.

cultural_comfort.note: Small counter-style izakaya run by a solo female proprietor; no English signage or menus reported, suited to Japanese-comfortable diners.

places.name: 大銀杏 名古屋西店 (Ooicho Nagoya Nishi-ten)
places.gf_detail: Standard yakitori with no gluten-free declaration. Tare (dipping sauce) used on many skewers contains wheat-based soy sauce (shoyu). The house salt blend is made from bonito and kombu dashi and is advertised as additive-free, but wheat-free status is unconfirmed. Charcoal grilling reduces cross-contamination risk versus frying, but shared grills and shared prep areas are standard. No dedicated GF menu or allergen guide found on any source. Celiacs should ask about tare vs. shio (salt) options and confirm shoyu ingredients before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: This is a dedicated yakitori restaurant — the entire menu revolves around grilled chicken and offal. The house seasoning salt is made from bonito (katsuobushi) and kombu dashi, making even the salt-grilled items non-vegan. No plant-based skewer alternatives are offered. Vegetarians and vegans have essentially no options here beyond side salads or cold tofu if available, but these are not confirmed allergen-free.
places.hours_raw: 17:30〜22:30（LO 22:00）; 定休日：木曜日（不定休の記載もあり）
places.neighborhood: Josai, Nishi-ku — 3-min walk from Asama-cho Station (Tsuruma
places.notes: Ooicho is a Nagoya-based charcoal yakitori chain with locations in Sakae, Meieki, and this Nishi-ku outpost near Asama-cho Station. No founding date or owner biography is publicly documented.

chef_bio.background: Ooicho is a Nagoya-based charcoal yakitori chain with locations in Sakae, Meieki, and this Nishi-ku outpost near Asama-cho Station. No founding date or owner biography is publicly documented.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen prioritizes sourcing quality local chicken and grilling over high-heat binchotan charcoal on a custom extra-large grill to seal in natural umami, letting the ingredient speak rather than heavy seasoning.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled chicken skewers using Aichi Prefecture's Nishiki-sawayaka chicken, finished with a housemade salt blended from bonito and kombu dashi — no chemical additives.

anecdotes.text: Rare cuts such as haatsu moto (chicken heart base, requiring 5-6 birds per single skewer) reflect the kitchen's commitment to whole-bird butchery.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated GF kitchen or allergen separation is documented. Shared charcoal grill used for all skewers including tare-marinated items.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tare (sweet soy-based glaze) used on standard yakitori skewers contains wheat. Shio (salt-grilled) option uses housemade salt from bonito and kombu dashi; wheat-free status of this salt is unconfirmed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All skewers are animal-based and the grill is entirely shared. Bonito-kombu salt is used across the menu. No vegan-safe items identified.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu or allergy handling policy is listed on any available source. Phone inquiry (052-325-5626) before visiting is strongly advised for any dietary restriction.

positives.text: Charcoal grilling rather than deep-frying eliminates shared-fryer cross-contamination risk. Shio (salt-only) skewers avoid tare sauce for those avoiding soy; ask staff to confirm which skewers can be ordered shio.

cultural_comfort.note: Neighborhood izakaya atmosphere near a residential area of Nishi-ku; Japanese menus and counter seating; basic Japanese useful for ordering at the charcoal counter.

places.name: 鰻 木屋 (Unagi Kiya)
places.gf_detail: Traditional unagi tare (kabayaki glaze) is made with standard wheat-containing soy sauce — a significant gluten source. Dashi used in soups is fish-based but likely made with standard ingredients. No GF claims on the menu or website. The restaurant does not advertise any allergen accommodations. Guests with celiac disease should call ahead (052-951-8781) and ask specifically about the soy sauce and tare; substitution is unlikely at a traditional specialist of this type. Cross-contamination risk from shared kitchen surfaces must also be assumed.
places.vegan_detail: Entirely eel-focused menu. All dishes built around unagi; soups are fish-based (鯉 or dashi); no plant-based options offered. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: 火～土 11:00〜13:30（昼のみ）。土曜夜は完全予約制で営業あり（17:30頃）。日・月・祝日・土用の丑の日は定休。
places.neighborhood: 東区 東大手 (Higashi Ward, Higashi-Ote)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in the late Edo period, Unagi Kiya is one of Nagoya's oldest surviving eel restaurants, now run by its fifth generation of ownership. It sits a short walk from Higashi-Ote Station in the historically layered Higashi Ward, not far from Nagoya Castle.

chef_bio.background: Founded in the late Edo period, Unagi Kiya is one of Nagoya's oldest surviving eel restaurants, now run by its fifth generation of ownership. It sits a short walk from Higashi-Ote Station in the historically layered Higashi Ward, not far from Nagoya Castle.
chef_bio.philosophy: Simplicity and continuity: the same tare has been topped off and carried forward across generations, the well water is still used to rest live eel before grilling, and the menu has stayed deliberately short.
chef_bio.specialty: Unagi don (eel rice bowl) and ohitsu-mabushi — the house style features Kansai-influenced belly-split eel grilled directly over binchotan charcoal without steaming, producing a crisper exterior than Edo-style preparations. The thick-cut eel is sourced from Kagoshima, stored live in well water before service, and finished with a century-old continuing tare.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant keeps live eel in well water before preparation — a practice said to reduce stress in the fish and improve texture and flavor at service.
Signed celebrity memorabilia is displayed inside, evidence of a long-standing following beyond the neighborhood regulars.
Liver dishes (kimo-yaki, umaki) are notably absent from the menu — a deliberate choice that sets Kiya apart from most unagi specialists in the city.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Traditional unagi tare (kabayaki glaze) is made with standard wheat-containing soy sauce. No GF alternative is offered or advertised. Cross-contamination from shared grilling surfaces and kitchen equipment must be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature tare — built up over generations from standard Japanese soy sauce (which contains wheat) — is central to every eel dish on the menu. There is no wheat-free soy sauce option indicated anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is eel-based; fish dashi is used in all soups. No plant-based cooking surfaces or utensils are separated. Not applicable for vegan diners.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy handling policy is published on the official site or Tabelog. Reservations are phone-only; calling ahead at 052-951-8781 is the only way to discuss dietary needs before visiting.

positives.text: Eel is prepared fresh to order (approximately 20–40 minutes), meaning there is no pre-made batch sitting with mixed ingredients — though the tare itself remains a fixed wheat source.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet, traditional unagi specialist with Japanese-only signage and menus; staff interaction is polite but not English-oriented — pointing to menu items works fine for ordering.

places.name: ブランカ (gallery+cafe blanka)
places.gf_detail: 小麦を含むフォカッチャ・キッシュ生地・パスタが主要メニュー。グルテンフリー対応の明示なし。醤油使用の有無不明。専用フライヤーや交差汚染対策の記載もなし。セリアック病対応は期待できず、事前確認必須。
places.vegan_detail: 野菜中心のメニューが多いが、キッシュ（卵・クリーム使用）、フォカッチャ、シフォンケーキなど乳製品・卵を含む料理が主力。ランチコースには豚肉（知多産）や魚介も登場する。完全ヴィーガン対応の明示はなく、隠しだし（鰹・煮干し）使用の有無も不明。事前に電話で確認することを強く推奨。
places.neighborhood: 中区丸の内（堀川沿い、丸の内駅8番出口より徒歩5分）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 明治40年（1907年）創業の材木商・沖正商店が営む老舗の蔵をリノベーションして誕生したギャラリーカフェ。堀川沿いに位置し、高い天井と木のぬくもりが特徴的な空間で、ギャラリーとカフェが一体となった稀有な場所。3つのギャラリースペースでは現代アートの展覧会、ポップアップ、朗読会などが開催される。

chef_bio.origin: 愛知県名古屋市中区丸の内（堀川沿い）
chef_bio.background: 明治40年（1907年）創業の材木商・沖正商店が営む老舗の蔵をリノベーションして誕生したギャラリーカフェ。 堀川沿いに位置し、高い天井と木のぬくもりが特徴的な空間で、ギャラリーとカフェが一体となった稀有な場所。 3つのギャラリースペースでは現代アートの展覧会、ポップアップ、朗読会などが開催される。
chef_bio.philosophy: アートを身近に感じられる場所をつくりたいという想いから生まれた空間。 高知県嶺北地域の野菜や愛知県産の地場野菜を中心に、身体にやさしい素材を大切にしたビストロ料理を提供。 亜硫酸塩添加量を抑えたナチュラルワインも揃える。
chef_bio.specialty: キッシュランチ（1日5食限定）／無農薬野菜たっぷりのサラダ

anecdotes.text: 2019年「愛知サラダメシ総選挙」でブロンズ賞を受賞。無農薬野菜を活かした彩り豊かなサラダが評価された。
明治40年創業の材木商が営む蔵をリノベーション。天井の高い開放的な空間に、ギャラリーとカフェが共存する。ギャラリーは独立した設計で、アート鑑賞だけを目的とした来訪者も食事客も快適に過ごせる。

gf_cross_contamination.text: グルテンフリー対応・交差汚染対策についての記載は公式サイト・各グルメサイトのいずれにも確認できなかった。フォカッチャやパスタなど小麦料理が主力のため、厨房内での小麦粉との接触は避けられない可能性が高い。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: 醤油の使用有無については公開情報に記載なし。フレンチ・ビストロ系メニューのため頻繁な使用は考えにくいが、確認は取れていない。

vegan_cross_contact.text: キッシュ・フォカッチャ・シフォンケーキなど乳製品・卵を含む料理が中心。完全ヴィーガンへの交差接触対策についての記載なし。

staff_allergy_handling.text: Tabelogの情報として「予算やメニューに関するご要望にも対応可能」との記載があり、事前相談に応じる姿勢は示されているが、アレルギー対応の専門的なプロトコルの有無は不明。来店前の電話確認（052-265-5557）を強く推奨。

positives.text: 無農薬野菜（高知県嶺北地域産・愛知県産）を積極使用。野菜中心のメニュー構成で、植物性素材の選択肢は他の一般的なカフェより多い。
2019年「愛知サラダメシ総選挙」ブロンズ賞受賞。野菜料理へのこだわりが第三者機関によって評価されている。

cultural_comfort.note: メニューや案内は日本語中心だが、カード決済対応・予約可能でアクセスしやすい環境。英語対応の明示はなし。

places.name: LIT（リト） (LIT (Rito))
places.gf_detail: あんかけスパゲッティは小麦粉の麺が主体であり、グルテンフリー対応の記載は一切ない。セリアック病・小麦アレルギーの方には非対応とみなすべき。
places.vegan_detail: メニューの看板はフィッシュプレートなど魚介系メニューで、ソースは野菜ベースのマイルドな旨味とされているが、ビーガン対応の明示はない。肉・魚を含まない選択肢があるかは不明のため、来店前に確認が必要。
