places.hours_raw: 火〜水・土〜日 11:30〜15:00（L.O.14:30）、木のみ夜18:00〜20:00（L.O.19:30）も営業、月・金定休。臨時休業あり（Instagramで確認要）。
places.neighborhood: 西区 上名古屋 / 浄心駅から徒歩10分
places.cuisine: あんかけスパゲッティ
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] もともとスパイスカレーの「LIT COFFEE」として出発し、その後約3年間キッチンカーであんかけパスタを全国のイベントで提供してきた。2026年1月12日、名古屋市西区上名古屋の路地に実店舗をオープン。店のロゴに馬をあしらったのは「自由に動き回るイメージ」から。年明けに合わせた開業は「午年のタイミング」とも重なった。

roles: オーナーシェフ（名前非公開）
chef_bio.origin: 名古屋市西区上名古屋
chef_bio.background: もともとスパイスカレーの「LIT COFFEE」として出発し、その後約3年間キッチンカーであんかけパスタを全国のイベントで提供してきた。 2026年1月12日、名古屋市西区上名古屋の路地に実店舗をオープン。 店のロゴに馬をあしらったのは「自由に動き回るイメージ」から。 年明けに合わせた開業は「午年のタイミング」とも重なった。
chef_bio.philosophy: キッチンカー時代の熱量をそのまま実店舗に持ち込む、移動する食の精神。 レトロ喫茶の雰囲気の中で、名古屋めしのあんかけパスタを真剣に掘り下げる。
chef_bio.specialty: あんかけスパゲッティ（S/M/L・20種以上のトッピングから選択可）。 看板はフィッシュプレート：揚げた白身魚に野菜の甘みを引き出したマイルドなあんかけソースをかけたもので、塩気と甘みのバランスが特徴とされる。

anecdotes.text: キッチンカー時代から看板だったフィッシュプレートは「旨みがぎゅっと詰まった」一皿として、実店舗オープン直後から話題を集め、営業時間中に売り切れることも珍しくない。
店のロゴはキッチンカー時代から使い続けている馬のモチーフ。「自由に動き回る」イメージを体現し、2026年の寅年（午年）開業とのタイミングも「素敵な一致」と地元メディアが紹介。

cultural_comfort.note: 小さなカウンター中心の店で英語メニューや英語対応の記載はなく、日本語での注文が基本となる。

places.name: ふじ原 (Fujiwara)
places.gf_detail: Standard Japanese soy sauce (containing wheat) is a foundational ingredient in kappo cooking at this level. Wheat appears in soy sauce marinades, tare, and likely in many braised and simmered preparations. Tempura is not prominent on documented menus, but shared prep surfaces and no dedicated GF protocols are documented. The chef is known for accommodating individual requests, so a celiac diner should communicate clearly at the time of reservation; however, the kitchen is not certified GF and cross-contamination risk cannot be ruled out.
places.vegan_detail: This is a traditional kappo restaurant built around seasonal seafood, fish-based dashi (likely katsuobushi/konbu blends), and standard Japanese soy sauce containing wheat. No vegan menu or plant-based options are advertised. Signature dishes include eel, sashimi, beef tendon, and sea urchin. A diner with advance notice might discuss a vegetable-forward arrangement with the chef — he has stated "please speak up casually if there's something you'd like to eat" — but no plant-based or vegan course is documented, and animal-derived dashi is almost certain to appear throughout.
places.hours_raw: 月〜土 17:30〜22:00、日曜・祝日定休。ランチ営業なし。
places.neighborhood: 東区泉・久屋大通
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Born in December 1969 in Aichi Prefecture, Fujiwara trained for 16 years at the celebrated Osaka kaiseki restaurant Sakuichi (作一) before returning to Nagoya and opening ふじ原 in 2004. The restaurant relocated and was fully renovated in 2016 to its current third-floor home inside th

chef_bio.background: Born in December 1969 in Aichi Prefecture, Fujiwara trained for 16 years at the celebrated Osaka kaiseki restaurant Sakuichi (作一) before returning to Nagoya and opening ふじ原 in 2004. The restaurant relocated and was fully renovated in 2016 to its current third-floor home inside the White Mates building on Hisaya-Odori.
chef_bio.philosophy: Fujiwara describes his goal as being 'worthy of the name Sakuichi' — a commitment to thorough, quality work inherited from his Osaka training. He emphasises seasonal ingredients and wants guests to feel they can 'speak up casually if there's something they'd like to eat,' making high-end kaiseki feel approachable.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal kappo-style Japanese cuisine rooted in Osaka (Naniwa-ryori) tradition: a rotating à la carte board of over 100 items including sashimi assortments, eel prepared two ways, braised beef tendon, and a vibrant five-item appetizer course with sea urchin, fish roe, and spring vegetables.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's daily menu is displayed on a board at the entrance rather than printed, with no prices listed — a reflection of the omakase spirit where trust in the chef guides the meal. A 2024 diner reported spending ¥18,480 for roughly two hours including several à la carte dishes and drinks.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free protocols or kitchen separation is documented. As a traditional kappo kitchen, wheat-containing soy sauce is a near-universal base ingredient across sauces, braises, and marinades, making cross-contamination a realistic concern.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (koikuchi shoyu, which contains wheat) is the expected foundation of this cuisine style. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternatives are mentioned on any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish-based dashi (likely katsuobushi) is a foundational element of kappo cooking. Seafood and animal proteins are present throughout service. No vegan cross-contact controls are documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The chef has stated guests should 'speak up casually if there's something they'd like to eat,' suggesting some willingness to accommodate requests, but no formal allergy protocol or allergen menu is documented on any platform.

positives.text: Owner-chef format with direct counter interaction means dietary needs can be communicated personally to the person preparing the food, which is more reliable than communicating through front-of-house staff in a large restaurant.

cultural_comfort.note: Upscale counter-style kappo restaurant where interaction with the owner-chef is central to the experience; English-language support is not indicated anywhere, and the menu is written on a board in Japanese with no prices listed.

places.name: 金鯱寿司 (Kinshachi Zushi)
places.gf_detail: Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) is used throughout; the menu features tempura (Mikawa clam tempura noted), shari rice seasoned with red and white vinegar (check for mirin), and dashi-based dishes — none of which are labelled gluten-free. No dedicated GF accommodation or tamari option is advertised anywhere. Celiac diners should call ahead and must ask specifically about soy sauce and hidden wheat in dashi and vinegar seasonings.
places.vegan_detail: Entirely seafood and animal-product focused: nigiri, sashimi, chawanmushi (egg custard), crab cream croquette, eel, Nagoya miso-seasoned beef, and dashi-based preparations. No plant-based alternatives are offered or implied.
places.neighborhood: 中区二の丸・金シャチ横丁（名古屋城駅徒歩1分）
places.notes: Muto grew up watching his father run a tonkatsu restaurant called Nabeyaki in Nagoya's Kita Ward. Initially reluctant to follow a culinary path, he was won over after spending one week helping his father and witnessing firsthand how much joy food brought to regular customers. Tha

roles: 寿司職人 (sushi chef at counter)
chef_bio.background: Muto grew up watching his father run a tonkatsu restaurant called Nabeyaki in Nagoya's Kita Ward. Initially reluctant to follow a culinary path, he was won over after spending one week helping his father and witnessing firsthand how much joy food brought to regular customers. That experience drove him to pursue hospitality with a goal of reaching even more people than his father had.
chef_bio.philosophy: Guard the best of traditional Edo-mae sushi while pursuing new challenges others haven't attempted — blending seasonal Nagoya ingredients (Mikawa large clams, Gifu Hatsushimo rice, Nagoya miso beef) with refined craftsmanship.
chef_bio.specialty: Creative Nagoya-style nigiri using both red and white vinegar for the shari, chosen strategically per ingredient; signature shrimp piece arranged to resemble Nagoya's golden kinshachi ornament.

anecdotes.text: Muto's father told stories of customers' delight every single day with obvious joy. Witnessing that firsthand was the moment Muto committed to the food industry — he wanted to 'expand food's possibilities and reach more people with skills surpassing my father's.'

The head chef at the counter was considered for a headmaster position at a sushi training institution before joining Kinshachi Zushi — underscoring the technical rigour behind the restaurant's approach.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura appears on the menu (Mikawa clam tempura), meaning wheat flour is in active use in the kitchen — cross-contamination risk for celiac diners is real and unaddressed by any public source.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned on any platform. Standard wheat-containing Japanese soy sauce must be assumed for all nigiri, sashimi dipping, and seasoning.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is built around seafood and animal products; dashi (fish stock) is almost certainly present in multiple preparations. No vegan cross-contact mitigation is mentioned.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, allergy-handling policy, or staff training information has been disclosed on any public platform. Direct telephone inquiry (052-386-6326) is the only recourse.

positives.text: Omakase counter format means direct chef interaction — a motivated diner can discuss ingredient concerns piece by piece with the person preparing the food.

cultural_comfort.note: Located in Kinshachi Yokocho, a tourist-facing food district built around Nagoya Castle, so staff are accustomed to non-Japanese visitors; basic Japanese greetings will suffice, though menus and staff communication are primarily in Japanese.

places.name: 二の丸茶亭 (Ninomaru Chatei)
places.gf_detail: Pure matcha and most traditional wagashi (nerikiri mochi, mitsumame bean jelly) are rice- and bean-based and typically contain no wheat. However, the Gold Shachi Morning set includes toast (明確に小麦含む). No dedicated gluten-free kitchen, no allergen labeling, no staff allergy training confirmed. Cross-contamination risk is unknown. Celiac diners should stick to matcha with mitsumame or nerikiri and confirm ingredients on the day — avoid the morning toast set entirely.
places.vegan_detail: Traditional wagashi (nerikiri, mitsumame) and plain matcha are typically free of meat, fish, and dairy — making them incidentally vegan in most cases. Mitsumame contains agar jelly, sweet azuki beans, and fruit, all plant-based. However, no vegan labeling exists, no confirmation that dashi or animal-derived additives are absent from sweets, and the Gold Shachi Morning toast almost certainly contains butter/milk. Ask about specific items before ordering.
places.hours_raw: 9:00–16:45 (last order 16:30); Gold Shachi Morning 9:00–11:00 limited qty daily; on the 8th of each month a formal tea ceremony is held and morning service is unavailable that day
places.neighborhood: 中区 本丸 (Nakaku, Honmaru — inside Nagoya Castle)
places.notes: 二の丸茶亭 is a traditional tearoom operated by the Nagoya Castle Promotion Company (名古屋城振興株式会社) inside the grounds of Nagoya Castle, situated beside the designated scenic Ninomaru Garden — historically the garden of the Owari Tokugawa lords, the largest castle-lord residential garden

roles: City-affiliated tearoom (名古屋城振興株式会社 operation)
chef_bio.origin: 名古屋城内 二之丸庭園 (Ninomaru Garden, Nagoya Castle)
chef_bio.background: 二の丸茶亭 is a traditional tearoom operated by the Nagoya Castle Promotion Company (名古屋城振興株式会社) inside the grounds of Nagoya Castle, situated beside the designated scenic Ninomaru Garden — historically the garden of the Owari Tokugawa lords, the largest castle-lord residential garden in Japan at roughly 30,000 square meters. The tearoom has been serving visitors for many decades as an integrated rest stop within one of Japan's most-visited castle complexes.
chef_bio.philosophy: A place to slow down mid-castle-visit and experience a moment of traditional tea culture, framed by a nationally designated scenic garden — the tearoom's ethos is quiet appreciation of season and setting rather than culinary ambition.
chef_bio.specialty: Matcha tea served with seasonal wagashi confections; gold-leaf matcha (金箔抹茶) is the premium signature offering

anecdotes.text: A replica of the legendary golden tea kettle (黄金の茶釜の写し) — said to be modeled after one cast from melted gold shachihoko roof ornaments destroyed during wartime — is displayed inside the tearoom. On the 8th of each month a formal tea ceremony is held using this kettle, and every Friday matcha is brewed from it for regular guests.

The Gold Shachi Morning (金シャチモーニング), offered daily 9:00–11:00 in limited quantities, pairs matcha, coffee, or orange juice with an original gold-shachi-shaped sweet toast topped with real gold leaf — a playful nod to Nagoya Castle's iconic gilded roof ornaments.

Despite its prime tourist location, 4travel reviewers note the tearoom is consistently less crowded than the main castle area, making it a peaceful escape — described as offering a '風流な雰囲気' (refined, cultivated atmosphere) with traditional red cloth seating and decorative red umbrellas.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information about kitchen separation or cross-contamination controls is publicly available. The tearoom is a small traditional operation with no allergen documentation found online.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No soy sauce-based dishes appear on the menu. The menu is limited to matcha, wagashi sweets, and mitsumame — soy sauce is not a standard ingredient in any of these. However, wagashi recipes are not disclosed and hidden wheat starch in confections cannot be ruled out.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No dairy or egg dishes visible on the menu alongside the matcha and wagashi offerings, but no formal separation protocol confirmed.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No information found regarding staff allergy training or allergy-aware ordering procedures. The tearoom is a compact, city-run facility without documented allergy protocols.

positives.text: The core menu — plain matcha and mitsumame (agar jelly, sweet azuki beans, fruit) — uses ingredients that are traditionally wheat-free, dairy-free, and meat-free, making incidental safety plausible for those with moderate sensitivities.
Menu is short and simple (matcha, wagashi, mitsumame), reducing the number of unknowns compared to a full kitchen restaurant.

cultural_comfort.note: Nagoya Castle draws international visitors and has basic English signage, but the tearoom itself is a quiet, traditional Japanese space where ordering is done simply by pointing at the menu; minimal English interaction expected from staff.

places.name: カバッチ (Kabacchi)
places.gf_detail: The restaurant emphasizes organic, additive-free ingredients and avoids chemical seasonings, but makes no gluten-free claims. Brazilian-style stews and grills may involve wheat-containing soy sauce or marinades. No dedicated GF menu, no allergen labeling found online. Cross-contamination risk is unknown. Celiac diners should contact the restaurant directly before visiting.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on Brazilian meat dishes (picanha steak, moqueca seafood stew, beef-tongue curry) with organic vegetables on the side. No dedicated vegan dishes are listed. Hidden animal-based broths and animal proteins are likely throughout. Ask staff before ordering.
places.neighborhood: 中区丸の内（地下鉄丸の内駅8番出口より徒歩4分）
places.cuisine: ブラジル料理・オーガニック・カフェ
places.notes: カバッチは2026年3月に名古屋・丸の内のHP丸の内ビル1階にオープンしたブラジル料理カフェ。農薬不使用の地元農家直送野菜とブラジル伝統料理を組み合わせたコンセプトを掲げ、女性一人でも入りやすいレトロでかわいらしい内装が特徴。

chef_bio.background: カバッチは2026年3月に名古屋・丸の内のHP丸の内ビル1階にオープンしたブラジル料理カフェ。 農薬不使用の地元農家直送野菜とブラジル伝統料理を組み合わせたコンセプトを掲げ、女性一人でも入りやすいレトロでかわいらしい内装が特徴。
chef_bio.philosophy: 化学調味料に頼らず素材本来の旨みを大切に。 地元農家から届くオーガニック野菜と南米の家庭料理の知恵を融合し、体に寄り添う食事を提供する。
chef_bio.specialty: ピカーニャ（ブラジル式300gステーキ）、ムケッカ（ブラジル伝統のコクミルクベースシーフードシチュー）、牛タンカレーランチ

cultural_comfort.note: Welcoming solo-diner layout and online reservation via HotPepper suggest a moderate degree of accessibility, though the menu and signage are Japanese-only.

places.name: vegetable cafe ＆ seafood bar saien (saien)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free labeling or dedicated GF menu exists. The menu includes multiple wheat-containing items: burgers with buns, tempura (red snapper tempura listed), curry (roux likely contains wheat), and standard Japanese soy sauce used throughout. Soy sauce in Japan typically contains wheat. No dedicated fryer information found. Cross-contamination risk is high in a busy kitchen that fries tempura and uses wheat-based sauces. Celiacs should not eat here without direct staff consultation and should be prepared for limited safe options.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is vegetable-forward but definitively omnivorous. Lunch-focused chopped salads can be customized with vegetable toppings, and a vegetable curry is listed. However, the menu also includes tebasaki (chicken wings), oysters, wagyu beef, seafood, and Nagoya specialty meat dishes. No dishes are labeled vegan, and hidden animal-derived ingredients (dashi, bonito, cheese) are likely present in many preparations. Vegans should inquire carefully before ordering.
places.neighborhood: 中区二の丸, 金シャチ横丁宗春ゾーン（Nagoya Castle Kinshachi Yokocho）
places.cuisine: Organic vegetable cafe, chopped salad, seafo
places.notes: An independent dining concept opened within Kinshachi Yokocho, the food and souvenir complex built in 2018 alongside Nagoya Castle as part of a city-led revitalization project. The Soharu Zone where saien sits is themed around Tokugawa Munenari, one of the castle's historical lor

chef_bio.origin: Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture; opened as part of the 2018 Kinshachi Yokocho development
chef_bio.background: An independent dining concept opened within Kinshachi Yokocho, the food and souvenir complex built in 2018 alongside Nagoya Castle as part of a city-led revitalization project. The Soharu Zone where saien sits is themed around Tokugawa Munenari, one of the castle's historical lords.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name 'saien' fuses two Japanese ideas: 菜園 (saien, vegetable garden) and 野菜で宴 (yasai de utage, a feast with vegetables). Owner Muto-san states the kitchen's commitment is to vegetables with vibrant colors used abundantly, sourced from the restaurant's own farm, paired with fresh seafood brought in daily from the market.
chef_bio.specialty: Chopped salad bowls (lunch) made with own-farm organic vegetables; soft-shell crab burger

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's concept draws on New York salad culture — the idea of building a complete, satisfying meal around a composed salad — and transplants it to the shadow of Nagoya Castle, combining it with local seafood and Nagoya specialty dishes like tebasaki and dote-ni.

A collaboration menu tied to Nagoya's three great unifiers (the three busho: Nobunaga, Hideyoshi, Ieyasu) featured a roasted miso vegetable platter, abalone risotto with raclette, and red snapper tempura with matcha salt, each priced at ¥999 as a nod to the castle grounds' cultural history.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura is on the menu (red snapper tempura listed in collaboration menus), indicating wheat-flour frying occurs in the kitchen. No information about a dedicated fryer or separate frying station was found.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned anywhere in the menu or restaurant descriptions. Standard Japanese soy sauce (which contains wheat) is expected to be used in seasoning.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen prepares meat (tebasaki chicken wings, wagyu steak, pork), dairy (raclette cheese dishes), and seafood alongside vegetable dishes on the same line; no separation policy is documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy handling policy, allergy menu, or staff allergy training information was found across any source reviewed (official site, Tabelog, HotPepper, Retty, or blog coverage).

positives.text: The restaurant's core identity is vegetable-forward: it grows its own organic vegetables on a dedicated farm and sources fresh seafood daily, meaning the produce quality is high and plant-based dishes are a genuine focus rather than an afterthought.

cultural_comfort.note: A Japanese-language environment inside a popular tourist-facing complex at Nagoya Castle; staff serve international visitors regularly but menus and signage are primarily in Japanese.
